steps.text:
## The field

*What private service is, and what it is not*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Define private service and distinguish it from hospitality, from the concierge industry, and from domestic staffing. Situate the profession historically, and understand why it re-emerged. Use the vocabulary of the field correctly, from the first day.

**1.1 · A definition**

Private service is the profession of running the personal life of a wealthy individual or family, from inside their trust rather than inside an institution. It is not a job in an industry with published prices, standard hours and an HR department. It is a relationship, formalised by a contract, and it lives or dies on a quality that cannot be audited: whether you can be relied upon absolutely.

**DEFINITION**

Private service. The direct employment of an individual by a private household or its family office, to manage some part of the principal’s personal life, logistics, residences, staff, travel, projects, or the whole of it, under conditions of confidentiality.

The word private is the whole of it. Everything else in your working life so far has been public in some degree: a hotel, a restaurant, a shop, an office. There was a brand between you and the guest, and the brand carried the standard. Here there is no brand. There is you, and there is a family, and the standard is whatever you personally are.

**1.2 · What it is not**

Four professions are routinely confused with this one, including by people who work in them. The confusion is not academic: each of the four hires differently, pays differently, and rewards different behaviour.

**Adjacent field.** How it differs

**Hotel concierge (Les Clefs d’Or).** Employed by a property, serving many guests, briefly. Rewarded for visible excellence and for the memorable gesture. The guest leaves; the relationship is transactional and warm. Excellent training, and a poor set of instincts to carry unexamined into a private house.

**Concierge company (membership businesses).** A company sells a subscription to a pool of clients and serves them through a team. You serve requests, not a person. The relationship belongs to the company, not to you, and the client can be handed to a colleague at any moment.

**Domestic staffing.** Housekeeping, cooking, driving, childcare. Essential, honourable, and organised around the maintenance of the house rather than the management of a life. Different skill, different ladder.

**Executive assistance.** Serving a person, but in their professional capacity. The boundary between the two is thin, and in private service it is routinely crossed, in one direction only: the private role absorbs professional tasks far more often than the reverse.

**1.3 · A short history, and why it matters**

The great European household of the nineteenth century was an organisation of thirty or fifty people, with a butler at its head, a codified hierarchy, and a career structure. It collapsed in the twentieth century, two wars, taxation, and the disappearance of a servant class that had no alternative. By 1960 the profession was widely believed to be finished. It returned, and it returned changed. The wealth created from the 1980s onward, finance, technology, energy, and later the emerging economies, produced a very large number of people who possessed extraordinary means and no inherited system for living with them. They did not want a household of forty. They wanted one person who could make everything work, and they were prepared to pay unusual sums for it. That is the role you are training for. Understand its lineage, because it explains its habits: the vocabulary is inherited from the old world (principal, butler, livery), and the expectations belong to the new one (speed, mobility, technology, absolute discretion in an age when everything leaks). You are, in a real sense, a modern profession wearing an old uniform. Both halves of that sentence matter.

**1.4 · One craft, several names**

Before anything else, a piece of vocabulary that will save you months of confusion, and that almost nobody explains.

This profession does not have one title. It has five or six, and they describe the same craft. Depending on the house, the country and the size of the operation, you will be called a private concierge, a lifestyle manager, a personal assistant, a house manager or an estate manager. The name on the contract changes. What you actually do does not: you look after the private life of one family, with discretion, anticipation and standards.

**The title.** What it usually means, in practice

**Private concierge · Lifestyle manager.** You look after the person. Their time, their travel, their requests, their life outside work. The purest expression of this craft, and the title most used in Europe and in the press.

**Personal assistant · Executive PA.** The same work, described in the language of the office. Calendar, correspondence, travel, personal affairs. This is the title you will see most often in an agency listing.

**House manager.** You look after the house. Suppliers, budgets, standards, staff, the physical running of one residence.

**Estate manager.** The same, at a larger scale. Several residences, real budgets, real teams. A senior step, reached after years.

**Chief of staff.** Both, across everything. The summit of a private-service career.

THE PRACTICAL CONSEQUENCE, AND IT MATTERS THIS WEEK

When you search for a role, search for all of these words, and not only the one that brought you here. An agency will very rarely advertise a "private concierge". It will advertise a personal assistant, a house manager, an executive PA. It is the same craft, the same house, the same principal, and the same skills you are learning here. The person who only searches for one word finds almost nothing, and concludes that the market is closed. It is not. She was looking for the wrong noun.

**1.5 · The vocabulary, from day one**

**Term.** Use

**Principal.** The person you serve. Never “boss”, never “client”, never “employer”. The word signals immediately that you know where you are.

**Household.** The residence and the people who run it. Not “the house” when you mean the organisation.

**Term.** Use

**The office.** Shorthand for the family office. “I’ll confirm with the office” is one of the most useful sentences you will ever learn.

**UHNWI.** Ultra-high-net-worth individual. Used in the industry and in agency briefs; never used in front of the principal.

**Live-in / live-out.** Whether accommodation is provided on the estate. It changes the job, the pay and the life. Ask early.

**Rota.** The staff schedule. In a serious household it is a document, not an understanding.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Private service is a relationship, not an industry role. There is no brand between you and the family. It is not hotel concierge work, not a membership company, not domestic staffing, not executive assistance, though it borrows from all four. The profession is old in vocabulary and new in expectation. Both halves shape how you are judged.

## A working taxonomy of wealth

*Because a role at $50M and a role at $2B share a title and nothing else*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Distinguish the tiers of private wealth, and what each means for your daily work. Recognise how the *source* of a fortune shapes the culture of the household. Ask, at interview, the two questions that reveal what the job actually is.

**2.1 · The tiers**

“Wealthy” is not a category. It is a ladder, and each rung is a different profession for you. This is not snobbery; it determines who employs you, what you do all day, who you report to, and what you are paid.

**Tier.** The household, and your role in it

**HNW · $1M to $30M.** One home, perhaps a second. Possibly a housekeeper and a nanny. A concierge, if any, is part-time and blended with other duties. There is no family office. This is not your market, and a role here will not teach you this profession.

**VHNW / lower UHNW · $30M to $250M.** Two to five residences. A small team: house manager, housekeeper, nanny, sometimes a chauffeur. Travel is private but often chartered. The family still decides most things personally, and you will speak to them daily. This is where most private concierge and lifestyle-manager roles live, and it is the best place to learn, because you will touch everything.

**Upper UHNW · $250M to $1B.** A structured operation. A family office exists. Above you sits an estate manager or a chief of staff. Aircraft owned rather than chartered, often a yacht. Security is a standing function. Roles are specialised: you will not do everything, and you will be measured on your own lane.

**Billionaire.** An institution. Dozens of staff across many properties, layers of governance, legal, communications, philanthropy, and a security apparatus. Vetting is serious and slow. You may not meet the principal for months, and you may serve them for years without a personal conversation. Extremely well paid, extremely constrained.

**2.2 · The source of the fortune shapes the house**

This is the kind of distinction that separates a practitioner from a beginner, and it is almost never written down. Two families of identical wealth can be entirely different employers, and the difference is usually where the money came from.

**Source.** The culture it produces

**Inherited / old money.** Understatement, patina, institutions, tradition. Staff are often long-serving, and the household has a memory. They notice codes acutely and forgive extravagance not at all. They will judge you on manners before competence. The pay is often below market; the tenure is long, and the reference is gold.

**Entrepreneurial / first generation.** Speed, informality, impatience, and a certain suspicion of ceremony. Decisions are instant and reversed without embarrassment. They pay well and expect to be answered at any hour. The risk is boundary collapse: with no inherited model of staff, they may treat you as a friend, then as an employee again, within the same week.

**Finance.** Process, metrics, reporting, delegation. They will want a system, and they will be pleased when you produce one. They understand professional distance and generally maintain it. Bonuses are real, and expectations during deal periods are absolute.

**Technology.** Youth, informality, unconventional hours, a horror of visible hierarchy, combined with an extreme, almost paranoid attention to privacy and data. Expect NDAs of unusual severity, device policies, and a distaste for anything that looks like ceremony.

**Royal / GCC households.** Scale, protocol, strict hierarchy, and gender-segregated staffing in some houses. Discretion is not a preference but a legal and cultural absolute. Extremely generous packages; a demanding and highly formal environment; an expectation that you understand religious and cultural observance without being told.

**Celebrity / entertainment.** Chaotic calendars, intense media exposure, and an entourage whose loyalties are unclear. Discretion is tested weekly rather than yearly. Tenure is short, and the greatest professional risk in the whole field of private service sits here.

**2.3 · The two questions**

You are permitted to ask them at interview, and you should. They are not impertinent; they are the questions a professional asks.

**ASK, ALWAYS**

“How many residences?” This tells you the true scale of the work, the travel, and the complexity of the logistics. “How many staff, and who would I report to?” This tells you whether you are the operation or a part of it, and it reveals the chain of command before you are inside it. Two numbers and one name. They describe the job more accurately than any job description you will ever be sent.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The tier of wealth determines the shape of the job more than the job title does. Most concierge and lifestyle-manager roles sit between $30M and $250M, and that band is the best classroom. The source of the fortune predicts the culture: old money judges manners; first-generation wealth erodes boundaries; tech obsesses over data; royal households formalise everything. Two questions at interview, residences and staff, reveal the truth of the role.

## The family office

*The chain of command, and how it breaks*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Describe the structure of a single- and multi-family office. Identify who signs, who decides, and whose trust must be won. Handle a contradiction between the principal and the office without being blamed by either.

**3.1 · What it is**

**DEFINITION**

Family office. A private structure established to manage the wealth and affairs of one family (a single-family office, SFO) or several (a multi-family office, MFO). Its remit typically includes investments, tax, legal, property, philanthropy, and, critically for you, the employment, payroll and contracts of household staff.

Above roughly a quarter of a billion, a family ceases to be a household and becomes an organisation with a head office. The consequences for you are immediate and practical: the person who interviews you may not be the person you serve; the person who signs your contract is rarely the person whose trust you must win; and the instruction you receive at 9 a.m. from one may be reversed at 3 p.m. by the other.

**3.2 · Who is who**

**Function.** What they control, and what it means for you

**Family office principal / CEO.** Strategy and governance. You will rarely deal with them, and when you do it is significant.

**Chief of staff.** Where one exists, this is your true line manager. They own the operation. Win them, and you are safe. Bypass them, and you are finished.

**Estate director / director of residences.** Properties, projects, budgets, vendors. Your work will pass across their desk constantly.

**Function.** What they control, and what it means for you

**Finance / controller.** Payroll, expenses, budget approval. Your monthly reporting goes here. They will judge you on tidiness, and they speak to everyone.

**Legal / general counsel.** Contracts, NDAs, incidents. If you ever need them, you need them urgently, and knowing when to escalate is itself a professional skill.

**Security director.** Movements, vetting, residences, travel. Nothing you plan happens without them.

**3.3 · The three questions of your first week**

Answer these before you do anything ambitious. They are not administrative details; they are the political map of your working life.

**· ·.** Who signs? Contracts, invoices, budget approvals. Usually the office. Who decides? On the day, in the house. Often the principal; often a spouse; sometimes a PA of fifteen years whom no one thought to put on the chart.

**·.** Whose trust must I win? Frequently a third person entirely, and it is the answer that decides whether you are still here in two years.

**3.4 · The contradiction, and the sentence that saves you**

This is the most common structural failure in the profession, and it destroys perfectly competent people. The principal asks you for something. The office refuses it, or would refuse it if asked. Both consider themselves final. You are in the middle, and whichever way you move, you are wrong. You do not choose a side. You do not quietly proceed and hope. You do not tell the principal that the office has refused, that is not your message to carry, and delivering it makes you the face of a refusal that is not yours.

**THE SENTENCE**

To the office: “The principal has asked me for this. I want to make sure it is handled correctly, how would you like me to proceed?” You have now returned the decision to the person whose decision it is, and you have made yourself the person who handles things correctly. Both are true, and both are visible. The staff member who resolves this alone, by guessing, is blamed by both sides. Always, and without exception.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Above roughly $250M, a family office sits between you and the family. Signing, deciding and trusting are three different people. Confusing them is the classic early error. A contradiction between house and office is escalated, calmly and in writing, never resolved by guesswork.

## The household as an organisation

*Stations, reporting lines, and the power map nobody draws*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Name every station in a private household and describe its remit. Distinguish the formal organisation chart from the real distribution of influence. Position yourself, as a newcomer, without provoking the people who can end you.

**4.1 · The stations**

Titles vary by country and by house. Functions do not. Learn them, and never step across a colleague’s station without being invited.

**Station.** Remit

**Principal.** The person you serve. There may be two, and they may not want the same things.

**Private / Executive PA.** Diary, correspondence, travel, and the border between professional and private life. Your closest ally or your rival. Choose ally, early and deliberately.

**House manager.** One residence: staff, vendors, standards, provisions, the running of the day.

**Estate manager.** Several residences, or one very large one. Budgets, contractors, projects, teams. Senior.

**Chief of staff.** The strategic head of the private operation. Years of accumulated trust, never a certificate.

**Butler.** The dining room, the cellar, the service, often the supervision of others. Anticipation and discretion, not formality.

**Head of housekeeping.** The condition of the house, every day. She sees everything. Underestimate her and your first month becomes impossible.

**Private chef.** The table, provisioning, the family’s dietary régime, and the guests’ requirements. Guard this relationship: a chef who likes you will save you.

**Nanny / governess.** The children. In a great many houses, the single most trusted person in the building.

**Station.** Remit

**Chauffeur.** Timing, routes, privacy, the door opened at the exact moment. Far more than the car.

**Close protection.** Movements, vetting, guest lists, residences, travel. Nothing you plan happens without them.

**Estate / grounds.** Gardens, maintenance, the outbuildings, the systems. Quiet, long-serving, and they hear everything.

**4.2 · The invisible hierarchy**

The organisation chart is not the power map, and it never has been. The nanny who has the mother’s ear may carry more weight than the estate manager. The chauffeur of fifteen years knows things the family office will never know. The head of housekeeping decides, in practice, whether your first month is pleasant or unsurvivable. Influence in a household is a function of three things, and none of them appear on a chart: tenure, proximity to the principal’s body and children, and the possession of information. Map these in your first month. Do it privately, and never speak of it.

**4.3 · The position of the newcomer**

You arrive as a threat. That is not paranoia; it is arithmetic. Every new hire implies that something was not working, and the people who were here before you know it. Your first month is not an audition of your competence, the family already believe you are competent, or they would not have hired you. It is an audition of your character, and the audience is the staff.

**·.** Be early. Not on time, early. It is noticed once, and then assumed, and being assumed reliable is the whole game.

**·.** Learn every name in the first week, including the gardener who comes on Tuesdays and the night security officer.

**·.** Take the staff meal with the staff, at least at first. The person who eats alone from day one is the person nobody warns when something is about to go wrong.

**·.** Change nothing for thirty days. Nothing, however obviously wrong it looks. The house has reasons you cannot see yet, and half of them are personal.

**·.** Never criticise another member of staff to the principal, even when asked directly.

Especially then: it is a test, whether or not they know it.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Know every station and its remit, and never cross one uninvited. Influence flows from tenure, physical proximity to the family, and possession of information. The newcomer is judged on character by the staff, and the staff are consulted.

## The economics of the role

*What it pays, what drives the number, and the shape of a career*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

State the indicative compensation bands for each role, and the factors that move them. Read a package rather than a salary. Describe a realistic ten-year trajectory in this profession.

**5.1 · Indicative bands**

The figures below are drawn from the 2026 private staff salary guides published by the specialist agencies of this sector, and from live agency briefs. They are a guide to the shape of the market, not a promise, and they vary enormously with residences, travel, languages, and the sensitivity of the household. Treat any single number in this profession with suspicion, including these.

**Role.** Indicative annual range (2026)

**Guest relations / villa or chalet host.** $55k to $120k, frequently seasonal, and often with accommodation provided

**Private concierge / lifestyle manager.** $70k to $150k+ (US) · £60k to £110k+ (UK and Europe)

**Private / executive PA to a UHNW principal.** up to $250k at the top of the US market · £70k to £100k+ (UK)

**House manager.** $110k to $190k in New York, the Hamptons and Palm Beach · £75k to £115k (UK)

**Estate manager.** median $185k, top quartile $260k+ (US) · £110k to £220k+ (UK)

**Chief of staff (private household).** $150k to $350k+

**Yacht / private aviation interior.** $50k to $130k+, structured by rotation

Two further facts, and they are the reason this profession is worth entering now. Estate manager pay has risen by twenty to thirty per cent since 2022. Demand for private assistants to UHNW principals has risen by twenty-five to thirty per cent over the same period. The supply of people who can genuinely be trusted has not moved at all.

**5.2 · Read the package, not the salary**

A stated salary in this field is frequently the smaller part of the story. Two offers that appear $30,000 apart can be identical, or the reverse.

**·.** Accommodation. Live-in, a cottage on the estate, or a rented flat paid for. Where it is provided on the estate it is commonly valued at $30k to $45k a year, and in Monaco, Geneva or London it can be worth far more.

**· ·.** Vehicle. Provided, or an allowance, or nothing. Travel. Whether you fly with the family, and in what class, and who pays for your journey home on your days off.

**·.** Health cover and pension. Enormously variable by country, and frequently the single largest hidden difference.

**·.** Bonus. Discretionary in nearly every house. Ask how it has been paid in the last three years, not how it is described.

**·.** The rota. Five days? Six? On call? Two weeks on, two off? This is not a benefit, it is the shape of your life, and it should be the first thing you negotiate and the last thing you concede.

**5.3 · What actually drives the number**

**Driver.** Effect

**Number of residences.** The strongest single driver. Each additional property adds vendors, budgets, staff and travel.

**Staff supervised.** A role that manages people is paid on a different scale from one that does not. This is the step-change in a career.

**Languages.** French, Italian, Spanish, Arabic, Russian, Mandarin. Each is a genuine multiplier in the right household.

**Mobility.** The willingness to relocate or to travel at short notice is priced, and priced well.

**Sensitivity.** Houses with unusual exposure, political, media, security, pay a premium for people who can carry it. The premium is real, and so is the cost.

**Driver.** Effect

Tenure and reference After the first role, you are paid for your reputation. This is why the first role matters mainly as a source of the second.

**5.4 · The trajectory**

A realistic shape, and you should hold it in mind rather than the fantasy, because the fantasy is what makes people quit in year two.

**Stage.** What it looks like

**Years 0 to 2.** The first role, very often below your ambition. Seasonal, or junior, or in a house that is not glamorous. Be impeccable in it. Its purpose is not the salary; its purpose is the reference.

**Years 2 to 5.** The second role, obtained largely on the strength of the first. Now the money moves. You begin to specialise, and the agencies begin to call you rather than the reverse.

**Years 5 to 10.** Seniority. You manage people, budgets, and projects. You are approached before roles are advertised, and the best positions in this field are never advertised at all.

**Years 10+.** Estate manager, chief of staff, or your own practice. Very few reach it, and those who do got there by never once damaging their name.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The bands are wide, and the package matters more than the salary. Residences, staff, languages, mobility and sensitivity drive the number. The first role is not the destination. It is the reference that buys the second, which is where the career actually begins.

## The service model

*Time, peace, discretion, and the request beneath the request*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Apply the three-lever test to any action before you take it. Unlearn the hospitality reflex, and know precisely why it is dangerous here. Decompress a request without pushing the work back onto the principal.

**6.1 · The triad**

They are not buying a service. They can buy any service on earth with a telephone call. They are buying three things, and they are buying them from you personally, because they cannot be bought from a company.

**TIME · PEACE · DISCRETION**

Time. Their day has twenty-four hours and far more claims on it than yours. Every decision you remove is time returned. This is measurable, and it is what justifies your salary. Peace. Not the absence of problems, the absence of the feeling of problems. A crisis you resolved and never mentioned did not occur. A crisis you resolved and then recounted in detail did occur, and you created it. Discretion. The permanent certainty that nothing about their life will leak, be sold, be posted, or become dinner-party currency. This is the one they can never verify, and therefore the one they watch most closely.

The test. Before any action: does this give them time, peace, or silence? If it gives them none of the three, you are decorating, not serving, and decoration in a private house is not neutral. It is noise, and noise is a cost.

**6.2 · The hospitality reflex**

**In a hotel.** In a house

**You are rewarded for what the guest notices.** You are rewarded for what the principal never has to notice.

**In a hotel.** In a house

**The flourish is the product. The upgrade is announced.** The flourish is an intrusion. The upgrade is simply there.

**The guest leaves in three days; you must be memorable.** The principal stays for years; you must be inevitable.

**Warmth is expressed.** Warmth is felt, and rarely expressed.

**Recovery is public: the apology, the compensation, the letter.** Recovery is invisible: it was handled before it arrived at their door.

This inversion is the hardest habit any hotelier has to unlearn, and it takes most people the better part of a year. The family will feel it long before they can name it. They will simply find you slightly tiring, and they will not be able to say why.

**6.3 · A taxonomy of requests**

**Type.** How it is handled

**Routine.** The reservation, the car, the delivery, the appointment. Handled without conversation. If a routine request requires a conversation, your systems are wrong.

**Exceptional.** The table that is full, the aircraft on four hours’ notice, the piece that is sold out. This is where your black book earns its keep, and where you are visibly worth your salary.

**Impossible.** It cannot be done. Say so, immediately, and arrive with the two best alternatives already secured. The professional is not the one who never fails; it is the one who never leaves a vacuum.

**Improper.** It should not be done. This is the category nobody trains you for, and the one that ends careers. Chapter IX and Module 2 are devoted to it.

**6.4 · The request beneath the request**

A request is almost never what it says. It is the visible tip of a need, and it arrives compressed, half-formed, and at an inconvenient hour. Your work is to decompress it without asking the principal to do that work for you. Every question you ask is a small tax on their attention, and four questions is an insult dressed as diligence.

**WORKED EXAMPLE**

21:04, Tuesday. The message reads, in full: “Do we still have that restaurant in Rome?” The beginner replies: Which restaurant? What date? For how many? Will Mr, be joining?, Four taxes on the principal’s evening, and no progress whatsoever. The professional does five things before replying. Reads the diary: Rome, Thursday. Reads the preference file: the restaurant, the table by the window, the two who dined there in March. Calls the restaurant: full. Calls the maître d’ she has cultivated for two years, precisely for an evening like this one: not full. Holds two times. Then replies, once: “Yes. Thursday, 8pm or 9.30pm, whichever suits. I have kept both.” One question remains, and it is easy to answer. That sentence is the entire profession in a single line.

Notice what made it possible, because none of it was improvisation: a diary that was read, a preference file that was maintained, and a relationship that was built long before the night it was needed. Anticipation is not a gift. It is infrastructure.

**6.5 · Two standing principles**

**·.** No surprises. Not to the principal, not to the office, not to your line manager. A bad fact delivered early is a manageable fact. The same fact delivered late is a crisis, and the crisis is now about you.

**·.** Never become the single point of failure. It is tempting to hold everything in your own head, the passwords, the contacts, the arrangements, because it feels like security. It is the opposite. Families do not reward staff who cannot be replaced; they fear them, and fear leads to a quiet reduction of your access. The people who last document everything so well that they could be replaced tomorrow, and are, for exactly that reason, never replaced.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Every action must buy time, peace or discretion. Otherwise it is decoration. Hotel instincts reward visibility; private service rewards invisibility. Unlearn it deliberately. Requests are routine, exceptional, impossible, or improper. The fourth is the one that ends careers. Decompress the request yourself. The number of questions in your reply is your professional signature.

## The principal

*A working psychological profile*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Explain why decision fatigue governs almost everything about how you communicate. Understand privacy as a security condition rather than a preference. Recognise the confidante trap before you are in it.

**7.1 · Decision fatigue**

They make consequential decisions all day, and the supply of that capacity is finite. Therefore: never bring them a decision they did not need to make. Bring two options and a recommendation, or bring nothing at all and tell them afterwards. The staff member who arrives with an open question is asking to be paid for work they have not done.

**7.2 · The asymmetry**

Almost every person in their life wants something: an investment, an introduction, a job, a donation, an association, a story. They have been asked for something by nearly everyone they have ever met, including people who claimed to want nothing. The consequence is the deepest source of your professional value: the person who asks for nothing becomes precious. And it explains a rule that seems severe until you understand it, never ask the principal for a favour. Not an introduction, not a recommendation for your cousin, not a ticket, not advice on your own investments. The moment you ask, you have joined the several thousand people who want something, and you can never leave that category again.

**7.3 · Privacy is safety**

What feels like secrecy to you is, to them, the thin membrane between their children and a real risk. Their address, their movements, the school run, the make of the car, the week the house is empty. Kidnap and extortion are not abstractions in this world; they are a line item in the security budget, and the security director you find tiresome is the person who has read the threat assessment that you have not. Understand this and you will never again be careless with an itinerary, a photograph, or a supplier who “just needs to know when the family will be away”.

**7.4 · Loneliness, and the confidante trap**

They are frequently lonely inside their own house, surrounded, and alone. You will be present at the difficult moments, because you are always present. One evening, tired, they will tell you something they should not have told you, and the next morning they will be formal and brisk as though it never occurred. That evening is not an invitation to intimacy. It is a debt of discretion, and it is the heaviest one you will carry. The following morning you are exactly as you were before: warm, professional, entirely unchanged. You do not soften your voice. You do not offer the sympathetic look. You never refer to it again unless they do. Being trusted feels like being chosen. It is not. It is a professional condition, not a personal one. The staff member who becomes a confidante is, in time, quietly moved on, because they have become a witness, and nobody wishes to live alongside a witness.

**7.5 · Two principals, and the children**

**·.** Where there are two principals, they do not always want the same thing. You will be caught between them, and it will be over something small. Serve both, favour neither, and never carry a message that has an edge on it. If asked to keep something from the other, decline gently and immediately, before it becomes a pattern you cannot escape.

**·.** The children are not your friends, and they are not your charges unless you are the nanny. Be warm, be consistent, be entirely uninvolved in discipline, and never, under any circumstances, become the adult a child confides in about their parents.

**·.** An ex-spouse is a permanent structural hazard. Information, schedules, and the children.

Ask the office, once, what your instruction is, and then follow it exactly.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Never bring a decision they did not need to make. The person who asks for nothing is precious. Never ask for a favour. Privacy is a security condition, not a preference. Trust is professional, not personal. The confidante becomes a witness, and the witness is moved on.

## The year

*The calendar these families live by*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Reproduce the seasonal calendar of the UHNW world from memory. Time your applications to the hiring seasons rather than to your own impatience. Schedule house projects into the windows when the family is away.

Nobody teaches this, and everyone senior simply knows it. These families move on a calendar, and the calendar is remarkably stable from year to year. Learn it and you become useful in your first month rather than your sixth.

**Period.** Where the world is

**December, March.** The Alps. Gstaad, Courchevel, Verbier, St Moritz, Zermatt, Aspen. Chalet and villa staff are recruited in September and October, and again, in panic, in late November.

**January.** Davos. The art and auction calendar begins. School terms govern everything where there are children, and they govern it absolutely.

**April, May.** The Riviera reopens; the villas are prepared. The Monaco Grand Prix makes the entire coast unbookable for a fortnight. Easter, and the first openings.

**June, August.** The Mediterranean. Saint-Tropez, Sardinia, Ibiza, the Greek islands, the yachts. The Hamptons for American families. Peak demand, peak improvisation, and the season in which reputations are made and lost.

**September.** The return. City, school, and the beginning of the works that were saved for the empty months.

**October, November.** Art fairs, auctions, the philanthropic season, the galas. Winter staff are hired now, and the good roles are gone by mid-November.

**TWO PRACTICAL CONSEQUENCES**

Hiring. Seasonal roles, the most realistic first rung for a newcomer, are filled three to four months in advance. If you apply in December for a winter season, you are applying for next year, and you did not know it. Projects. Moves, renovations, deep cleans and installations are scheduled into the weeks the family is away. The empty house is not your quiet period. It is your busiest, and it is where your real work is done and seen.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The UHNW year is stable and knowable. Learn it before your first interview. Seasonal roles are filled three to four months ahead. The family’s absence is your working season, not your holiday.

## Failure

*How careers end in this profession, and how they are saved*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

List the five causes of dismissal, and recognise that none is technical. Detect the early signals that a household is withdrawing from you. Handle your own error in the ninety minutes that decide everything.

**9.1 · The five dismissals**

Note what is absent from this list: technical incompetence. By the time you are hired, competence is assumed. You will not be dismissed for failing to secure a table. You will be dismissed for one of these five.

**Cause.** Why it is fatal

**Indiscretion.** However small, however innocent. A photograph, a name, a story told to one friend. It is the fastest, and it is final, and it is unforgiven because it cannot be undone.

**A lie about a mistake.** Never the mistake itself. Every house has seen a hundred errors. What no house forgives is the discovery that it was softened, delayed or managed. Once they believe you manage them, everything you say is re-read.

**Familiarity.** The slow drift from professional to friend. It feels like success. It is the beginning of the end: they cannot manage a friend, and they will not dismiss one, they will simply find less and less for you to do.

**Making them feel a burden.** A sigh. A hesitation. A visible calculation before you answer. It will never be mentioned to you, and it will never be forgotten.

**Visible fatigue or complaint.** The hours are genuinely hard. Carrying that privately is part of what you are paid for. Complain to your partner, your friends, your mentor. Never in the house, and never to another member of staff.

**9.2 · The early signals**

A household almost never dismisses without a period of withdrawal first. Learn to read it, because it is recoverable if you catch it early and almost never recoverable once it is complete.

**· · · · ·.** You are told things later than you used to be. A task you used to own is given, without explanation, to someone else. The principal begins to copy the office on messages that used to come to you alone. An outside supplier is engaged for something you would have handled. Politeness increases and warmth decreases. In this world, that combination is the loudest possible signal. If you see two of these, do not become anxious and do not become obsequious, both accelerate it. Ask for ten minutes, and ask one question: “Is there anything you would like me to be doing differently?” Then listen without defending. The single most common reason a household lets someone go is that they never asked.

**9.3 · The ninety minutes after your own error**

You will make a serious mistake. The flight on the wrong day; the guest who was not on the list; the payment that did not go. What is tested is not the error. It is the ninety minutes that follow it.

**THE PROTOCOL**

1. Establish the facts. Precisely, quickly, without speculation. What is true, what is not yet known. 2. Build the remedy before you speak. What are the actual options now? Arrive with the problem and two solutions, and a recommendation. If you have none, you are not ready to have the conversation. 3. Tell them the same day. Plainly: “I made an error with the date. Here is where we are, here are the two ways forward, and I recommend the first.” 4. Never use the passive voice. “The booking was made incorrectly” is a lie wearing grammar. They will hear it, and they will hear it as a lie. 5. Then fix the system, not the incident, and tell them what you have changed. That sentence is what converts an error into a reason to trust you more than before.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Nobody is dismissed for incompetence. They are dismissed for indiscretion, for concealment, for familiarity, for burdening, or for complaint. Withdrawal precedes dismissal, and it is legible. Politeness up, warmth down. Own an error the same day, with the remedy already in motion, in the active voice.

## Glossary of the module

Principal, The individual served. The correct and only term in a private household. Family office, The private structure managing a family’s wealth and affairs; frequently the legal employer of household staff. SFO / MFO, Single-family office (one family) / multi-family office (several). Chief of staff, The senior head of a private operation, above estate and house management. Estate manager, Manager of one or several residences: staff, budgets, contractors, standards. House manager, Manager of a single residence and its daily running. Preference file, The structured record of a principal’s preferences, aversions, people and dates. The central instrument of anticipation. Black book, The private register of trusted suppliers and contacts, built over years and never lent. Rota, The staff schedule: duty, cover, rotation, time off. Live-in / live-out, Whether accommodation is provided. It changes the role, the pay, and the life. Close protection, The security detail around a principal and their family. NDA, Non-disclosure agreement. The floor of discretion, never its standard. The triad, Time, peace, discretion, the three things a principal is actually buying. Improper request, A request that should not be fulfilled, distinct from one that cannot be. The category that ends careers.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing, without returning to the text. If you cannot answer eight of these, read the module again before you begin Module 3.

1. Name the three things a principal is actually buying, and rank them for a first-generation technology entrepreneur. Defend the order. 2. What are the two questions you ask at interview, and what does each reveal?

3. A principal asks you for something the family office would refuse. What is the exact sentence you use, and to whom? 4. Give three sources of influence in a household that do not appear on the organisation chart.

5. What is the difference between an impossible request and an improper one? Give an example of each. 6. You discover at 23:00 that you booked tomorrow’s flight on the wrong day. Describe your next ninety minutes, in order. 7. Why must you never ask the principal for a favour? Answer in terms of the asymmetry.

8. What five signals suggest a household is withdrawing from you?

9. When are winter chalet roles recruited, and what does that mean for your applications?

10. Explain the “single point of failure” error, and why holding all the information is a professional weakness rather than a strength. 11. Why is the hospitality reflex dangerous in a private house? Give two concrete behaviours you must unlearn. 12. The principal confides something painful late one evening. Describe your conduct the following morning, in detail.

**Further study**

None of the following is required. All of it will make you better, and it is the kind of reading that shows in a conversation.

**·.** Read the agency briefs, weekly. Morgan & Mallet, Silver Swan, Tiger, Career Group, The

Calendar Group. Not to apply, to learn the language in which this world describes what it wants. Read fifty of them and you will write a CV that fits the field without being told how.

**·.** Read the wealth reports. The annual UHNW population and geography studies published by the large property and consulting firms. You will learn where the money is going, which is where the roles are going.

**·.** Read one serious book on the history of service, the great household, the butler tradition, the brigade. It will give you the lineage of every word you use.

**·.** Follow the calendar. The Monaco Grand Prix, Art Basel, the auction seasons, the school terms of the great schools. Know when the world moves.

**·.** Study the Dictionary of Luxury, which accompanies this programme, entry by entry.

Week Two will assume it.

Maison Amarine · The Private Concierge Academy · Confidential, for the use of our mentees only

## The economy of trust

*Why your name is the only asset you truly own*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Explain why reputation, not skill, is the currency of this profession. Understand the structure of the market that makes this true. Apply the two-year rule to your first role.

**1.1 · A village of a few thousand people**

Private service feels vast from outside and is, in reality, extremely small. A few hundred serious agencies. A few thousand households that employ at this level. Consultants who have known each other for twenty years, and who speak. Principals who discuss their staff at dinner, in front of other principals, with complete frankness and no expectation of consequence.

The practical consequence is absolute, and it governs every decision in this module: you will change houses four or five times in a working life, and every one of those doors will be opened, or closed, by someone who knew someone. Your reputation is not a component of your career. It is your career, and everything else, your languages, your competence, your charm, is a decoration upon it.

**1.2 · What this changes**

· Never leave a house badly, whatever the provocation. Serve your notice, hand over impeccably, and say nothing. The satisfaction of the honest exit speech lasts an afternoon; its cost lasts a decade. · Never take a role you intend to abandon in three months. A short tenure with no explanation is the most damaging line on a private CV, and you will be asked about it in every interview for the rest of your life. · Never burn a consultant. Not by applying twice for the same role through two agencies, not by cancelling an interview at short notice, not by accepting a position and then withdrawing. Their reputation is on the table when they present you, and they will not risk it twice. · Never speak ill of a former household. Ever. Not to a friend, not to a colleague, not in an interview when invited to, and not when you are certain you were wronged, which you sometimes will have been.

**THE TWO-YEAR RULE**

Take the first honourable role you are offered, even if it is beneath your ambition, and be impeccable in it for two years. Two years is the minimum tenure that reads as stability. It is also, not by coincidence, roughly the time required for a household to trust you completely. The second role is given by reputation. The third is offered before you have asked. There is no way to skip this, and every attempt to do so is visible to everyone but the person attempting it.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The industry is a village. Everything you do is known. Reputation is not part of your career; it is your career. Two years, impeccably served, buys everything that follows.

## Information hygiene

*A doctrine, not a virtue*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Classify every piece of information you hold, and know its permitted channel. Apply the three questions before speaking. Operate your devices and your social presence as a professional, not as a private person.

**2.1 · Discretion is not silence**

Most people believe discretion means keeping secrets. It does not, and that misunderstanding is why so many careful people fail. Anyone can keep a secret; a secret announces itself as one. The craft is in the thousand pieces of information that are not secrets, a departure date, a supplier’s name, a child’s school, a mood at breakfast, the fact that the office called twice, and which, assembled by someone patient, describe a family completely.

**DEFINITION**

Information hygiene. The disciplined management of the flow of information around a principal: what is recorded, where, in what channel, to whom, and for how long. It is a system, practised identically on quiet days and difficult ones.

**2.2 · A classification**

**Class.** Examples, and the rule

**Public.** What the principal has themselves made public. Even here: you do not comment, you do not confirm, you do not amplify. Silence costs nothing.

**Household.** Routines, suppliers, staff, standards. Shared within the house on a need-to-know basis, and never outside it. Not even with your family, who will not understand why.

**Movement.** Where the family is, when, for how long, with whom, and when a residence is empty. This is security information. It travels only through the channels the house has designated, and never in reply to a friendly question.

**Personal.** Health, relationships, finances, children, conflicts. It does not leave you. It does not enter a message. It is not discussed with colleagues, including those who already know.

**Class.** Examples, and the rule

**Legal / incident.** Anything that may become a matter for lawyers, police or press. It goes to the office immediately, in writing, and you make no decisions of your own.

**2.3 · The three questions, before you speak**

· Does this person need this in order to do their work? · Would the principal be comfortable hearing me say this, in this room, to this person? · If this sentence were repeated to a stranger, could it be traced back to the house? If any answer is uncertain, the sentence does not get said. There is no penalty for having said less.

**2.4 · Devices, messages, and the record**

· Assume everything you write will one day be read by the principal, by a lawyer, or by a journalist. Write accordingly. This is not paranoia; it is simply true, and it costs nothing to obey. · Never use a personal messaging application for household business unless the house has instructed it. Use the channel they designate, and only that one. · Never photograph anything. Not the view, not the aircraft, not the table you laid beautifully. The photograph that ends a career is almost never malicious; it is proud. · Lock your devices, and enable remote wipe. A lost telephone containing a principal’s diary is a security incident, and it must be reported as one, immediately, whatever it costs you in embarrassment. · Keep your own record, dated, factual, unemotional, of instructions received and decisions taken. Not to defend yourself, but because one day you will need to remember exactly what was said, and memory is not evidence.

**2.5 · Social media**

This is the fastest career-ending mechanism in the profession, and it claims someone every season.

· No photograph taken inside a residence, a car, an aircraft or a yacht. None. Ever. Not for your mother. · No location. No “I can’t say where I am, but ”. That is a boast, and a boast is a breach, it announces both that you know something and that you enjoy holding it. · No public account that permits an employer to be deduced. Consultants will look, and so will the family’s security team, and so, occasionally, will a journalist. · This is not a recommendation. In most serious houses it is a term of employment, and its breach is immediate cause.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Discretion is a system, not a virtue: classify, channel, and say less. Movement information is security information. Treat it that way always. Everything you write will be read by someone else. Everything you photograph can end you.

## Money, gifts, and the quiet

*corruptions*

Almost nobody is dismissed for dishonesty; a great many for ambiguity

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Operate a household budget in a way that is unimpeachable. Recognise and refuse the supplier commission, in all its disguises. Receive a gift correctly.

**3.1 · Why this chapter exists**

Trust in a household is destroyed by money more often than by any other single cause, and it is almost never destroyed by theft. It is destroyed by ambiguity, a receipt that was not kept, a sum that was advanced and then mentioned, a supplier who is “a friend”, a bonus that was assumed. From outside, ambiguity and dishonesty are indistinguishable, and you will not be given the opportunity to explain the difference.

**3.2 · The rules of the household budget**

· Never advance a significant sum from your own funds. It feels generous and eager, and it is neither: it creates an unclear debt and makes you look either naïve or as though you expect something. Ask for a house card or a float, calmly, and insist gracefully until you have one. · Every receipt, every time, without exception. The one missing receipt is the one that will be remembered, and it will be remembered at exactly the wrong moment. · Report before you are asked. A short monthly summary, unprompted, in the same format each month. A family office will forgive a great deal from someone whose numbers are always tidy and always early. · Separate your money from theirs, absolutely. Never a shared account, never a personal card “just this once”, never a loan in either direction, never holding cash at home. · Where there is a threshold for approval, respect it even when it is inconvenient, and especially when the principal has told you not to bother. Their instruction does not protect you; the office’s record does.

**3.3 · The commission, and why it is fatal**

A supplier will one day offer you a percentage. It will not be presented as a bribe. It will be a “finder’s fee”, a “trade discount we can share”, a case of wine at Christmas that becomes two, a hotel that upgrades you personally. It is one of the oldest arrangements in this industry and it is entirely normal in some adjacent trades, which is precisely why it is so easy to accept.

Refuse it, and tell the house the same day. Not because you are pious, but because of what it does to you structurally: the moment you take it, you no longer work for the family. You work for the supplier, and every recommendation you make thereafter is compromised, and you will know it, and eventually so will they. In this world it is called a kickback, and there is no recovery from it.

If a supplier offers a genuine trade discount, it belongs to the household, and you say so, in writing, and the household is delighted with you.

**3.4 · Gifts**

**HOW TO RECEIVE**

You will be given things: a dress, a watch, a bag, an envelope, a case of wine, a holiday. Accept graciously. Thank once, briefly, in person if you can. Then never mention it again. Do not photograph it. Do not wear it in front of them for at least a season. Never speak of its value, and never to another member of staff, the gift that is discussed becomes a grievance in someone else’s hands. A gift accepted with too much emotion changes the relationship as surely as a gift refused.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Ambiguity about money is indistinguishable from dishonesty. Eliminate ambiguity. Never advance your own funds. Every receipt. Report early, always. Refuse the commission and disclose it the same day. It is not a moral question; it is a structural one.

## The politics of a household

*A sealed system with no appeal*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Position yourself among long-serving staff without threatening them. Refuse, gracefully, to take a side. Serve two principals who do not want the same thing.

**4.1 · What kind of system this is**

A private household is a small, sealed political system with no human resources department, no grievance procedure, and no appeal. Decisions are made on feeling, and they are made quickly. Understand it and you will thrive; ignore it and it will destroy you regardless of how well you do the actual work.

**4.2 · The long-serving**

The PA of twelve years. The chauffeur of fifteen. The nanny who raised the children. They cannot be beaten, and they should not be beaten. They can only be made into allies, and the method is very simple and slightly humbling: ask their advice, take it visibly, and credit them in front of the principal. You will lose nothing. They have no ambition to take your role, they have their own, and it is secure, and in exchange they will tell you things that would otherwise take you three years to learn.

**4.3 · Never take a side**

· In a staff conflict there is no winning side. There is only the side that leaves, and it is usually the newest person, because a family will always protect the old. · Never criticise a colleague to the principal, even when asked directly, especially then. It is a test, whether or not the principal knows they are administering one. “She is finding her feet, and I am helping her” is a complete and adequate answer, and it is also, usually, true. · Do not carry grievances upward. Solve what you can, absorb what you must, escalate only what genuinely endangers the family or the house.

**4.4 · Two principals**

Where there are two, they will not always want the same thing, and one day you will be standing between them holding a message. Serve both. Favour neither. Never carry a message that has an edge on it, and never soften one either, you are not a diplomat, and improvised diplomacy between spouses is how staff are dismissed by the one they were trying to protect.

If you are asked, gently, to keep something from the other, decline immediately and warmly, before it becomes a pattern from which there is no exit: “I’d rather not be in the middle of that, I hope you understand.” It will cost you a moment of awkwardness. Its alternative costs you the role.

**4.5 · The single point of failure**

It is tempting to make yourself irreplaceable by holding everything in your own head: the passwords, the contacts, the arrangements, the file only you can read. It feels like security.

It is the opposite. Families do not reward staff who cannot be replaced; they fear them, and fear does not produce promotion, it produces a quiet, deliberate reduction of your access, and eventually a replacement recruited in secret. The professionals who last are those who document everything so completely that they could be replaced tomorrow, and who are, precisely for that reason, never replaced.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The household is a political system without an appeal process. Make allies of the long-serving by asking, taking and crediting their advice. Take no side, carry no edged message, keep no secret between two principals. Document everything. Irreplaceability is a weakness disguised as power.

## The improper request

*The category nobody prepares you for*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Distinguish the impossible from the improper. Deploy the prepared refusal without moralising, arguing, or improvising. Escalate correctly, the same day, in writing.

**5.1 · The shape it takes**

It comes for everyone, usually within the first year, and never in the form you rehearsed. It will be small. It will be reasonable. It will be delivered with a smile by someone charming, and it will be very easy to say yes.

· A journalist who is “only confirming something we already have”. · A friend of the family, whom you have met at the house a dozen times, who asks warmly how long the house will be empty in August. · A supplier who would be so grateful for an introduction, and who has been so good to you. · The assistant of someone important, who needs “just the address, we have it anyway”. · A member of the family, often not the principal, who asks you to keep something from the other. · An instruction to alter a date on a document, or to say that you were somewhere you were not. It will be trivial. It will be the beginning.

**5.2 · Why the charming version is the dangerous one**

Note the technique in the first example: the caller claims to know already, so that confirming feels like nothing. And note the second: familiarity dissolves judgement, which is exactly why a real breach almost always arrives wearing the face of a friend rather than the face of a stranger.

**5.3 · The prepared refusal**

**LEARN THESE BY HEART**

To the press: “I’m not able to help with that.” Then end the call, courteously, and add nothing. Do not confirm. Do not deny, a denial is information. Do not say “I’ll have to check”, which is a confirmation that you could. To the charming friend: “I honestly don’t keep track of the family’s plans, the office would know.” Warm, unremarkable, and closed. To the supplier: “I don’t make introductions, I’m afraid, but do send your details to the office.” To anything that troubles you: “I’m not able to help with that, but let me pass it to the office.” You do not argue. You do not moralise. You do not improvise. A line drawn in calm holds; a line you must find in the moment does not.

**5.4 · Then escalate**

The same day. In writing. One paragraph, factual: who asked, when, what they wanted, what you said. No drama, no accusation, no speculation about their motive.

Two things follow from this, and both are worth the small discomfort. First, if it was innocent, nothing happens and you have lost nothing. Second, if it was not, you are the person who noticed, and being the person who notices is precisely the person a household cannot afford to lose.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The improper request is small, charming, and easy to grant. That is its design. Have the sentences ready before you need them. Refuse without argument, escalate the same day, in writing, without drama.

## Boundaries, and the cost of this work

*The part of the profession that is never advertised*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise the confidante trap and the friendship drift. Protect a life outside the house, deliberately and structurally. Know what burnout looks like in this profession, and what to do before it arrives.

**6.1 · Warmth without intimacy**

You will be warm. You will be present at births, illnesses, funerals, arguments, and the ordinary Tuesday evenings of a family’s life. And you will not be their friend, and they will not be yours, and the whole art of the profession is holding those two facts at the same time without becoming cold.

Being trusted feels like being chosen. It is not. Trust here is a professional condition. The staff member who becomes a confidante is, in time, quietly moved on, not out of cruelty, but because they have become a witness, and no one wishes to live alongside a witness.

**6.2 · The drift, and how to notice it**

· You are invited to sit down and stay when the work is finished, and you begin to want to. · You are told things about the other principal. · You find yourself defending them to your own friends, with feeling. · You feel a small, private pleasure at being needed at midnight. · You stop taking your days off, and you tell yourself it is because they need you.

Each of these is a symptom of the same thing: the role has begun to supply something it was never meant to supply. It ends badly, always, and the ending is not usually theirs, it is yours, in the form of a collapse.

**6.3 · Structural protection**

· Take your days off, physically away. A day off spent in the house is not a day off; it is a day of quiet duty. · Keep one relationship, outside this world, that has nothing to do with it, and never tell them anything you should not.

· Have a professional confidant: a mentor, a former colleague at another house, a supervisor. Someone with whom you can discuss the shape of a problem without ever giving its content. This is what keeps people sane. · Money is not a substitute for a life, and this profession pays well enough that you may not notice you have made that trade until several years have passed.

**6.4 · Leaving well**

One day you will leave, and how you leave will be worth more than everything you did while you were there. Serve your notice in full. Hand over completely, the file, the black book’s relevant contacts, the systems, the calendar, all of it, generously and without withholding. Say nothing to anyone, then or later. Ask, at the end, for a written reference, and ask warmly.

The household you leave impeccably will recommend you for the rest of your working life. That is not sentiment. That is the entire mechanism of this industry, described in one sentence.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Warmth, always. Intimacy, never. Holding both is the art. Protect your days off, your outside life, and one professional confidant. Leave impeccably. The exit is the reference, and the reference is the career.

## Houses I have seen

*Five incidents, and what they teach*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise the structure of a breach before it becomes one. Extract the general rule from the particular failure.

**The proud photograph**

The situation. A guest-relations manager in a chalet photographs a beautifully laid table, at night, empty, with the mountains behind it. No family, no faces, no names. She posts it. Three days later a journalist identifies the chalet from the window, and the family’s presence in the valley, which had been kept quiet, is published.

The lesson. She was dismissed, and she was correct in believing that she had done nothing malicious. The lesson is not about malice. A photograph does not need to contain a person to reveal one. The window did it. The lesson: no photographs, of anything, from inside the private world. There is no safe version of this.

**The friendly supplier**

The situation. A house manager accepts, over two years, a series of small kindnesses from a favoured contractor: a case of wine, a weekend, a discount on his own kitchen. He continues to award the contractor work, and the work is genuinely good.

The lesson. A routine audit by the family office notices the pattern. The work being good made no difference whatsoever. He no longer worked for the family, and everyone could see it but him. The lesson: refuse, disclose, and route every trade advantage back to the household.

**The empty diary**

The situation. A PA, asked at a party by an old university friend what her employer is “really like”, says almost nothing, but she does say, laughing, that she will finally get a holiday in August because “they’ll all be away for three weeks”.

The lesson. The friend repeats it, harmlessly, to someone else. The residence is burgled during the second week of August. There is no evidence of any connection, and it does not matter: she is asked to leave, because the household can no longer be certain. Movement information is security information, and it does not become safe because the room is friendly.

**The softened problem**

The situation. A concierge realises he has missed a visa requirement for a trip four days away. He believes he can resolve it. He says nothing for two days, works desperately, and nearly succeeds. On the third day he tells the office, who cannot now fix it in time.

The lesson. He was not dismissed for the visa. He was dismissed for the two days. A bad fact delivered early is a manageable fact; the same fact delivered late is a crisis, and the crisis is now about you. No surprises. Ever. Even when you believe you can save it.

**The confidante**

The situation. An estate manager becomes, over eighteen months, the person a principal talks to. She is discreet. She never repeats a word. She is also, by the end, the only person in the house who knows the state of the marriage.

The lesson. When the marriage ends, she is let go, with generosity and without explanation. She had done nothing wrong, and that is precisely the point. She had become a witness, and the family needed the witness gone. The lesson is not that she should have been colder. It is that she should have understood, from the first evening, what she was becoming, and remained, warmly, exactly what she was hired to be.

## Glossary of the module

Information hygiene, The systematic management of what is recorded, said, sent and kept about a principal. Movement information, Where the family is, when, and when a residence is empty. Always treated as security information. Kickback, A commission or benefit from a supplier. Fatal, and structurally so. The improper request, A request that should not be fulfilled, as distinct from one that cannot be. The confidante trap, The drift from trusted professional to personal confidant, which ends in removal. No surprises, The standing principle that a bad fact is delivered early, always. The two-year rule, The minimum tenure that reads as stability, and the time required to be trusted completely.

**Self-assessment**

Write your answers. Bring the two you found hardest to your mentorship session.

1. Why is reputation, rather than skill, the currency of this profession? Answer in terms of the structure of the market. 2. Classify these into the five information classes: the chef’s name; the date of departure for Sardinia; the principal’s medication; a public gala photograph; a threatening letter received at the house. 3. A contractor offers you fifteen per cent on everything you send him. Give your answer to him, and your next action. 4. The principal’s spouse asks you not to mention a purchase to the principal. What exactly do you say?

5. What are the five signs that you are drifting into the confidante trap?

6. Write, from memory, the four prepared refusals.

7. You have made an error that you believe you can fix quietly in two days. Explain, in one paragraph, why you will not. 8. Why does a household fear an irreplaceable member of staff?

9. You are leaving a house that treated you badly. Describe your exit, in detail, and explain what it buys you.
