## How to work a case

*And why this is the hardest module of the week*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what a case examines, and what it does not. Adopt a repeatable method rather than an instinct.

A case in this profession is never a test of knowledge. You will not be asked which fork, or which vintage. You will be asked what you do at 21:04 on a Tuesday, with incomplete information, when the person who could clarify it is precisely the person you must not disturb.

What is being examined, in every case below, is judgement under three constraints: incomplete information, an unequal relationship, and the impossibility of asking too many questions. That triangle is the whole profession. Everything else is logistics.

Work them honestly. Write the answer you would actually give, not the answer that sounds professional. The distance between those two answers is exactly the distance you have left to travel, and the point of this module is to measure it while it is still cheap to do so.

## The analytical framework

*Five questions, in this order, every time*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Apply a repeatable five-step method to any request or incident. Produce a defensible action, not a reaction.

**Step.** The question

**1 · Read.** What has actually been said, and what has been left out? Write the missing information down; do not fill it with assumption.

**2 · Sources.** What do I already possess that answers it? The diary. The preference file. The last time this happened. Almost every request is half-answered by a document you already hold.

**3 · The need.** What is the need beneath the request? Time, peace, or discretion, which of the three is at stake here? Name it explicitly.

**4 · The people.** Who signs, who decides, whose trust is at risk in this specific situation? Who else in the house is affected, and have they been told?

**5 · The action.** What do I do, in what order, and what single easy question, if any, remains for the principal? If my reply contains more than one question, I have not finished working.

**THE DISCIPLINE**

Every question you ask the principal is a small tax on their attention. Four questions is not diligence; it is an invoice for work you were paid to do. The number of questions in your reply is your professional signature. Senior practitioners ask one, or none.

## The ten cases

Write your answer to each case first. Only then tap Reveal the debrief on that case.

Case I · The restaurant 21:04, Tuesday. The principal writes, in full: “Do we still have that restaurant in Rome?” Nothing else. No date, no restaurant, no number of guests.

**· · ·.** What do you do first, and what do you deliberately not do? Which documents already in your possession answer part of this? What exactly do you write back, and how many questions does your reply contain?

Case II · The contradiction The principal asks you, directly, to book a villa in Sardinia for three weeks in August. Two days later the family office informs you that no discretionary expenditure above a threshold is approved this quarter, and that they were not consulted.

**· · ·.** Who is right? What do you do today, and in what channel? What must you never do, and why is it fatal?

Case III · The journalist A courteous, well-informed caller says they are preparing a profile, that they already have most of the details, and that they need only to confirm the spelling of a name and whether the family still owns the house in the country.

**· · ·.** What do you say, word for word, in the moment? What do you do in the following ten minutes? Why is “I’ll have to check” a catastrophic answer?

Case IV · The empty house A charming friend of the family, whom you have met at the house a dozen times, mentions in passing that they hear the family will be away in August, and asks warmly how long the house will be empty.

**· · ·.** Is this an innocent question, and does it matter whether it is? What do you answer? Does your familiarity with this person change anything? Be honest about what it does to your instinct.

Case V · The mistake At 23:00 you discover that you have booked tomorrow morning’s flight for the wrong day. The family are travelling with two children and a nanny.

**· · ·.** What is your first action, before any communication? When exactly do you tell them, and what are your opening two sentences? Which grammatical construction must not appear anywhere in your message, and why?

Case VI · The confidence Late one evening, tired and unusually open, the principal tells you something painful and personal about their marriage. The next morning they are formal and brisk, as though the conversation had never occurred.

**· · ·.** What do you do the following morning, in detail, voice, manner, eye contact? What do you do with what you now know? What is the trap, and on what timescale does it close?

Case VII · The commission A contractor you like, and whose work is genuinely excellent, offers you “a small finder’s fee, everyone does it” on the works you have been sending him for the past year. He is entirely relaxed about it, and slightly surprised by your hesitation.

**· · ·.** What do you say to him? What do you do next, and by when? Explain, without moralising, why accepting would end your usefulness to the family.

Case VIII · The colleague The head of housekeeping, who has been in the house for eleven years, is openly cold to you. She contradicts you in front of junior staff and has twice “forgotten” to pass on a message. The principal asks you, pleasantly, how you are finding the team.

**· · ·.** What do you say to the principal? What do you do about the housekeeper, and on what timescale? What would the natural, satisfying answer be, and why would it destroy you?

Case IX · The improper instruction A member of the family, not the principal, asks you to say, if anyone should ask, that they were at the London house on Thursday evening. It is presented lightly, almost as a joke, and it is a very small thing.

**· · ·.** What do you say, in the moment? Do you escalate, and to whom? Why is the smallness of the request the most dangerous feature of it?

Case X · The withdrawal Over six weeks, three things have happened: a task you owned has quietly gone to someone else; the principal has begun copying the office on messages that used to come to you alone; and the tone towards you has become notably more polite.

**· · ·.** What is happening? What are the two responses that will accelerate it? What exactly do you do, and what single question do you ask?

## The debriefs

Reveal a debrief only once you have answered that case. Each debrief names what the case was testing, gives a defensible answer, and states the general rule.

I · The restaurant What it tests. Whether you push your own work back onto the principal.

The diary says Rome, Thursday. The preference file says which restaurant, which table, and who dined there in March. The restaurant says it is full. The maître d’, whom you have cultivated for two years precisely for an evening like this, says it is not. You hold two times.

You reply once: “Yes. Thursday, 8pm or 9.30pm, whichever suits. I have kept both.”

Notice that nothing here was improvised. A diary that was read, a file that was kept, a relationship built long before the night it was needed. Anticipation is not a gift. It is infrastructure.

**THE RULE**

The number of questions in your reply is your professional signature. One, or none.

II · The contradiction What it tests. Whether you understand that signing, deciding and trusting are three different people.

You do not choose a side. You do not quietly proceed. You do not tell the principal that the office has refused, that is not your message to carry, and carrying it makes you the face of a refusal that is not yours.

To the office, in writing, the same day: “The principal has asked me for this. I want to make sure it is handled correctly, how would you like me to proceed?”

You have returned the decision to the person whose decision it is, and made yourself the person who handles things correctly.

**THE RULE**

The staff member who resolves a contradiction alone, by guessing, is blamed by both sides. Always.

III · The journalist What it tests. Whether you can be flattered into a confirmation. Note the technique: they claim to know already, so that agreeing feels like nothing.

“I’m not able to help with that.” Then end the call, courteously, and add no further sentence.

Do not confirm. Do not deny, a denial is information. Do not say “I’ll have to check”, which confirms that you could, and therefore that you are someone worth calling again.

Then tell the house immediately: the number, the time, the exact question asked. Same day, in writing, without drama.

**THE RULE**

There is no off the record. There never was.

IV · The empty house What it tests. Whether familiarity dissolves your judgement. It usually does, which is exactly why a real breach almost always wears the face of a friend.

It may be entirely innocent, and it does not matter. Movements and occupancy are security information, and they are never yours to give, not to a friend, not to a cousin, not to a supplier who needs to schedule works.

“I honestly don’t keep track of the family’s plans, the office would know.” Warm, unremarkable, closed.

Then you mention it. Not as an accusation, as a fact. Being the person who mentions it is being the person who is trusted.

**THE RULE**

Security information does not become safe because the room is friendly.

V · The mistake What it tests. Not the error. Everyone makes the error. What is tested is the ninety minutes that follow it.

First the remedy: what are the real options at 23:00? A later slot, a different aircraft, a repositioning, a commercial alternative, a revised schedule at the far end. You arrive with the problem and two solutions and a recommendation, or you are not yet ready to speak.

Then, the same night: “I made an error with the date. Here is where we are, here are the two ways forward, and I recommend the first.”

The construction that must never appear is the passive voice. “The booking was made incorrectly” is a lie wearing grammar, and they will hear it as one.

**THE RULE**

Houses forgive errors. They do not forgive being managed.

VI · The confidence What it tests. Whether you will mistake a moment of human weakness for an invitation to intimacy.

The next morning you are exactly as you were before: warm, professional, entirely unchanged. You do not soften your voice. You do not offer the sympathetic look. You do not refer to it, then or ever, unless they do.

What you know changes nothing about what you say, and everything about what you anticipate. You protect the calendar. You are gentler with the schedule. You never explain why.

The trap closes slowly, over months, and it closes on you.

**THE RULE**

Being trusted feels like being chosen. It is not. The confidante becomes a witness, and the witness is moved on.

VII · The commission What it tests. Whether you understand corruption structurally rather than morally.

To him, without heat: “I don’t take anything from suppliers, but if there is a trade discount, the household will be glad of it.” He will not be offended. He has asked before, and been refused before.

Then disclose it to the office the same day, factually, without accusation.

The reason is not that you are pious. It is that the moment you accept, you work for him and not for the family. Every recommendation you make thereafter is compromised, and you will know it, and eventually so will they.

**THE RULE**

Refuse, disclose, and route every trade advantage back to the household.

VIII · The colleague What it tests. Whether you will buy short-term relief at the price of your position.

To the principal: “The team have been very generous with me. We are finding our rhythm.” Nothing else. It is a test whether or not the principal knows they are administering one, and the natural, satisfying answer, the truthful complaint, destroys you.

To the housekeeper: ask her advice. Publicly, in front of others, on something that genuinely falls within her domain. Take it, and credit her. Repeat this weekly. She does not want your job; she wants to be respected in her own, and she has been here eleven years while you have been here three weeks.

Give it ninety days. If she is still obstructing at ninety days and it materially affects the house, you raise it once, factually, with your line manager, never with the principal.

**THE RULE**

In a staff conflict there is no winning side. There is only the side that leaves, and it is usually the newest.

IX · The improper instruction What it tests. Whether you can refuse a small thing charmingly, before it becomes a large thing you cannot refuse.

“I’d rather not, I’m sure you understand.” Warm, immediate, and without argument, moralising or explanation. Do not laugh along, and do not say “this time”.

Yes, you escalate, to the office, factually, the same day. It will feel disproportionate. It is not.

The smallness is the danger. A large improper request is easy to refuse, because it announces itself. A small one establishes a precedent, and the second request will refer to the first: “you did it before.”

**THE RULE**

A line drawn in calm holds. A line you must find in the moment does not.

X · The withdrawal What it tests. Whether you can read the household, and whether you can act without either panic or grovelling.

What is happening is withdrawal, and it precedes almost every dismissal in this profession. Politeness up, warmth down, access reduced. It is legible, and at this stage it is still recoverable.

The two responses that accelerate it: anxiety (asking repeatedly whether everything is alright, apologising for nothing) and obsequiousness (working visibly harder, hovering, over-communicating). Both confirm the suspicion that something is wrong with you.

What you do: ask for ten minutes, and ask one question, “Is there anything you would like me to be doing differently?” Then listen without defending yourself, thank them, and change the thing.

**THE RULE**

The most common reason a household lets someone go is that they never asked.

## Marking scheme

*How your cases will be read, and what to bring*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the standard against which your judgement is measured. Prepare the two cases you most want to work through in your mentorship session.

**Band.** What it looks like

**Distinction.** A method is visible. The candidate names which of the three levers is at stake, identifies who signs and who decides, and produces an action with at most one easy question remaining. Where they refuse something, they refuse without argument.

**Pass.** The right instinct, reached without a visible method. The answers are defensible but would not survive an unfamiliar situation. This is where most of you will be, and it is an honourable place to begin.

**Rework.** The answer pushes work back onto the principal, takes a side, improvises a refusal, or resolves a contradiction by guessing. Any one of these is a rework, however elegant the prose.

**What to bring to your session**

Not the two cases you found easiest. The two that made you uncomfortable. The discomfort is the lesson: it is the precise location of the gap between the person you are and the person this profession requires. We will spend the session there, and nowhere else.

And notice, before you close this document, the single feature common to all ten cases: in not one of them was the difficulty technical. Not one required a skill you do not already possess. That is the point of this week, and it is the point of the profession.

## What you are producing, and why

*The ancestor of the preference file*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

State the purpose of a client empathy map in private service. Understand its relationship to the preference file you will keep for decades. Apply the confidentiality rule that governs the exercise itself.

**1.1 · The document**

A single page that answers, for one principal, the questions no job description ever asks: what they are truly buying, what they fear, what irritates them, what they will never say but always expect, how you will know they are displeased before they say so, and what you would do in your first month without being asked.

**1.2 · Why it matters more than it looks**

This document is the ancestor of the preference file, the structured record that, in Week Four, you will learn to keep for a lifetime, and which is the central instrument of anticipation in this profession. Everything you will later record about a principal (their table, their allergies, their aversions, the flower she cannot bear, the dates that are difficult) begins as a discipline of attention, and this page is where that discipline is first trained.

**DEFINITION**

Client empathy map. A one-page synthesis of a principal’s needs, fears, irritations, unspoken expectations and warning signals, written to produce action rather than understanding.

The distinction in that definition is the whole exercise. This is not a psychological portrait, and you are not being asked to be perceptive for its own sake. Every line must imply something you would do differently tomorrow morning. If a line implies nothing, it is an observation, and observations do not belong in this document.

**1.3 · Whom you may write about**

· A real principal you have served, in which case you will change every identifying detail: name, country, industry, the number and nature of the residences, the ages of the children. Everything. · A public figure you have studied seriously, drawing only on what is genuinely public.

· The composite family used in this programme (see Chapter IV).

THIS INSTRUCTION IS PART OF THE EXERCISE

A map that names a real principal, or that could identify one, will be returned unread. That is not a punishment. It is the profession. Discretion is not a chapter of this programme; it is the condition of it, and it begins with the first document you hand to me.

## The specification

*Seven sections, and what belongs in each*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Produce each of the seven sections to the required standard. Rank the three levers for a specific principal, and defend the order.

**Section.** What belongs in it

**1 · The situation.** Tier of wealth. Number of residences. Number of staff. Structure above the house, is there a family office, a chief of staff? Children and their ages. The shape of their year. Facts only. No interpretation, no adjectives. Six to eight lines.

**2 · What they buy.** Rank time, peace and discretion for this principal, and justify the order in one sentence each. They are never equal. A candidate who says they are has not looked.

**3 · What they fear.** Exposure? Waste? Being managed? Their children’s safety? Losing control of their own diary? Being made to feel foolish in front of people they respect? Name it precisely. Fear drives more behaviour in this world than desire does.

**4 · What irritates them.** The small things. The question asked twice. The unnecessary choice. The person who explains at length. The flourish. The email that could have been three words. Be specific: “bad service” is not an answer, it is a refusal to observe.

**5 · What they will never say, but always expect.** The heart of the document, and the section that separates the professionals from everyone else. At least five entries. At least two should be uncomfortable to write.

**6 · The tells.** How will you know they are displeased before they tell you? Silence? Brevity? Excessive politeness? Copying the office? In this world, going quiet is almost always the signal, and warmth withdrawing while manners increase is the loudest one there is.

**7 · Your first three anticipations.** Three specific actions you would take in the first month, unasked, each serving time, peace or discretion. Name which lever each one serves. They must be executable by someone else, on Monday morning, using only your page.

## Standards

*Weak, adequate, professional, the difference is specificity*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Distinguish a platitude from an observation. Write section 5 to a professional standard.

Section 5 is where beginners write platitudes and professionals write the truth. Compare, and be honest about which of these you were about to write.

WEAK · WHAT A BEGINNER WRITES

“They expect excellent service.” “They expect discretion and professionalism.” “They expect me to be available and proactive.” Every one is true, useless, and could be written about any wealthy person who has ever lived. It tells me you have not observed anyone, you have imagined them.

PROFESSIONAL · WHAT A PRACTITIONER WRITES

“He expects the car to be moving within ninety seconds of him standing up, and he will never say so.” “She expects to be told about a problem only once it has a solution attached. She will forgive the delay. She will not forgive the naked problem.” “They expect me to know which of their friends may be given the address, and never to ask them to clarify it twice.” “He expects the chef to have been told about the dinner guests before he is asked to remember to tell me.” “She expects that when she is short with me on a Monday, I will behave on Tuesday exactly as I did on Sunday.”

Notice what the professional lines share. Each is falsifiable, it could be shown to be wrong. Each is observable, it was learned by watching, not by imagining. And each implies an action you could take tomorrow. That is the standard, and there is no other.

## A complete worked example

*The Marchand family, a composite, and entirely fictional*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read a finished map at the required standard. Use it as the shape of your own, never as its content.

Use this family if you have no principal of your own to write about. Do not copy its content, a copied map is visible in four seconds, and it will be returned.

**1 · The situation**

Tier: upper UHNW, approx. $400M. Source: second-generation industrial, professionalised by the son. Four residences: Geneva (principal), a chalet in Verbier, an apartment in Milan, a summer house in Sardinia. Fourteen staff across the four. A single-family office in Geneva with a chief of staff, to whom I report. Two principals, he, 54; she, 49, and three children (17, 14, 9). The eldest is at school in England. The year: Verbier from Christmas to March; Geneva in term time; Sardinia from mid-July to late August; Milan for a week each month.

**2 · What they buy, ranked**

1. Peace. He has an operating business and a board. What he purchases at home is the absence of one more thing requiring his attention. Every unnecessary message to him is a small theft. 2. Discretion. A public family name in a small city. The children are recognisable at school. She fears photographs more than she fears anything else in this document. 3. Time. Real, but third. They are not short of hours; they are short of quiet.

**3 · What they fear**

That something about the children reaches the press or the school gates. That the household becomes a subject of gossip in Geneva, where everyone knows everyone. That they are being handled, softened, or managed by their own staff. That an expensive mistake will be discovered by the office before it is disclosed by us.

**4 · What irritates them**

Being asked a question whose answer exists in a document I already hold. A message longer than four lines when three words would do. The flourish: the card with the flowers, the announced upgrade, the “surprise”. Any suggestion, however implied, that something was difficult to arrange. Staff who address him before he has addressed them, in front of guests.

**5 · What they will never say, but always expect**

“I expect the chalet to be at 21°C, the fire lit and the kitchen provisioned, three hours before we land, and I expect never to have discussed it with you.” “I expect you to know which of our friends may be given the Sardinia address, and never to ask me twice.” “I expect a problem to arrive with a solution already attached to it.” “I expect you to have told the chef about the guests before I remember to tell you.” “I expect that if I am short with you on Monday, you will be exactly as you were on Sunday.” “I expect that you will never, under any circumstances, photograph my children.”

**6 · The tells**

She goes quiet, and becomes markedly more polite. This is the signal, and it is unmistakable once you have seen it once. He begins to copy the chief of staff on messages that used to come to me alone. This means access is being reduced, and it is the beginning of the end unless it is addressed within a fortnight. A request that used to come to me goes to the housekeeper instead. Same signal, quieter.

**7 · My first three anticipations**

Peace. Build the arrival protocol for the chalet, temperature, provisions, fire, cars, staff briefed, so that the first arrival of the season requires not one message from either principal. Test it on the empty house first. Discretion. Establish, in writing, with the office and not with the family, the standing rule on who may be given each address, so that I never have to ask them the same question twice. Time. Produce a one-page weekly summary of household matters, sent Friday, four lines, the same format every week, so that nothing needs to be raised with them ad hoc for the rest of the week.

## The marking rubric

*How I will read it, and what I am looking for*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Anticipate the five criteria against which your work is judged. Understand which single failure results in the document being returned unread.

**Criterion.** What earns the mark

**Specificity.** Concrete, observable behaviours. Not adjectives, not aspirations. Any line that could apply to any wealthy person will be struck through.

**Ranking.** Time, peace and discretion are ranked for this principal, with a defensible reason for each position. They are never equal.

**The unspoken.** Section 5 contains at least five entries, and at least two are uncomfortable. The comfortable entries are, in my experience, usually the invented ones.

**Action.** The three anticipations could be executed on Monday morning by another person using only your page. If they could not, they are wishes.

**Discretion.** No real name, no identifying detail, anywhere. A breach here means the document is returned unread, regardless of the quality of the rest.

**Band.** Standard

**Distinction.** Every line implies an action. The ranking is argued, not asserted. Section 5 contains at least one line that made you hesitate before writing it.

**Pass.** The structure is respected and the observations are real, but several lines describe rather than imply. Honest work, and the ordinary starting point.

**Rework.** Platitudes in section 5; an unranked section 2; anticipations that are attitudes rather than actions; or any identifying detail. Returned, with the reason, and resubmitted before Week Two opens.

## The American annex

*The same profession, on your side of the Atlantic*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know where this work actually sits in the United States, what an American package contains beyond the salary, and the one legal fact that governs every household job you will take here.

**A1.1 · Where the households are**

The work concentrates, and it concentrates hard. New York and the Hamptons. Palm Beach, Miami and Naples. Greenwich and the Connecticut shoreline. Los Angeles, Beverly Hills, Malibu and Montecito. The Bay Area. Aspen and Jackson Hole. Dallas, Houston and Austin. Nashville, and increasingly Scottsdale and Park City.

Two things follow. Mobility is priced here more than almost anywhere, because a household in Palm Beach and a household in the Hamptons are frequently the same household six months apart, and the person who moves with the season is worth more than the person who does not. And a national search is not a strategy. Choose two markets and be known in them.

**A1.2 · Read the American package**

A stated salary tells you less here than anywhere, because health insurance in the United States is not a benefit, it is a number.

**Health cover.** Who pays the premium, what the plan actually covers, and whether it extends to your family. The gap between a household that covers you fully and one that hands you a stipend can be twenty thousand dollars a year, and it never appears in the headline figure.

**Retirement.** Whether there is a 401(k), whether the household matches, and at what percentage. Many private households offer nothing. A household that has set one up is telling you it thinks in years.

**Paid time off.** There is no statutory minimum in the United States. What is written is what you have, and what is not written does not exist.

**A1.3 · The fact that governs everything**

**THE RULE**

You are a household employee. You are not an independent contractor, and you should not agree to be treated as one.

A family that pays you on a 1099 is shifting its half of your payroll taxes onto you and leaving you outside every protection that attaches to employment. The IRS is not ambiguous here: a worker in a private home whose work is directed by the family is an employee. The household owes Social Security and Medicare tax on cash wages above a threshold that stands at three thousand dollars for 2026, reports it on Schedule H with its own return, and issues you a W-2. It is set out in IRS Publication 926, the Household Employer's Tax Guide, which is free and short and worth an hour of your evening.

What this sounds like in a room. If an offer includes the phrase "we will just 1099 you", you do not argue in the interview. You say, level and unbothered: "I am set up as a W-2 household employee, which I think is cleanest for you as well." Most families are not attempting anything. They have never employed anyone before, and they will be relieved that you knew.

This is orientation and not legal advice, and your state adds rules of its own. One conversation with a household payroll service or an employment attorney in your state, before you sign your first contract, is the cheapest professional expense of your career.

## Common failures, and submission

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Avoid the five errors that produce a rework. Submit correctly and on time.

· Writing the map you think I want to read. I will know, and it wastes the only session you have this week. · Confusing perceptiveness with usefulness. A beautiful psychological portrait that implies no action is a failed document, however impressive it is. · Adjectives in section 1. The situation is facts. Your interpretation has six other sections in which to live. · Anticipations that are attitudes. “Be attentive to her moods” is not an anticipation. “Send the Friday summary, four lines, same format, so nothing needs raising ad hoc” is. · Leaving in a detail you thought was harmless. The industry, the city, the school, the number of aircraft. Any two of these identify a family. Change everything.

**Submission**

One page. PDF. Submitted before your mentorship session, not during it, I read it beforehand, and the session is spent on the correction rather than on the reading.

And bring me the part you are least sure about. Not the part you are proudest of. That is where the session does its real work.

## What a code is

*And why it cannot be faked for long*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Define a code, and distinguish it from a rule. Understand why codes exist, and what they are actually for. Accept the two-year rule of cultural fluency.

**1.1 · A definition**

**DEFINITION**

A code. A shared, unwritten convention by which a group recognises its own. Codes are never explained to newcomers, because explaining them would destroy their function.

That last clause is the whole of it. A rule can be published, learned and obeyed. A code cannot, because its purpose is precisely to distinguish those who had to learn it from those who did not. This is not cruelty; it is how every closed group on earth protects itself, from a village to a boardroom to a family.

**1.2 · What codes are actually for**

You will be tempted to find them absurd. Why should it matter whether one says settee or sofa, whether the napkin goes left or right, whether one calls a wine "nice"? It matters for a reason that has nothing to do with the words: a code is a cheap and instant test of belonging. A family cannot investigate everyone who enters their life. They rely on a thousand small signals, processed in seconds, mostly unconsciously.

And here is the consequence that governs your entire career: you will not be corrected. A guest will not tell you that you used the wrong word. A principal will not explain that you were too loud. They will simply feel, faintly, that you are not quite right, and they will begin, without deciding to, to hand you less. By the time you notice, it will be over.

**1.3 · The honest timeline**

**HOW LONG THIS TAKES**

You will learn the vocabulary in a week. You will learn to use it in a year. The vocabulary is the easy half. The hard half is the restraint: knowing which of the things you now know should never be said at all. The single fastest way to be identified as an outsider is to demonstrate that you have recently learned the codes. Study everything. Display nothing.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

A code is an unwritten test of belonging, and it is never explained to you. You will not be corrected. You will simply be trusted less, and you will not know why. Learn everything. Display nothing. The display is itself the error.

## The language

*The words that betray you, and the ones that never appear*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

List the words that are never used by people who live inside this world. Replace description with precision. Understand the mathematics of the word "luxury".

**2.1 · The rule, and it is almost mathematical**

The more a person says the word "luxury", the further they are from it. The people who live inside this world do not describe it, because to describe it would be to stand outside it and point. They name things precisely, and then they stop talking.

It is a test you can apply to anyone, including yourself, within thirty seconds of meeting them.

**Never said.** Said instead

**"A luxury car.".** The marque. "The Bentley." "The old Land Rover." Nothing more.

**"A fine wine.".** The estate, and the year. Or nothing at all, and a small nod.

**"An exclusive club.".** Its name, said quietly, once, and only if it is relevant.

**"A VIP guest.".** A guest. There are no other kinds.

**"An iconic piece.".** Iconic is used perhaps twice a decade. Its power is entirely in its scarcity.

**"High-end", "upscale", "prestigious", "premium".** Nothing. These are the words of the people who sell this world to the people who are not in it.

**"Amazing", "incredible", "stunning".** "Lovely." "Very good." "That was a pleasure." Understatement is not modesty here; it is fluency.

**2.2 · The vocabulary of the professional**

Certain words mark you immediately as someone who knows the house, and they cost nothing to adopt today.

**Word.** Why it matters

**Principal.** The person you serve. Never "boss", never "client", never "employer". This single word is the most efficient signal available to you.

**The office.** The family office. "I will confirm with the office" is one of the most useful sentences you will ever own.

**Certainly.** The correct answer to almost anything. Not "no problem", not "sure", not "of course" (which can sound like impatience).

**The house.** The household as an organisation, not the building.

**A gentleman / a guest.** Never "a client". You are not in hospitality any more.

**I will see to it.** The sentence that ends a conversation cleanly, and promises exactly what it says.

**2.3 · The great error: bluffing a reference**

You will one day be in a room where a name is mentioned, and you will not know it. A house, a vineyard, a designer, a school, a village in Switzerland. Every instinct will tell you to nod as though you knew.

Do not. This world is small, and the bluff is detected almost immediately, and it is remembered for years. The professional says, without embarrassment: "I don't know it. Tell me." That sentence costs you nothing, and it frequently earns you something, because people enjoy explaining what they love.

The rule beneath the rule: a confident error is far more damaging than an honest gap. Ignorance is temporary. A reputation for bluffing is not.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The word "luxury" is a distance marker. The people inside never use it. Replace description with precision: the marque, the estate, the year, or nothing. Say "principal", say "certainly", say "I will see to it". Never bluff a reference. Say "I don't know it. Tell me."

## The register

*How one speaks inside a private house*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Adopt the correct forms of address, and hold them under pressure. Master the four sentences that will carry ninety per cent of your conversations. Understand why brevity is the deepest code of all.

**3.1 · Forms of address**

· Follow the house, exactly. Some households are first names from the first day. Others are Madame and Monsieur for a decade. Neither is more correct; what is incorrect is guessing. · If first names are offered, accept once, and never in front of guests. In public you revert. This protects the principal, and it protects you. · Never use a diminutive. Not for the principal, not for the children, not for a colleague in front of the family. · Address the guest as the family addresses them. Listen for thirty seconds before you speak, and you will never be wrong.

**3.2 · The four sentences**

Learn these by heart. They will carry you through most of a working life, and each one is a code in itself.

**THE FOUR SENTENCES**

"Certainly." The answer to a request. It closes without flourish, and it commits. "I will see to it." When the matter is now yours, and they need never think of it again. "Leave it with me." Warmer, for the small things, and it removes the burden visibly. "I am not able to help with that, but let me pass it to the office." The refusal. Calm, complete, and without argument. Learn it in the exact words.

**3.3 · Brevity, and why it is the deepest code**

The length of your sentences is a status signal, and almost nobody realises it. The person who explains at length is asking for something: understanding, credit, forgiveness, admiration. The person who says four words and stops is asking for nothing.

So: never explain how hard it was. Never narrate the obstacles. Never say "I finally managed to". The correct account of a difficult afternoon is: "It is arranged." If they wish to know more, they will ask, and they almost never will.

And write as you speak. An email of four lines from a member of staff reads as competence. An email of twenty lines reads as anxiety, and anxiety is contagious, which is why they will stop reading you.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Follow the house on names. Revert to the formal register in public, always. Certainly. I will see to it. Leave it with me. I am not able to help with that. Brevity is the code beneath all the others. Never explain how hard it was.

## The body

*Presence, dress, and the art of being unmemorable*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Dress correctly for a private household, which is not the same as dressing well. Manage your physical presence in a room. Eliminate the six physical tells that identify an outsider instantly.

**4.1 · The governing rule of dress**

Always one level below the family. Never above. This is not humility; it is function. Your clothing exists to make you legible and forgettable, so that attention flows past you to where it belongs.

**THE TEST**

If a guest could describe what you were wearing an hour later, you were wearing the wrong thing. Simple. Impeccable. Unmemorable. Excellent fabric, no visible brand, nothing that moves, nothing that makes a sound.

· No visible logo. Anywhere, on anything you own, including your bag and your telephone case. A logo announces that the object was expensive, which is the one thing this world never announces. · Nothing that sounds. Bracelets that chime, heels that announce you on a stone floor, keys that jingle. You must be able to enter a room without being heard. · Perfume: almost none, and never in the morning. A scent that arrives before you is a form of talking. · Nails, hair, hands: immaculate and undramatic. Your hands will be six inches from a principal's plate and their children's faces. They will be looked at more than your face. · Shoes clean, always, including the heel. Old money looks at shoes. It is an ancient reflex and it has not weakened.

**4.2 · Presence**

Presence is not charisma. In this world it is the opposite: it is the ability to be entirely available and entirely unobtrusive at the same time.

· Enter quietly, and pause at the threshold. Never cross a room while someone is speaking. · Stand still. Fidgeting, shifting, checking, adjusting: all of it reads as anxiety, and anxiety in staff makes a family anxious.

· Do not fill silences. The person who is comfortable in a silence is the person who is trusted in it. · Never sit unless invited, and never eat in front of the family unless invited twice. · Leave before you are noticed leaving. The exit is part of the service.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

One level below the family, always. If they can describe your clothes, they were wrong. No logo, no sound, no scent, no drama. Immaculate hands, clean shoes. Enter quietly, stand still, say less, leave before you are noticed leaving.

## The table

*The one arena in which you will be tested in public*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Lay and read a formal table without hesitation. Know the service conventions, and the errors that are visible to everyone. Speak about wine and food precisely, or not at all.

**5.1 · Why the table matters more than anything else**

Because it is the only place where your competence is on display to the family, to their guests, and to the other staff, simultaneously, in real time. An error at the table is not private. It is theatre.

**5.2 · The essentials, and they are not negotiable**

**Convention.** The rule

**Cutlery.** Outside in, working towards the plate as the meal progresses. Dessert cutlery above the plate. The guest never has to wonder.

**Glasses.** Above the knives, to the right. Water, then white, then red, ordered by the shape of the glass. Champagne where the house prefers it.

**Service.** From the left when serving, from the right when clearing. Wine poured from the right. Never reach across a guest, ever.

**Silver service.** Food transferred from platter to plate with a spoon and fork held in one hand. It is a genuine skill; where it is expected, clumsiness is instantly visible, and where it is not, using it looks like a performance.

**Napkins.** Placed, not folded into shapes. A folded swan is a code, and the code it sends is "hotel".

**Clearing.** Never clear a plate until everyone has finished. Never stack. Never scrape at the table.

**The pause.** Cutlery crossed, or angled into an inverted V, means the guest is pausing and is not finished. Knife and fork laid parallel together across the plate, handles to the lower right, means they are. Learn to read this from across a room, and never clear a crossed pair.

**5.3 · The drill, and it is the only one this week you must do standing up**

The chapter above says the table is a public examination. A thing that is examined has to be practised, so practise it, tonight, on your own kitchen table, with whatever you own.

**Lay it.** A four-course cover, from memory, in under three minutes. Soup, fish, main, dessert. Cutlery outside in, dessert above the plate, glasses above the knives to the right in the order they will be used. Then photograph it, close this page, and lay it again tomorrow without looking. The second attempt is the one that tells you whether you know it.

**Read it.** Set a plate with the cutlery crossed. Walk to the far side of the room, turn, and look at it. Then set it parallel to the lower right and do the same. You are training yourself to read a plate at eight metres in a low light, at the exact moment your attention is on nine other things, and that is the only condition in which you will ever actually need it.

**Say it.** Out loud, three times: "From the left to serve, from the right to clear. Wine from the right. Never across a guest." It sounds absurd on a Tuesday evening in your own kitchen. It will not sound absurd the first time you are in a dining room with eleven people in it.

**The self-test, and be honest.** Could you lay a formal cover, right now, without looking anything up? Could you tell from the doorway whether the woman at the far end has finished? If the answer to either is no, this is the chapter to return to before your session, and it is worth more of your week than the reading.

**5.4 · Speaking about wine**

The safest posture in the world of wine is curiosity, never authority. A sommelier respects the person who asks a good question far more than the person who performs knowledge.

· Never say a wine is "nice", and never say it is "amazing". Say what it does: it is fresh, it is closed, it needs air, it is drinking beautifully now. · Offer to decant a mature red without being asked. It is a gesture of care, and it improves the thing they are about to enjoy. · Know that serious Champagne is increasingly served in a white wine glass rather than a flute, to release the aroma. Knowing this quietly is a mark of real fluency. · Suggest a magnum for a large table and a half bottle for a solitary diner. Both show an eye for the occasion rather than for the price.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The table is the only public examination of your competence. Prepare for it accordingly. Outside in. Left to serve, right to clear. Never reach across a guest. On wine: curiosity, never authority. Describe what it does, not how much you like it.

## The material codes

*What objects say, and what they never say*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read a room, and understand what its objects are telling you. Distinguish inherited taste from purchased taste, without ever remarking on either.

**Code.** What it signals

**Patina.** The soft wear of age on leather, silver, wood, a watch. Old money treasures it as proof of endurance. Never polish it away, never remark on it, and never apologise for it.

**The monogram.** Discreet, tone on tone, small, on linen or luggage. Large and contrasting is a different signal entirely, and not the one you want.

**Flowers.** Loose, seasonal, from a garden if possible. A tight commercial arrangement in cellophane reads as "ordered", and ordered is the opposite of arranged.

**Fresh vs new.** Old money buys once and repairs. New money replaces. Neither is your business, and both change how you manage a house, a budget and a supplier.

**Silence.** A house with soft closing doors, felt under objects, and no ringing telephones is a house that has spent money on quiet. Quiet is the most expensive commodity in this world, and you are, in large part, employed to produce it.

**The absence of things.** An empty console with a single object on it costs more than a full one. Restraint is the code that contains all the other codes.

And a warning that costs people their positions: never comment on the value of anything. Not the art, not the watch, not the car, not the house. Not to admire it, not to praise it, not even to compliment the taste behind it. Noticing the price of things is the single clearest signal that one is not accustomed to them.

## The dress codes, decoded

*What an invitation is actually saying*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read any invitation correctly, at a glance. Prepare a principal without ever appearing to correct them.

**On the invitation.** What it means

**White tie.** The most formal. Black tailcoat, white waistcoat, white bow tie; for women, a full-length gown. Rare, exacting, and unforgiving of error.

**Black tie.** Dinner jacket, black bow tie; gown or elegant cocktail dress. A business suit here is a quiet failure, and it will be seen by everyone.

**Black tie optional.** It is not optional. Wear black tie.

**Cocktail.** Short dress, dark suit. The most misread of all the codes, and the one where people most often overdress.

**Smart casual.** A jacket, no tie. On the Riviera in August, a linen jacket and no socks. It is the hardest code, because it has no rules and everyone is watching.

**Resort / beach formal.** Linen, colour, no tie, no jacket after sunset. Extremely easy to get wrong by trying.

**YOUR ROLE IN THIS**

You will one day see a principal about to leave for an evening dressed incorrectly for it. You do not correct them. You say: "The invitation says black tie, if that is useful." Then you say nothing further, and you look at something else. You have given them the fact. What they do with it is theirs. That sentence, delivered without a flicker of judgement, is worth more than any qualification you will ever hold.

## The cultural map

*The codes are not the same everywhere, and the difference matters*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Adjust the codes to the culture of the house you serve. Avoid the classic error of applying English or French codes universally.

**World.** What changes

**Old European money.** Understatement, patina, tradition, and an acute sensitivity to language. They will forgive a lapse in service far more readily than a lapse in manners.

**American UHNW.** Warmer, more direct, more informal in address, and considerably more demanding about speed and responsiveness. Formality can read as coldness. Adjust.

**Gulf and royal households.** High formality, strict hierarchy, and cultural and religious observance that you are expected to know without being told. Learn the calendar, the greetings and the boundaries before your first day, never during it.

**Asian family offices.** Age, seniority and face govern everything. Never correct anyone senior in front of anyone. The indirect refusal is a genuine refusal, and hearing it correctly is a skill.

**Tech wealth.** Informality bordering on the anti-ceremonial, combined with severe privacy and data discipline. Do not confuse the casual dress with a casual standard: it is often the most exacting house of all.

The error to avoid is the one nearly everyone makes: assuming that the codes you have just learned are universal. They are not. What is universal is the attentiveness that lets you read a house within a week, and adjust without ever announcing that you have.

## The tells

*How an outsider is identified, in four seconds*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise the tells in others, and eliminate them in yourself. Understand that none of them will ever be mentioned to you.

Here is the list. Read it slowly, and be honest: at least two of these are yours.

· The word "luxury", or any of its family. The fastest tell there is. · A visible logo. On a bag, a belt, a scarf, a phone case. · Perfume that enters the room first. · A telephone placed on a table. Face down changes nothing. · Laughter louder than the room. · Photographing anything. The food, the view, the table you laid so beautifully. · Commenting on the price or the value of an object. Including admiringly. Especially admiringly. · An anecdote about a previous employer. This one does not merely mark you as an outsider. It ends the conversation, and often the candidacy. · Bluffing a name. A house, a vintage, a designer, a school. · Superlatives. Amazing, incredible, stunning, iconic. Restraint is the code; enthusiasm is the tell.

Notice what unites every item on that list: each one is a small act of self-display. The scent, the logo, the volume, the photograph, the anecdote, the bluff. In this world, the display is always the error, and it is always the thing that is noticed.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Every tell is a form of self-display, and every one of them is noticed in silence. You will not be corrected. You will be trusted less. Find your own three this week. Everyone has three.

## Glossary of the module

Code, An unwritten convention by which a group recognises its own, never explained to newcomers. Understatement, The governing aesthetic of this world. Precision instead of praise, and silence instead of superlatives. Patina, The wear that age gives to fine materials. Treasured as proof of endurance, never polished away. Silver service, Transferring food from platter to plate with spoon and fork in one hand. A trained skill. Black tie / white tie, Evening dress codes. White tie is the more formal, and the more unforgiving. The tell, A small, unconscious act of self-display that identifies an outsider instantly. The bluff, Pretending to recognise a name. The fastest way to lose credibility permanently. Restraint, The code that contains all the others: study everything, display nothing.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing, from memory. Bring the two you found hardest to your session.

1. Why can a code never be explained to a newcomer? Answer in terms of its function.

2. Give the mathematical rule about the word "luxury", and explain what it actually measures.

3. Rewrite these as a professional would: "It was an amazing evening at a really exclusive place." "I finally managed to get you an incredible table." "That is a stunning luxury car." 4. Write the four sentences from Chapter III, from memory, and say when each is used.

5. A name is mentioned that you do not recognise. Give your exact words.

6. Why is brevity a status signal? What does a long explanation ask for?

7. What is the governing rule of dress, and what is the test that verifies it?

8. The invitation says black tie. The principal is dressed in a business suit and about to leave. What do you say, word for word, and what do you do next? 9. Name six of the ten tells, and identify the single thing they all have in common.

10. Which three of the tells are yours? Be honest. This is the only question in this module that matters.

**Further study**

· The Dictionary of Luxury, which accompanies this programme. Read every entry this week. Module 4 will assume it. · Listen, in any room you are ever in, for the word "luxury". Count it. You will learn more from that exercise in a month than from any book. · Study one great house, one great maison and one great estate properly: its history, its makers, its vocabulary. Depth in one subject reads as culture. Breadth in none reads as a search engine. · Watch how the most senior person in any room holds themselves, and notice how little they do. Then do less than that.

## The words you will never hear

*And what saying them announces about you*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Eliminate the vocabulary of the outsider from your speech, permanently. Understand what each forbidden word actually reveals.

**1.1 · The mathematics of the word**

The more a person says "luxury", the further they are from it. You will hear that sentence again in your session, because it is the most portable thing I can give you. It works on brands, on people, on hotels, on other candidates, and on yourself.

The reason is structural. To describe a world, you must be standing outside it and pointing at it. The people inside do not point. They name, precisely, and then they stop.

**The word.** What saying it announces

**Luxury.** That you are looking at this world from a shop window.

**Exclusive.** That you have been excluded, and are still thinking about it.

**VIP.** That you work somewhere that sorts people into tiers, and tells them which tier they are in.

**Iconic.** That you read the caption rather than the object.

**Prestigious, high-end, upscale, premium.** That you have read the brochure. These four words appear on no invitation, in no household, and in no serious conversation.

**Amazing, incredible, stunning.** That you are surprised. Being surprised by nice things is the clearest possible signal that you are not accustomed to them.

**"To be honest with you".** That the rest of the time you are not.

**"No problem".** That there might have been one.

THE REPLACEMENT, AND IT IS ONE WORD

Certainly. It closes, it commits, it asks for nothing, and it contains no emotion whatsoever. It is the most useful word in this profession, and you can begin using it today.

## The grammar of understatement

*How to say that something was extraordinary*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Speak in the register of the house: precise, brief, unemotional. Convert enthusiasm into observation.

You will need, one day, to say that something was very good. Here is how it is done, and how it is not.

**The outsider says.** The professional says

**"It was an amazing evening.".** "It went well." Or, if it truly did: "It was a very good evening."

**"The chef is incredible.".** "The chef is excellent. You would like him."

**"That wine was stunning.".** "That was drinking beautifully." Or nothing, and a small nod.

**"I finally managed to get the table.".** "It is arranged."

**"It was really difficult, but I sorted it.".** "It is done."

**"You look absolutely stunning.".** Nothing. You do not comment on the principal's appearance. Ever.

Notice the direction of travel in every line: from emotion to fact, and from length to brevity. That is the entire grammar of this world, and it takes most people a year to hear it, and another year to speak it without effort.

THE RULE THAT GOVERNS ALL OF IT

Never explain how hard it was. The effort is the thing you are paid to absorb. Handing it back to them, in the form of a story about your afternoon, is handing back the very thing they bought.

## The nine seconds

*What is read about you before you have spoken*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Eliminate the physical tells that precede your first word. Understand that none of this will ever be mentioned to you.

Long before your vocabulary is tested, a great deal has already been decided. Not consciously, and not unkindly. It is simply how humans read one another, and in a closed world the reading is faster and less forgiving.

· Your scent. If it entered before you did, you have already spoken, and you have spoken loudly. · Your hands. They will be near a plate, a child, a document. They are looked at far more than your face. · Your shoes, including the heel. An ancient reflex, and it has not weakened. · Any logo. A logo says: this object was expensive, and I would like that noted. It is the exact opposite of every code in this module. · Sound. Bracelets, heels on stone, keys, a bag that rustles. You must be able to enter a room without being heard. · Your telephone. On a table, it is a small flag announcing that something more interesting may arrive at any moment. And the most important of all, which is not a thing you wear but a thing you do: stillness. Fidgeting, adjusting, shifting your weight, checking, smoothing, touching your hair. Every one of these reads as anxiety, and anxiety in the staff makes a family anxious, which is the precise opposite of what you were hired to produce.

## What to do when you do not know

*The page you will use most often*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Handle a reference you do not recognise, without bluffing and without embarrassment. Convert ignorance into credit.

**4.1 · Why the bluff is fatal**

A name is mentioned. A château, a designer, a school, a village, a vintage, a family. You do not know it. Every instinct will tell you to nod, to smile, to say "of course".

Do not. The world you are entering is extremely small, and the person speaking has usually known the subject for thirty years. Your bluff is detected almost instantly, and it does something far worse than reveal a gap in your knowledge: it reveals that you are willing to pretend. And a person who will pretend about a wine will pretend about an expense, an error, or a message they forgot to pass on. That is the inference, and it is made in silence, and it is never revised.

**THE EXACT WORDS**

"I don't know it. Tell me." Four words. No apology, no explanation, no self-deprecation, and no anxiety. They will tell you. People enjoy explaining what they love, and you have just given them the pleasure of doing so. You will leave that conversation better regarded than the person who nodded, and you will also, incidentally, know something new.

**4.2 · The three ways this goes wrong**

· The apologetic version. "Oh gosh, I'm so sorry, I really should know that." You have now made your ignorance into an event, and asked them to reassure you. Never ask a principal to reassure you. · The deflecting version. "Ah yes, I've heard of it." This is a bluff wearing a coat. · The over-correcting version. Going home, reading for two hours, and mentioning it pointedly the next day. That is a display, and displays are the error. If it comes up naturally in a month, you will simply know it.

## The codes nobody writes down

*The small things that decide a great deal*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Absorb the conventions that appear in no book and are never explained.

· Never comment on the value of anything. Not the art, not the watch, not the car, not the house. Not to admire it. Especially not to admire it. Noticing the price of things is the clearest signal that one is unaccustomed to them. · Never photograph anything. The table you laid beautifully. The view. The dish. The photograph that ends a career is almost never malicious; it is proud. · Never repeat a compliment you were given by a guest, to the principal. It reads as a request for credit, and credit is the one thing you must never appear to want. · Do not laugh at a joke that is not funny. Warmth, yes; performance, never. They can tell, and it makes them tired. · Do not touch. Not an arm, not a shoulder, not a child's head. In a great many houses, and a great many cultures, this is not a preference. It is a line. · Never be the last to leave a room if the family are still in it, unless you are working in it. · Eat before service, never during, and never standing in a doorway. · Learn the names of the staff before you learn the names of the guests. The staff will decide your first month; the guests will not remember you at all. · Say less than you want to. Then say less than that.

AND ONE THAT IS WORTH MORE THAN THE REST

Never let a principal see you calculate. Not the cost, not the time, not the effort, not whether it is worth it. The visible hesitation before you answer, the small breath before you say yes: that is the moment they feel themselves become a burden, and no one ever forgets being made to feel that.

## Houses I have seen

*Five people, and the thing that gave them away*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise the shape of a code failure before it is yours.

The candidate who said "luxury" The situation. A very strong candidate, twelve years in five-star hotels, excellent references, interviewed for a lifestyle-manager role in a Geneva household. In forty minutes she used the word "luxury" nine times, and the phrase "high-end clientele" twice.

The lesson. She was not selected, and the family could not have told you why. The consultant could: every use of the word placed her outside the room she was asking to enter. She was describing their life to them, as a brochure would.

**The one who admired the watch**

The situation. A house manager, three weeks in, admiring a watch on the principal's wrist, said warmly: "That is a beautiful piece. Is it the new one?"

The lesson. Nothing was said. But he had just demonstrated that he knew what it cost, that he had been looking, and that the object interested him. Never notice the value of anything. The correct response to a beautiful object in a private house is no response at all.

**The one who explained**

The situation. A concierge who had spent six hours rescuing a dinner after a chef cancelled, and who told the principal, in some detail, what she had done to save it.

The lesson. The principal thanked her warmly, and never asked her to arrange a dinner again. She had handed back the effort. The correct sentence was: "It is arranged." Nothing more, ever.

**The one who bluffed**

The situation. Asked whether she knew a small château in the Loire, she said "of course, it's wonderful". The principal, who had grown up ten kilometres from it, asked a friendly follow-up question.

The lesson. It took nine seconds. He never mentioned it, and he never trusted a word she said again. The bluff does not reveal a gap. It reveals that you are willing to pretend, and that inference travels to everything else you touch.

**The one who was heard**

The situation. A guest-relations manager, excellent at her work, whose bracelets could be heard before she entered a room, and whose perfume remained in it afterwards.

The lesson. She was moved, gently, to a role with less contact with the family. Nobody told her why, and she believed it was a promotion. In this world, you must be able to arrive without announcing yourself. Everything else is negotiable. That is not.

## Glossary of the module

Understatement · The governing register. From emotion to fact, from length to brevity. The tell · A small act of self-display that identifies an outsider in seconds. The bluff · Pretending to recognise a reference. It reveals not ignorance but willingness to pretend. "Certainly." · The correct answer to almost any request. Closes, commits, contains no emotion. "It is arranged." · The correct account of a difficult afternoon. Nothing more, ever. "I don't know it. Tell me." · The four words that save a career, and occasionally build one.

**Self-assessment**

1. Explain, in your own words, why saying "luxury" places you outside the room.

2. Convert these into the professional register: "It was an amazing evening." "The chef is incredible." "I finally managed to get the table." "It was really difficult, but I sorted it." 3. A vintage is mentioned that you do not know. Give your exact words, and then give the three wrong versions and why each fails. 4. Why is a visible logo a code failure? Answer in terms of what it announces.

5. What is the single thing you must never let a principal see you do, and why is it unforgivable?

6. Of the nine unwritten codes in Chapter V, which two are hardest for you? Be honest.
