## What you are producing

*And why the signature matters*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the function of a written, signed code. Distinguish a commitment from an intention.

**1.1 · Why written**

Because a line drawn in calm holds, and a line you must find in the moment does not. Everything that goes wrong in this profession goes wrong at speed, at the end of a long day, when someone charming asks a question at an odd angle. In that moment you will not reason. You will reach for whatever is already there. This document is what will be there.

**1.2 · Why signed**

Because signing changes something. It is not magic and it is not a legal instrument; it is simply the difference between an idea you agree with and a decision you have taken. You will know which of the two you have made when you read it back in six months.

**DEFINITION**

Your code of conduct. A one-page document, in the first person, stating what you will always do, what you will never do, and what you will do when you do not know. Signed and dated by you.

## The specification

*Seven sections*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Produce each section to the required standard.

**Section.** What it contains

**1 · What I will never do.** Between five and eight absolute commitments. No conditions, no "unless", no "generally". These are the lines that do not move at three in the morning.

**2 · What I will always do.** Five commitments about conduct: earliness, reporting, consistency, errors, escalation.

**3 · My channels.** Where household information may live, and where it may never. Devices, applications, cloud, notes, photographs. Be specific: name the applications you use today, and say what changes.

**4 · My sentences.** The eight scripts from Module 3, written from memory. If you cannot write them from memory, you have not done the work, and you will not have them when you need them.

**5 · The lines I will not cross, whatever the price.** The improper request. Name three specific things you will refuse even if refusing costs you the position. Be honest, and be specific: vague nobility here is worthless.

**6 · How I protect myself.** Your days off. Your one relationship outside this world. Your professional confidant, named. Your rule about the drift.

**7 · The signature.** Your name, and the date. Written by hand, and photographed into the document.

## Standards

A commitment is falsifiable. An intention is not.

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Distinguish a real commitment from a comfortable sentence.

WORTHLESS · WHAT MOST PEOPLE WRITE

"I will always be discreet." "I will act with integrity at all times." "I will be professional and respect confidentiality." Every one of these is unfalsifiable, and none of them would stop you doing a single thing you were about to do. They are decoration.

REAL · WHAT A COMMITMENT SOUNDS LIKE

"I will never photograph anything inside a residence, a car, an aircraft or a yacht. Including for myself. Including the empty room." "I will never state a departure date, a return date, or the occupancy of a residence to anyone outside the channels the house designates. Including to friends. Including when the question is affectionate." "I will report every error to the office on the same day, with two options and a recommendation attached, before I attempt to fix it quietly." "I will take my two days off away from the residence. I will not answer non-urgent messages on them. If I break this twice in a month, I will tell my mentor." "I will never say 'I passed it on' unless I passed it on."

Each real commitment names a specific behaviour, closes the obvious exception before you can reach for it, and could be broken, which is precisely what makes it worth signing.

## A complete worked example

*A composite, honestly done*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read a finished code. Use its shape, never its content.

1 · WHAT I WILL NEVER DO

I will never photograph anything inside a residence, a vehicle, an aircraft or a yacht. Including the empty room. Including for myself. I will never disclose a movement, a departure date, or the occupancy of a residence outside the designated channel. Including to my family. I will never speak of a former household, in any room, to anyone, for the rest of my life. I will never conceal, delay or soften an error of mine. I will never take, accept or be owed anything by a supplier. I will never say something untrue on behalf of anyone in the house, however small, however lightly it is asked. I will never repeat a compliment about myself to the principal.

2 · WHAT I WILL ALWAYS DO

I will be early. Not on time. I will report every error the same day, with two options and a recommendation attached. I will be the same on Tuesday as on Sunday, whatever was said to me yesterday. I will escalate anything that troubles me to the office, in writing, the same day, even when it feels disproportionate. I will keep a dated, factual log of instructions and decisions. Facts only. No opinions, ever.

3 · MY CHANNELS
Household information lives in two places and nowhere else: the household email account, and the shared household calendar. That is the whole list.
What changes today. The household account comes off my personal phone's mail app and goes into a separate work profile. WhatsApp is for logistics with the staff and never for anything about the family; anything that arrives there about the family is moved to the household email and deleted from WhatsApp the same day. Notes and Reminders on my personal phone hold nothing about the house, not a name, not an address, not a flight time. No household document goes into my personal cloud, and I have checked that the household account is not backing up to it. My camera roll contains no interior, no vehicle, no document, no child. My log is on paper, in a notebook that stays in the house, and if it ever leaves the house it is because I am carrying it, and it does not leave again.
What I have deleted this week: four photographs taken inside a former residence, one of them of an empty hallway, and a set of contact entries that identified a former principal by name.

4 · MY SENTENCES
To the press. "I am not able to help with that." Then end the call, courteously, and add nothing.
To the charming friend. "I honestly do not keep track of the family's plans. The office would know."
To the supplier who wants an introduction. "I do not make introductions, I am afraid. Do send your details to the office."
To anyone at all, about anything that troubles me. "I am not able to help with that, but let me pass it to the office."
To the request to say something untrue. "I would rather not. I am sure you understand."
To the invitation to gossip. Nothing. A pleasant expression, and: "Is the car booked for four?"
To the principal, about my own error. "I made an error with the date. Here is where we are, here are the two ways forward, and I recommend the first."
To the friend who asks what it is like. "It is demanding, and I love it."

5 · THE LINES I WILL NOT CROSS, WHATEVER THE PRICE

I will not lie to a lawyer, an investigator, or an official, for anyone, at any price. I will not conceal anything that endangers a child. I will not carry a message between two principals in conflict, and I will say so the first time I am asked.

6 · HOW I PROTECT MYSELF

Two days off, away from the residence. Not answering non-urgent messages on them. My sister. She knows nothing about my work and never will, and that is exactly why she is the one. My professional confidant: [name], former colleague, another house. We speak monthly, about the shape of things and never the content. My rule about the drift: if I am ever invited to sit down after the work is finished, and I want to, I will note the date. If it happens three times, I will speak to my confidant. I would rather notice it early than be moved on gently in a year.

7 · THE SIGNATURE
Signed, in my own hand, on 14 October 2026.
[Handwritten signature, photographed and placed here]
I have read this document in full. I wrote every line of it myself, and I have removed two commitments that I did not believe I would keep at three in the morning. What remains is what I will actually do.

## The marking rubric

*How I will read it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Anticipate the criteria.

**Criterion.** What earns the mark

**Falsifiability.** Every commitment could be broken. If it could not, it is decoration and it will be struck out.

**The closed exception.** Each line closes the obvious escape route before you can reach for it: "including for myself", "including to my family", "including when the question is affectionate".

**The sentences.** The eight scripts, from memory, correct. This is not a memory exercise. It is the reason you will still be employed in five years.

**The uncomfortable lines.** Section 5 names three specific things you will refuse even at the cost of the position. Vague nobility earns nothing.

**The signature.** By hand. Dated. If you are not prepared to sign it, the document is not finished, and neither is the decision.

## The American annex

*What an agreement can and cannot do here*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand at-will employment, the real limits of an American confidentiality agreement, and what happens when you are checked.

**A3.1 · At-will**

In almost every American state, private employment is at-will. Either side may end it at any time, for any reason that is not an unlawful one, with no notice required unless your written agreement says otherwise. There is no statutory probation period and no statutory notice period. Whatever protection you have is the protection you negotiated, in writing, before you started.

The practical consequence is one sentence long. Get notice in writing, on both sides. A household that will not put thirty days on paper has just told you what its last three departures looked like.

**A3.2 · The limits of the agreement you sign**

Sign it. Honour it. And know its edges, because a professional knows what she signed.

**It cannot cover a crime.** No confidentiality agreement requires you to conceal unlawful conduct, to lie to an investigator, or to obstruct one.

**It cannot silence a harassment or assault claim in advance.** The Speak Out Act, federal law since December 2022, makes a pre-dispute non-disclosure or non-disparagement clause unenforceable in disputes involving sexual harassment or sexual assault. A clause you signed before anything happened cannot be used to stop you speaking about that.

**It cannot always prevent a report to a regulator.** Whistleblower protections exist, they vary, and they are real.

If you are ever in this territory you do not reason it out alone, and you do not raise it inside the house first. You call an employment attorney in your state, and you do it before you say anything to anyone.

None of this lowers the standard of this week. It is the floor, not the ceiling, and you will never stand on it if you never talk.

**A3.3 · You will be checked, and so may your credit**

Expect identity, criminal record, employment and reference checks, and in this profession frequently a credit check, because you will be handling household money.

When a household or an agency uses a third party to run them, the Fair Credit Reporting Act applies. You must be told in writing, you must consent, and if something in the report costs you the role you are entitled to a copy and to dispute it.

Run your own credit report once, now, before you begin applying. Finding the error yourself in March is a completely different event from a consultant finding it in October.

## What anticipation is

*And why it is a method, not a talent*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Define anticipation precisely, and distinguish it from guessing. Understand why anticipation is the single skill that decides your salary.

**1.1 · A definition**

**DEFINITION**

Anticipation. The systematic construction of a future need from present evidence, resolved before the principal becomes aware that the need existed.

Read the definition again and notice the three parts. Systematic: it is a process, repeatable, teachable, not a flash of inspiration. From present evidence: it is built from what you observed, not from what you imagined. Before they become aware: that is the whole art. If they had to notice, you were late.

**1.2 · The uncomfortable truth about your value**

Competence is not rare. A household can hire competence in a week. Any decent agency can send them three people who will book flights correctly, manage a renovation and never lose a receipt. What is rare, and what is paid, is the person who removes friction from a life before the friction arrives. That is the difference between an excellent assistant at 70,000 and an estate manager at 185,000. It is not more work. It is a different kind of attention.

**1.3 · The sentence you are working towards**

THE MEASURE OF THE WHOLE WEEK

The principal never says thank you, because the principal never knew there was anything to thank you for. If you need to be thanked, you will not be able to do this work. The praise you are looking for is silence, and a life that runs.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Anticipation is a method built on evidence, not a talent and not a guess. Competence is cheap. Removing friction before it arrives is what is actually paid. The reward for perfect anticipation is that nobody notices. Make peace with that now.

## The three levels of service

*And why only one of them is a career*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Place any action on the reactive, proactive or anticipatory scale. Recognise which level you currently operate at, honestly.

**Level.** What it looks like, and what it is worth

**Reactive.** They ask, you deliver, well and quickly. This is the floor. It is what "good service" means everywhere else in the world, and it is entirely replaceable. Paid: an ordinary salary.

**Proactive.** You see a need forming and you offer. "Would you like me to arrange a car?" Better. But notice what you have done: you have handed them a decision. You have made them think. Paid: a good salary.

**Anticipatory.** The car is downstairs. The bag is in it. Nothing was asked, nothing was offered, nothing was discussed, and there was no decision to make. Paid: what this profession actually pays.

**2.1 · Why the middle level is a trap**

Most of the ambitious people in this field get stuck at proactive, and they are proud of it. They think that offering is service. But every offer is a small tax on the principal's attention, and their attention is the scarcest thing they own. Every question you ask is a small failure of preparation. That is harsh, and it is not entirely fair, and you should still write it down. Some questions are unavoidable and this module will tell you exactly which ones. But the count matters. Track it for a week and you will improve without doing anything else.

**2.2 · The exception, which is important**

Anticipation never applies to matters of consent, money above your threshold, the children's safety, or anything irreversible. There you always ask, always, and asking is not a failure: it is the rule. We will return to this in Chapter IX, and it is the chapter that keeps people employed.

## Observation

*The discipline nobody teaches, because everyone assumes it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Practise structured observation rather than passive noticing. Know the six categories of signal, and record them.

**3.1 · The difference between noticing and observing**

Everyone notices. Almost nobody observes. Noticing is passive, it happens to you, and it is gone by evening. Observation is deliberate, categorised, and written down within the hour. If it is not written down, it did not happen, because you will not remember it in March.

**3.2 · The six categories**

**Category.** What you are collecting

**Preference.** The coffee, the pillow, the seat on the aircraft, the side of the bed, the temperature of the room, the brand of water. Trivial individually. Decisive collectively.

**Pattern.** What happens every Tuesday. Who calls before a difficult trip. What always runs late. Patterns are where anticipation actually comes from.

**Threshold.** How much lateness is tolerated. What level of spend needs approval. How much noise is acceptable before nine. Every house has thresholds and no house writes them down.

**Aversion.** What they will never say aloud but never tolerate twice. A tone. A supplier. A flower. An assistant who explains too much.

**Rhythm.** Energy across the day. When they are approachable. When they are not. When decisions get made and when they get postponed.

**Anomaly.** Anything that departs from the pattern. An anomaly is either a problem forming or a preference changing, and both matter urgently.

**3.3 · The discipline**

Ten minutes, at the end of every day, without exception. You write what you observed in the six categories. Not opinions. Not feelings. Not "she seemed annoyed". Facts: "left the table before coffee, second time this week, both times after a call with the London office." Do this for ninety days and you will know things about the household that nobody has told you and nobody could have told you. That file is your career.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Observe in six categories: preference, pattern, threshold, aversion, rhythm, anomaly. Ten minutes a day, in writing, facts only. Never opinions, never emotions. Anomalies are urgent. They are either a problem forming or a preference changing.

## The preference file

*What goes in it, what never does, and where it lives*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build a preference file that is useful and that could survive being read aloud in court. Understand the confidentiality rules that govern it.

**4.1 · The rule that governs everything in this chapter**

Everything you learned in Week 3 applies here without exception. The preference file is the most sensitive document you will ever hold, and it is the one people are most careless with, because it feels administrative.

THE TEST, BEFORE ANYTHING IS WRITTEN

Write nothing you would not be comfortable reading aloud to the principal, in their study, tomorrow morning. No health. No relationships. No finances. No judgements. No shorthand you would have to explain. If the file were found, it should be boring.

**4.2 · The structure**

**Section.** Contents

**The person.** How they take coffee and tea, at what hour. Water, still or sparkling, brand, temperature. Breakfast. Allergies and dietary rules, recorded exactly, never approximately. Scents they cannot tolerate.

**The house.** Temperature by room. Lighting in the evening. Flowers: which, and which never. Music, or the absence of it. Who is allowed in which rooms, and when.

**Travel.** Airline, seat, cabin. Hotels: the property, the room, the floor, and the rooms they have refused. Cars: model, driver, temperature, whether conversation is welcome. What is always packed. What is never packed.

**Section.** Contents

**The table.** Wines, waters, coffee. The restaurants and the tables within them. The dishes they return to. The ones they sent back, once, and never mentioned.

**Rhythm.** The hours. When to approach and when not to. The day of the week that is always difficult, and why, if you know.

**Standing decisions.** Everything they have already decided so that they never have to decide it again. This section is the whole point of the file.

**4.3 · Where it lives**

In the channel the house has designated, and nowhere else. Not on your personal telephone. Not in your personal cloud. Not in a notebook that travels home with you in a handbag on a train. When you leave the house, it stays. It is not yours, it was never yours, and taking it is theft of the most damaging kind. The professionals who take their preference file to the next house are the professionals who are never hired at that level again.

## The anticipation loop

*Four movements, in order, every time*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Run the loop deliberately until it becomes automatic. Understand why the fourth movement is the one that separates professionals from staff.

**Movement.** What you actually do

**1 · Observe.** Collect the evidence. What is on the calendar. What was said this morning, and in what tone. What changed. What always happens next in this pattern.

**2 · Infer.** Construct the need. "She flies Thursday. She always calls her mother the evening before a long flight. She has never once found the charger in the bag she takes." This is the movement that decides whether you are anticipating or guessing, and the chapter after this one sets out how it is actually done.

**3 · Prepare.** Resolve it, quietly, in advance, and prepare the second option in case the first fails. Prepared, not announced. Nothing is said.

**4 · Withdraw.** Say nothing. Take no credit. Do not hover in the doorway waiting for it to be noticed. Withdraw completely, and go and do the next thing.

**6.1 · Why the fourth movement is the hard one**

Anyone can be taught the first three. The fourth is where careers are made, and it is almost purely a matter of ego. The person who prepares beautifully and then lets it be known that they prepared beautifully has undone the work. They have converted a smooth morning into a small debt of gratitude, and gratitude is a weight. You were hired to remove weight.

**5.2 · The loop, worked through**

**Evidence.** The inference, and the preparation

**The principal has a board meeting Thursday at 09:00 in Geneva, and it is now Monday.** Thursday needs a suit. The suit was worn in Milan and has not been cleaned. The car must leave at 06:40, not 07:00, because the works on the airport road have not finished. The Geneva office has never once had still water in the room. All four of these are resolved on Monday, and none of them are mentioned.

**The younger child has been quiet at breakfast for three days.** This is an anomaly, not a preference. You do not interpret it, you do not investigate it, and you certainly do not comfort the child in a way that positions you. You note it factually and you make sure the person whose responsibility it is has the opportunity to see it too. Anticipation has a boundary, and this is it.

**Two suppliers have both said "next week" for the third time.** The renovation is going to miss its date. The principal does not yet know. You do not wait to be asked, and you do not present a disaster: you present the revised date, the reason in one sentence, and the two options, before they hear it from anyone else.

## How to infer

*The movement everybody skips, written down*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Turn a set of observations into a prepared action deliberately, with a rule for how certain you must be before you move, and a rule for what to do when two signals disagree.

**5.1 · The three-occurrence rule**

One occurrence is an event. Two is a coincidence. Three is a pattern, and only a pattern earns an action.

This rule exists because the most expensive error in this movement is not failing to anticipate. It is anticipating something that was never there, acting on it, and being seen to have decided something on a household's behalf on the strength of one Tuesday. Write the first two down and do nothing with them. Wait for the third.

The exception is an explicit statement. If the principal says once, in words, that she does not take calls before eight, that is not one occurrence. That is an instruction, and it counts immediately.

**5.2 · The two questions**

Every inference is the same two questions, in this order.

**What will this person need that they have not asked for?** Construct the need out loud, in a full sentence, with the evidence in it. "She flies Thursday. She has called her mother the evening before each of the last three long flights. The charger has not been in the cabin bag on any of them."

**What would have to be true for me to be wrong?** Answer it honestly before you move. If the answer is "she would have to have changed her mind, which she does often", you are not preparing, you are guessing.

**5.3 · The confidence ladder**

This is the whole method, and it fits in four lines. Two questions decide everything: how certain are you, and can it be undone.

**Certain, and reversible.** Act. Say nothing. This is the ordinary work of the job.

**Certain, and irreversible.** Prepare it completely, to the last detail, and then ask before you execute. Being right does not make it your decision.

**Uncertain, and reversible.** Prepare the option and hold it. Do not execute. The car is booked and cancellable, the table is held under your name, the second flight is on standby. If it turns out to be needed, it takes you ninety seconds.

**Uncertain, and irreversible.** Ask. Plainly, now, in one sentence, without building a case for what you think.

If you learn nothing else this week, learn the second line. Almost every catastrophic overstep in this profession is a person who was certain and correct, and who acted on something that could not be undone.

**5.4 · When two signals disagree**

They will, constantly, and the order of precedence is fixed.

**An instruction given today beats a pattern observed over months.** People change their minds and are not obliged to tell you why.

**Written beats spoken.** Not because writing is more true, but because it is what you will be judged against.

**This house beats your last house.** An inference imported from a former household is the single most common error made by experienced people, and it is worse than having no inference at all, because it is delivered with confidence.

**When two people in the house want different things, you do not choose.** You return the decision, exactly as Week 1 set out. You are not the arbitrator of a marriage, and any household in which you become one will not keep you.

**5.5 · The four bad inferences**

**The flattering one.** You inferred the thing that would make you look clever if you were right. Discard it and wait for the third occurrence.

**The imported one.** It was true in your last house. It has no standing here until you have seen it here, three times.

**The single-source one.** One member of staff told you what the principal likes. That is one occurrence, from an interested party, and it is not evidence.

**The stale one.** It was true in March. Preferences decay, households change, and a file that is not revised becomes a set of confident errors. Reread your preference file monthly and mark what you have not seen in ninety days.

## Reading a room, and reading a mood

*Without ever appearing to be reading anything*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Adjust your presence to the state of the room within seconds of entering it. Never mistake mood for instruction.

**6.1 · The scan**

Every time you enter a room, before you speak, three seconds:

**·.** Who is here, and what is the relationship between them right now? A room with a difficult conversation in it feels different, and you will feel it.

**·.** What is the energy? Light, heavy, hurried, still. This decides your pace, your volume, and whether you speak at all.

**·.** Is this a moment for me, or should I not be here? The correct answer, more often than new staff believe, is that you should not be here. Leave quietly and return in ten minutes.

**6.2 · Matching, not mirroring**

You match the room's register. In a light room you may be light. In a heavy room you become quiet, brief and efficient, and you do not attempt to lift the mood, because lifting the mood is a form of commentary and commentary is not your role.

**6.3 · The rule that will save you**

**MOOD IS NOT INSTRUCTION**

A principal who is irritated has not given you an order. A principal who is charming has not given you permission. You read the mood in order to calibrate your presence, never in order to change your standards. The standards are the standards on the good days and on the bad ones, and that is precisely why they are worth something.

## The second option

*And the pre-mortem, which is the most useful hour of your week*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Run a pre-mortem on anything that matters. Always hold a second option, and never announce it.

**7.1 · The pre-mortem**

**DEFINITION**

The pre-mortem. Before the event, you assume it has already failed, and you write down why. Not a list of risks. A post-mortem of a failure that has not happened yet.

It takes twenty minutes and it is the single highest-return habit in this profession. The question is not "what could go wrong". The question is "it went wrong. What was it?" The second question produces specific answers, and the first produces vague ones.

**7.2 · The three failures you are looking for**

**·.** The single point of failure. One car, one driver, one supplier, one flight, one key.

Anything that exists once is a failure waiting for a date.

**·.** The assumption nobody has checked. "The restaurant knows." Does it? Someone confirmed it, in writing, this week, with a name attached?

**·.** The handover. Almost every failure in this work happens at a boundary: between you and the driver, the driver and the hotel, the office and the house. Nothing fails in the middle of a well-run system. It fails at the seams.

**7.3 · The second option**

For anything that matters, there is a second option. The second restaurant is held. The second car is on standby. The second flight is known, with the price and the time. And it is never announced. You do not tell the principal that you have a backup, because telling them means telling them the primary might fail, and that is precisely the anxiety you were hired to remove. The second option is your private insurance, and its purpose is that nothing ever appears to have gone wrong.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The pre-mortem: assume it failed, then explain why. Twenty minutes, before anything that matters. Failures live at the seams: handovers, boundaries, assumptions nobody confirmed. Hold a second option for everything that matters, and never say a word about it.

## Standards, mise en place, and the reset

*The invisible architecture of a house that runs*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the reset, and why it is done at night. Hold a standard when nobody is watching, which is the only time it counts.

**8.1 · Mise en place, borrowed from a better trade**

**DEFINITION**

Mise en place. Everything in its place, before it is needed. From the kitchen, where it is the difference between a service that works and a service that collapses at eight o'clock.

In a house, it means the same thing. The bag is packed the night before. The documents are printed before the meeting, not during it. The car is fuelled on Sunday. You do not do things when they are needed. You do them before, when nobody is watching, and that is why they appear effortless.

**8.2 · The reset**

Every evening, the house returns to its baseline. Not because it is untidy: because a household that begins each day from the same state is a household in which nothing is ever lost, and nothing is ever hunted for at seven in the morning.

**8.3 · The standard when nobody is watching**

The wine is poured correctly when they are not in the room. The flowers are turned when the house is empty. The car is spotless on the day nobody is being driven anywhere. This sounds like theatre and it is exactly the opposite of theatre. A standard that is performed for an audience is not a standard, it is a display, and everyone in this world can tell the difference within a fortnight.

## The limits of anticipation

*The chapter that keeps you employed*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise over-anticipation, presumption and the servile drift. Know exactly where you always ask, without exception.

**9.1 · Over-anticipation**

Anticipation applied without evidence is guessing, and guessing that turns out well is worse than guessing that turns out badly, because it teaches you to do it again. The staff member who books the restaurant they think the principal will love, having never once seen them eat there, has not anticipated anything. They have imposed a preference and called it service.

**9.2 · Presumption**

There is a line, and it is not subtle once you know it exists. On one side you are removing friction. On the other you are making decisions about their life, and no salary in this profession comes with that right.

**You always ask, without exception.** Why

**Anything irreversible.** A cancellation with a penalty, a signature, a purchase that cannot be returned, a message sent on their behalf. Irreversible means you ask, every time, even when the answer is obvious.

**Money above your threshold.** Every house has a figure. Learn yours in the first week and never test it. Being right about a spend you were not authorised to make is still a breach.

**The children.** Their movements, their health, their schooling, their friendships. You execute, and you do not initiate.

**Anything involving other people's consent.** You do not volunteer them, commit them, or share their information, however convenient it would be.

**You always ask, without exception.** Why

**Anything you would rather they did not find out about.** That instinct is not shyness. It is your judgement telling you the answer, and it is almost always right.

**9.3 · The servile drift**

Anticipation is a professional discipline, not an act of devotion. The person who begins to anticipate emotionally rather than operationally, who wants to be needed, who takes pleasure in being the only one who knows how the coffee is taken, is drifting towards exactly the trap you studied in Week 3. You are anticipating a household, not a person. Hold that sentence. It is the difference between a long career and a short, intense one that ends without explanation.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Anticipation without evidence is guessing, and it is not your job. Irreversible, expensive, involving the children, or involving anyone else's consent: you ask. Always. You anticipate a household, not a person. The moment that inverts, you are in danger.

**Glossary**

Anticipation · The systematic construction of a future need from present evidence, resolved before it is felt. The anticipation loop · Observe, infer, prepare, withdraw. In that order, every time. The preference file · The written record of a household's preferences, patterns and standing decisions. Boring by design, confidential by rule. Standing decision · Something the principal has already decided, recorded so they never have to decide it again. Threshold · The limit, usually unspoken, beyond which you must ask: spend, delay, deviation. Anomaly · A departure from an established pattern. Either a problem forming or a preference changing. Mise en place · Everything in its place, before it is needed. The reset · The nightly return of the house to its baseline state. The pre-mortem · Assuming the failure has already happened, and writing down what it was. The second option · The prepared alternative, held privately and never announced. Over-anticipation · Acting on a need you invented rather than observed. Presumption · Crossing from removing friction into deciding on their behalf.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing, before the practice.

1. Define anticipation in one sentence, and explain what makes it different from a good guess.

2. Place these on the three-level scale, and justify each: booking a car when asked; offering to book a car; the car being downstairs. 3. Name the six categories of observation, and give one real example of each from your own life this week. 4. What are the five things that must never appear in a preference file?

5. Write out the four movements of the anticipation loop, and explain why the fourth is the difficult one. 6. You enter a room and the atmosphere is heavy. What do you change, and what do you never change? 7. Run a pre-mortem on a dinner for twelve at a restaurant, on a Friday, in winter. Give three specific failures and their second options. 8. Give three examples of over-anticipation, and explain why being right would not have saved you. 9. List everything you would always ask about, without exception, and explain the principle that unites the list. 10. Where, honestly, is your own tendency: reactive, proactive, or anticipatory? What is the single habit that would move you up one level?

**Further study**

**·.** Keep the observation log for seven days. Ten minutes each evening, the six categories, on your own life or a household you know. It will feel pointless on day two and obvious on day six.

**·.** Count your questions for one day. Every question you asked that better preparation would have answered. Write the number down. Do it again in a month.

**·.** Read on mise en place, from the kitchen rather than from the service literature. The chefs write about it more honestly, because for them it is survival rather than style.

**·.** Run one pre-mortem on something real this week. Anything: a journey, a meeting, a dinner. Then compare what actually went wrong with what you predicted. That comparison is the whole lesson.

## What they are actually paying for

*It is not the tasks*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the real product you are selling. Stop measuring your value in tasks completed.

Every new person in this profession makes the same error, and it takes about a year to correct. They believe they are being paid for what they do. They are not. They are being paid for what the principal no longer has to think about. That distinction changes everything. It means a day in which you did eleven things badly is worse than useless. It means a day in which you did four things, and the household ran without a single decision reaching the principal, was an excellent day, even though it will look empty on any timesheet.

**THE REAL CURRENCY**

Their attention. Not their time, not their money. Their attention. Every question, every choice, every small friction you pass upwards is a withdrawal from an account that is almost empty. The professionals who understand this are the professionals who become indispensable, and they are usually the quietest people in the house.

And here is the part that is difficult to accept: this means your best work will be invisible, permanently. Nobody will ever say "thank you for the crisis that did not happen". You will be thanked, occasionally, for the visible things, which are usually the least skilled things you did that week. If you need recognition, you will drift towards visible work, and visible work is junior work. This is the fork in the road, and it appears in about month four.

## Noticed, and never mentioned

*The scorecard nobody shows you*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know what is actually being counted. Understand why nothing on this list will ever be discussed with you.

Nobody will tell you that any of this is being observed. All of it is.

**·.** Whether the same thing has to be said to you twice. This is the first and most important measure, and you are permitted almost no failures.

**·.** Whether you write things down. Not because they doubt your memory. Because writing it down is visible proof that you took it seriously.

**·.** Whether you follow up without being chased. The person who has to be reminded has already been reclassified in someone's mind, and nobody will inform them of it.

**·.** Whether you handle a change of plan without visible strain. The sigh. The pause. The very slightly tight smile. All noticed. All counted.

**· ·.** Whether you are the same on the difficult days. Anyone can be excellent in a calm house. Whether you make other people's work easier or harder. The staff talk, always, and their view of you reaches the principal by a route you will never see.

**·.** Whether you leave the small thing undone because it was not your job. Technically you are correct. It will still be held against you, and correctly.

**WHAT THIS MEANS IN PRACTICE**

There is no performance review in a private household. There is a slow accumulation of small observations, and then, one day, a decision. You will not be warned. You will not be given a chance to improve. That is not cruelty: it is simply that in a family's home, a conversation about your shortcomings is more uncomfortable than a replacement.

## The invisible hierarchy

*The one on the organogram is not the real one*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read the real power map of a household. Avoid the errors that new staff make in their first month.

Every house has two hierarchies. One is written down, if you are lucky, in a contract. The other one is real.

**Who.** What you need to understand about them

**The principal.** Rarely the person who runs anything day to day. Their attention is the scarce resource. They will be pleasant and they are not your friend.

**The other principal.** Frequently the one who actually sets the standards inside the house. Underestimating this person is the classic error of ambitious new staff, and it is fatal.

**The family office.** Money, legal, contracts, and often your own employment. They are not the household and they do not always understand it. Keep them informed in writing, always.

**The long-serving staff member.** The housekeeper of nineteen years. She has no title, no budget and no authority, and she can end you in a sentence at the right moment. Treat her as senior to you, because she is.

**The principal's assistant.** Controls the calendar, therefore controls access, therefore controls a great deal. Ally with this person early and honestly.

**The children.** Not staff, not principals, and not your friends. Warm, correct, boundaried. Never a confidant, never a channel, never a source.

**3.1 · The rule for your first month**

Assume everyone outranks you, and behave accordingly. You will never be punished for having been too respectful to the housekeeper. You can be ended for the opposite.

## How to be right without being asked

*The mechanics of getting away with it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Present anticipated work correctly. Understand why the presentation matters as much as the work.

You have anticipated correctly. The problem is solved. Now comes the part that people get wrong, which is what you say about it.

**4.1 · You say almost nothing**

**Situation.** What you say

**It was resolved and they never knew there was anything to resolve.** Nothing. Not a word, not now, not later, not as a light remark in three weeks.

**It was resolved and they will find out anyway.** One sentence, flat, at the right moment. "The Geneva car is at seven, the road is closed." Not: "I noticed the road was closed so I changed the car."

**It was resolved and they need to make a decision about the next step.** The decision, framed, with two options and your recommendation. Never the story of how you got there.

**You anticipated wrongly and it cost nothing.** Nothing. Absorb it. It is your error, it cost them nothing, and it is not their problem.

**You anticipated wrongly and it cost something.** Immediately, the same day, in writing, with the fact, the cost, and what you have already done about it. See Chapter V.

**4.2 · The sentence that ruins it**

"I took the liberty of..." It sounds humble and it is the opposite. It announces the anticipation, it asks for the credit, and it plants a seed of doubt about whether you took a liberty you should not have taken. The work was invisible until you said that sentence. Now it is a small event, and events cost attention. Never announce a favour. Never narrate your own thoughtfulness. The car is downstairs. That is all.

## When you get it wrong

*And you will, and it is survivable, unlike Week 3*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Handle an anticipation error correctly. Understand the one thing that makes it unsurvivable.

You will misread something. You will prepare for the wrong thing, book the wrong table, pack for the wrong weather, assume a pattern that was a coincidence. This is normal, it happens to everyone at every level, and it is not what ends careers. What ends careers is the handling.

**The move.** The result

**You say it.** Absorbed. Sometimes not even remembered. You have proved that bad news immediately, plainly, travels through you at full speed, which is worth more than the error cost. with the cost and the fix

**You wait for a good moment.** There is no good moment, and the delay itself becomes the story. What was an error becomes a concealment, and concealment is Week 3, and Week 3 is final.

**You explain how it happened, at length.** You have made your error into their problem. They do not want the reasoning. They want the fact, the cost, and the correction, in that order, in under thirty seconds.

**You blame a supplier, an airline, the weather.** Even when it is entirely true. You are the person who was supposed to have a second option. Say what happened, say what you have done, and stop talking.

**THE FORMULA, MEMORISED**

"The table at eight is gone. I have Le Jardin at eight fifteen, the same room, and the car is adjusted. I should have confirmed in writing on Monday and I did not." Fact. Fix. Ownership. Three sentences, no excuse, no drama, and then you move on and so do they.

## Houses I have seen

*Six mornings that decided a career*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise these situations before you are inside one.

**The umbrella**

A house in London. An assistant of three months. The principal leaves at eight every morning and walks the four hundred metres to the office, and has done for eleven years. One morning it is raining. She puts an umbrella by the door. She says nothing. He takes it, and says nothing. It is the smallest possible act, and it is not the umbrella that mattered. It was that she had understood, without being told, that he walks, that he would not ask, and that the umbrella had to be there rather than offered. She was made estate manager within two years. Nobody ever mentioned the umbrella again, and she has never mentioned it either.

**The restaurant she loved**

A very capable, very confident assistant. Six weeks in. She books a celebrated restaurant for the principal's anniversary dinner, one she knows is superb, because she wants it to be perfect. The principal had eaten there once, eleven years earlier, with a person he does not discuss. Nothing was said to her. Nothing at all. She was not reprimanded and she was not corrected, and her contract was not renewed at the end of the year, and she has still never understood why. She anticipated from her own taste rather than from evidence, and there was no evidence, because she had not built a preference file.

**The two thousand euros**

A country house. A guest arrives unexpectedly and the estate manager, wishing to be excellent, arranges a helicopter transfer at short notice, at a cost of just over two thousand euros. The guest is delighted. The principal, when he learns of it, says nothing at all.

The threshold in that house, unwritten, was five hundred euros, and he had never authorised a single expense above it without being asked. The transfer was the right decision. It was also not his decision to make. He was right, and he was still wrong, and being right made it worse rather than better, because it demonstrated that he would act on his own judgement about their money. He left eight months later, with a good reference, for reasons that were never explained to him.

**The charger**

A principal who travels constantly and who had, for years, arrived at hotels without a charger, and had for years bought another one at the airport, and had never once mentioned it. A new assistant, in her second week, put a second charger in the outer pocket of every bag, permanently, in every residence. She did not mention it. He never mentioned it. It simply stopped being a thing that happened. Three years later he described her to a friend, in a sentence she never heard, as "the reason I do not think about anything". That sentence is what this profession pays for.

**The one who hovered**

Excellent work. Genuinely excellent, for eighteen months. Everything prepared, everything anticipated, nothing missed. And after each one, she stayed in the doorway. Half a second. A small, hopeful pause. Waiting for it to be seen. The house found her exhausting and could not have explained why. She had turned every act of service into a small request for acknowledgement, and a hundred small requests a week is a weight, and she had been hired to remove weight. She was let go, warmly, with an excellent reference, and she thinks to this day that it was about money.

**The one who said nothing at all**

A private chef of many years. The principal's wife had, quietly, stopped eating dinner. He noticed it because it was his profession to notice it. He said nothing to her, and nothing to the household, and nothing to the staff. He simply began making the lunches larger, and warmer, and made sure something was always in the kitchen at eleven at night.

That is the whole story, and the loop is in it four times over. He observed. He inferred. He prepared. And he withdrew, completely, and never once let her know that she had been seen.

**Glossary**

Attention · The principal's scarcest resource, and the thing you are actually paid to protect. Invisible work · The crisis that did not happen. Your best work, and the work you will never be thanked for. The invisible hierarchy · The real power map of a house, which is never the one in the contract. The threshold · The unwritten spending limit. Being right about exceeding it is still exceeding it. The hover · The half second in the doorway, waiting to be noticed. It undoes the work. Fact, fix, ownership · The three-sentence formula for reporting your own error. In that order, in under thirty seconds.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing. Be honest, particularly on the last one.

1. What are you actually being paid for? Answer without using the word "tasks".

2. Why is a day with eleven visible achievements potentially a worse day than one with four?

3. List five things that are being counted and that will never be discussed with you.

4. Who, in a household, is likely to be more senior than their title suggests, and why?

5. Rewrite this sentence correctly: "I took the liberty of moving your car to seven, because I saw the road was closed." 6. You booked the wrong restaurant and it cost the principal an hour. Write your exact words, in three sentences. 7. In the case of the two thousand euros: he was right. Explain, precisely, why being right made it worse. 8. In the case of the one who hovered: she did everything correctly. What was she actually asking for, and why could the house not tolerate it? 9. Read the last case again. Name each of the four movements of the loop as he performed them. 10. Which of the six cases is closest to a mistake you would make? Do not choose the flattering answer.

## How this week is practised

*Four exercises, in order*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what each exercise is training, and why the order matters.

**Exercise.** What it trains, and how long it takes

**1 · The observation log.** Structured attention. Ten minutes a night, seven nights. This is the foundation and everything else is built on it.

**2 · The question count.** Awareness of how often you outsource your own preparation. One day, no extra time, considerable discomfort.

**3 · Twelve scenarios.** Judgement under ambiguity, including knowing when not to act. About two hours.

**4 · The pre-mortem.** Structured pessimism. Twenty minutes on something real in your own life.

## Exercise one

*The observation log, seven days*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Practise structured observation on a real environment. Produce evidence, not impressions.

Choose one environment you are in every day. Your household. A workplace. A family you know. It does not need to be a wealthy one, and it is better if it is not, because the discipline is what is being trained, not the context. Every evening, ten minutes, in writing, in the six categories. Facts only. If a sentence contains the words "seemed", "felt", "probably" or "I think", it is not an observation and it does not belong in the log.

**Category.** What you write

**Preference.** One line. Something someone likes, precisely, not approximately.

**Pattern.** Something that has now happened more than twice at the same time or in the same way.

**Threshold.** Something that was tolerated, and something that was not.

**Aversion.** Something nobody said aloud and everyone worked around.

**Rhythm.** When this person or this house is approachable, and when they are not.

**Anomaly.** Anything today that departed from the pattern.

**WHAT YOU WILL FIND**

Days one to three are empty and it will feel like a waste of time. Around day four the log starts to fill on its own, because you have begun to see differently during the day rather than remembering at night. That shift is the entire purpose of the exercise, and it cannot be reached by reading about it.

Submit: the seven days, as written. Not tidied. Not improved afterwards.

## Exercise two

*The question count*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Measure how often you outsource your own preparation.

For one full day, count every question you asked someone that better preparation on your part would have answered. Not questions of consent, not questions about irreversible things, not questions about money. Only the ones where you were, in truth, asking someone else to do your thinking. Write the number down. Write down the three worst ones, in full. Then, for each of the three, write the single thing you could have done the day before that would have made the question unnecessary. That list is the beginning of your playbook.

## Twelve scenarios

*Answer each one first, then reveal its debrief*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Apply the loop under ambiguity. Recognise the scenarios in which the correct action is no action at all.

For each: what do you do, what do you say, and what do you deliberately not do. Two or three sentences. Then tap Reveal the debrief on the case.

**1 · The rain**

It is 07:40. The principal leaves on foot at 08:00, as she does every day. It has begun to rain and it was not forecast.

**2 · The airline**

You learn at 21:00 that tomorrow morning's flight, on which the principal is due at a board meeting in Geneva at 09:00, has a high probability of being cancelled. It has not been cancelled yet.

**3 · The anniversary**

You realise, from a photograph in the hall, that next Tuesday is the principal's wedding anniversary. Nobody has mentioned it. Nothing is in the calendar.

**4 · The quiet child**

The younger child, twelve, has been silent at breakfast for four days. He has always been talkative. Nobody else appears to have noticed.

**5 · The friend**

The principal's closest friend telephones the house and asks, warmly and without any apparent motive, whether the family are still going to Sardinia in August.

**6 · The supplier**

The contractor on the London renovation has said "next week" three times. You are now certain the date will be missed by a month. The principal believes it is on schedule.

**7 · The second bottle**

At a private dinner, the principal opens a second bottle of a wine that you know, from the cellar book, is the last of its case and was described to you as irreplaceable.

**8 · The upgrade**

A hotel offers, at no cost, to move the family to a better suite. It is objectively better. The family have stayed in the same suite for six years.

**9 · The message**

The principal says, in passing and while looking at his telephone, "tell Mark I will call him". You do not know which Mark, and there are two.

**10 · The empty diary**

Thursday afternoon, normally the busiest of the week, is entirely empty in the calendar, and has been cleared without explanation.

**11 · The other principal**

The principal's wife asks you, pleasantly, what time her husband got back last night. You know.

**12 · The four hundred euros**

A delivery arrives with the wrong item. The correct one can be couriered today for four hundred euros, or arrive free in six days. The threshold in this house is five hundred. It is the child's birthday gift, and the birthday is tomorrow.

## The debriefs

*Read only after you have written all twelve*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Compare your judgement to the professional standard.

**1 · The rain**

The umbrella is by the door. Nothing is said. You do not ask whether she would like one, because asking hands her a decision at 07:55 on a morning she has already planned. If she walks past it, that is information, and it goes in the file. This is the simplest scenario in the set and it is the one that most people over-complicate by speaking.

**2 · The airline**

Anticipate and inform, in that order. You hold the alternative: the earlier flight, the private option with the price, the train, whichever is real. Then, that evening, one sentence: "There is a risk on the 07:10. I have you held on the 05:50 and the car adjusted." You do not present a problem, you present a resolved problem. And you do inform, because the alternative involves his time and his money, and those are always his.

**3 · The anniversary**

You do nothing, and you say nothing, and this is the trap in the set. It is not your anniversary to manage. You are not their marriage. What you may legitimately do is make sure that the day is not sabotaged by the household: no dinner scheduled, no contractor in the house, the diary quietly kept clear if you have the authority to do so. That is service. Flowers on their behalf, a booking they did not ask for, a card: that is presumption, and it is the servile drift.

**4 · The quiet child**

You observe, you record it factually, and you make certain the person whose responsibility it is has the chance to see what you saw. You do not investigate. You do not ask him what is wrong. You do not become the adult he confides in, however kind that instinct is, because that is the confidante trap in its most seductive form. The correct action is small, boring, and correct: a factual line, to the right person, once.

**5 · The friend**

"I am not able to help with that, but I will let them know you called." Warm, complete, final. Movement information does not travel to friends, and the fact that they are close is not evidence, it is the reason the question is dangerous. You then tell the principal, without drama, that the question was asked. That last part is what separates a professional from someone who simply refused politely.

**6 · The supplier**

Escalate today, and escalate correctly. Not "there may be a delay". You bring: the revised date, the reason in one sentence, the cost, and two options. The error people make here is waiting for certainty. You already have certainty, and every day you sit on it converts a supplier's failure into your own concealment.

**7 · The second bottle**

You say nothing and you pour it. It is his wine. This is the boundary of anticipation in its purest form: you may prepare, you may prevent friction, you may not manage his choices. What you do is note it, and replace what can be replaced, and tell the cellar. The staff member who says "sir, this is the last one" has just made himself the guardian of a grown man's decisions in front of his guests.

**8 · The upgrade**

You decline it, politely, and you do not mention it. Six years in the same suite is not an accident, it is a preference, and a preference of that depth outranks any objective improvement. If you are uncertain, you hold both and say nothing. The rule underneath this one is large: better is not a reason. Evidence is a reason.

**9 · The message**

You ask. Immediately, in the same breath, before he has looked back at his telephone. "Mark Denison, or Mark at the office?" One second, no friction, done. This is the scenario that tests whether you have understood the module or merely enjoyed it: guessing between two Marks is not anticipation, it is a coin toss with your career on it. Anticipation is built on evidence, and you have none.

**10 · The empty diary**

You do nothing, you ask nothing, and you protect it absolutely. You do not fill it, you do not enquire, you do not let anyone else book into it, and you do not speculate, to anyone, ever, about why it is empty. A cleared afternoon is one of the most confidential objects in a household.

**11 · The other principal**

"I am not sure of the exact time." And then nothing else, and no elaboration, and no expression, and you do not look away. This is Week 3 arriving in the middle of Week 4, and it will arrive exactly like this: warmly, from someone with every right to ask, about something trivial. You do not carry information between principals, in either direction, ever. You will feel dishonest. You are not being dishonest, you are being a professional, and the day you carry one sentence between them you have become a channel and channels are removed.

**12 · The four hundred euros**

You ask, and you ask in a way that costs one second of attention: "The gift will not arrive in time. Four hundred to have it tomorrow, or it is six days. Shall I?" You have prepared everything, you have decided nothing. This is the model answer for the entire week. It is under the threshold, it is obviously the right call, you are certainly correct, and it is still not your decision, because it is their child and their money. Perfect preparation, presented as a one-second decision, is the highest form of this craft.

## Exercise four

*The pre-mortem*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Run a real pre-mortem, and then check it against reality.

Choose something real that is happening in your own life in the next fourteen days. A journey, a dinner, a meeting, a move. Twenty minutes. Assume it has failed. Write down what the failure was, in the past tense, with specificity. Then for each failure, write the single-point-of-failure, the unchecked assumption, or the handover that caused it. Then write the second option. Afterwards, when it has happened, compare. Submit both: the pre-mortem, and one paragraph on what actually went wrong and whether you had seen it. That second paragraph is worth more than the first.

## What you are producing

*A working instrument*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what the playbook is for.

The playbook is the document you open on your first morning in a new household, and again at the end of your first week, and again after your first mistake. It is the written form of your own method, in your own words, and by the end of the programme it will be one of the most valuable things you own. It has four pages and five sections. It is written for a reader who is tired, in a hurry, and slightly frightened. That reader is you, in month one.

## Section by section

*What goes in each*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build each section correctly.

**Section.** What it contains

**1 · My observation system.** Your six categories, written as questions you will ask yourself every evening. The time of day you will do it. Where the log will live. Be specific: "22:00, in the black notebook, before I put the telephone away." Vague systems are not systems.

**2 · The preference file: my structure.** Your blank template, ready to be filled on day one. Every field you will collect, in order. And, written at the top of it, the five things that must never appear in it.

**3 · My anticipation loop.** The four movements, in your own words, with one line each on how you personally will fail at each of them. You know your own weaknesses. Write them down where you will see them.

**4 · My thresholds and my asks.** The list of situations in which you will always ask, without exception. This is the section that will keep you employed. Write it as commands, not as principles: "I always ask before anything irreversible." Not: "irreversibility is an important consideration."

**5 · My pre-mortem checklist.** The five to eight questions you will run before anything that matters. Yours, not mine. If it does not sound like you, you will not use it.

## The template

*The shape of the document*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Follow the structure.

**PAGE ONE**

My observation system. The six categories as six questions. The hour. The place. The rule: facts only, never "seemed", never "felt".

**PAGE TWO**

The preference file. Your blank structure, field by field, ready to use. And the five prohibitions at the head of it, in bold.

**PAGE THREE**

The loop, and my thresholds. Observe, infer, prepare, withdraw, each with the sentence "the way I will get this wrong is...". Then the list of things you will always ask about, written as commands.

**PAGE FOUR**

The pre-mortem. Your questions. And, at the foot of the page, the three sentences you will say when you have made an error: the fact, the fix, the ownership. Written out in full, so that you do not have to compose them on the worst morning of your year.

## Two finished examples

*One day of a log, and one page of a playbook*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

See what the finished instruments look like, so that you are not guessing at the standard.

Both are invented, and both are deliberately ordinary, because ordinary is what these documents look like when they are done properly. Read them, close them, and write your own.

**A · One day of the observation log**

*Tuesday 14 October. 22:00. Six categories. Facts only.*

**Preference.** Coffee returned to the kitchen twice this week at the same point, about half drunk, both times after it had stood for more than ten minutes. Fresh cup taken at 08:20 both mornings.

**Pattern.** Third Thursday running that the 18:00 call has overrun by forty minutes or more. Dinner has been pushed each time.

**Threshold.** Approved the $600 florist invoice without comment. Queried a $1,400 invoice for the same category in September. The line sits somewhere between the two and I have not asked yet.

**Aversion.** Asked twice this month that the hall lamps be off before the family come down. Said nothing the second time, turned them off himself.

**Rhythm.** Leaves the house between 07:40 and 07:50 on weekdays, without exception since I started. Saturdays are unstructured and I should stop scheduling into them.

**Anomaly.** The younger child has taken breakfast in the kitchen rather than the dining room three days running. Recorded, not investigated, not mentioned.

Notice that there is no interpretation anywhere in it. Not one sentence says what any of it means, and not one contains the words seemed, felt, probably or I think. The inference happens later, on paper, once there is enough of this to be worth inferring from.

**B · Page three of the playbook**

*The loop, and my thresholds*

MY LOOP, AND HOW I WILL PERSONALLY FAIL AT IT

**Observe.** Ten minutes at 22:00, six categories, black notebook, facts only. *The way I will get this wrong is that I will skip it on the days that were hardest, which are the days it is worth most.*

**Infer.** Nothing becomes a pattern until it has happened three times. Two occurrences is a note, not a conclusion. *The way I will get this wrong is that I will decide I know someone after eight days, because I am quick and it has flattered me before.*

**Prepare.** The thing is ready before it is asked for, and it is ready quietly. *The way I will get this wrong is over-preparing visibly, so that the preparation is noticed, because I want the credit.*

**Withdraw.** It is presented in one flat sentence, or not presented at all. *The way I will get this wrong is the phrase "I took the liberty of", which I have said out loud twice this year.*

I ALWAYS ASK, WITHOUT EXCEPTION

I always ask before anything irreversible. Anything cut, sold, given away, thrown out, cancelled without a replacement held.

I always ask before spending above the threshold, and I ask what the threshold is in my first week rather than discovering it.

I always ask before anything involving a child's schedule, health, school or travel, however small, and I never act on what a child tells me without confirming it with a parent.

I always ask before anything that touches health, medication, or a doctor.

I always ask before giving any information about the family to anyone outside the household, including a supplier who already knows it.

I always ask before hiring, dismissing, or changing anyone's hours.

I always ask before a first-time supplier crosses the threshold of the house.

I always ask before anything goes into writing that the family have not seen, if it names them.

Notice that every line is a command rather than a principle, that each one is short enough to be found at seven in the morning, and that section three names three real weaknesses rather than flattering ones. If your version of that section makes you look good, you have wasted the page.

## How it will be judged

*Honestly, and against the standard of the profession*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what a weak playbook looks like, so that you do not write one.

**What I am looking for.** What that means

**Specificity.** "I will observe carefully" is worth nothing. "22:00, six categories, black notebook, facts only" is worth something. Every line of this document should be executable by a tired person.

**Your own weaknesses, named.** Section 3 asks you to write down how you will personally fail. If you have written something flattering there, you have wasted the exercise and I will say so.

**The asks.** If your threshold list is short, or vague, or noble rather than specific, you have not understood Chapter IX of Module 1, and that is the chapter that keeps people employed.

**Usability.** Four pages. Could you find the right line in fifteen seconds, at seven in the morning, in a house you do not know? If not, rewrite it.
