## The operating system

*Six domains, and how they fail*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Map any household onto the six operational domains. Understand that every domain fails in a predictable way.

**1.1 · The domains**

Every private household, whatever its size, runs on six operational domains. A two-person flat in Paris and a six-residence family office have exactly the same six. Only the scale changes.

**Domain.** What it covers, and how it fails

**The brief.** How instructions enter the system. Fails when an instruction is heard rather than confirmed, and the failure surfaces three weeks later.

**Sourcing.** Finding and vetting the people who do the work. Fails when a supplier is chosen for their price or their charm rather than their discretion.

**Money.** Budgets, thresholds, spend, reconciliation. Fails quietly, and it is the failure that ends careers even when nothing was stolen.

**Logistics.** Movement of people and things. Fails at handovers, always, and never in the middle.

**The residence.** The physical asset. Fails through deferred maintenance, and deferred maintenance is always cheaper today and always ruinous later.

**Staff.** The people. Fails through unclear authority, and unclear authority is the normal condition of your role.

**1.2 · What this week is really teaching**

Not tasks. Systems. A person who can book a flight is worth what a person who can book a flight is worth. A person who has a travel system that never fails, that survives their own absence, and that a replacement could pick up tomorrow, is worth six times as much and is almost never available. The test of every system in this module is the same: would it still work if you were unavailable for a week and nobody could reach you? If the answer is no, you have not built a system. You have built a dependency on yourself, and a dependency is not an asset, it is a risk, and houses eventually remove risks.

## The brief

*How an instruction is taken, and why most people take them badly*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Take a brief correctly, and confirm it in writing. Understand the six questions that must be answered before you act.

**2.1 · Where things actually go wrong**

Most operational failures in private service are not failures of execution. They are failures of the brief. Someone said "book somewhere nice for Thursday", and everyone was satisfied, and nothing was true.

**2.2 · The six questions**

Every instruction, before you move: who, what, when, where, how much, and who else knows. If any of the six is missing, the brief is incomplete, and an incomplete brief is a decision you are about to make on their behalf.

**Question.** What you are pinning down

**Who.** Exactly who is attending, travelling, being served. Not "the family". Names, and how many, and any guests, and whether the guests know each other.

**What.** The actual outcome desired, not the task requested. "Somewhere nice" is not an outcome. A quiet table, no photographs, a kitchen that will handle an allergy, is an outcome.

**When.** The times, including the ones nobody stated: when do they need to leave, not when do they need to arrive.

**Where.** The place, and the alternative, and whether the place matters at all or whether it is the outcome that matters.

**How much.** The threshold. Ask early, ask once, and never test it.

**Question.** What you are pinning down

**Who else knows.** Who else in the house, in the office, in the staff, is aware of this and needs to be. Half of all operational failures are a person who should have been told and was not.

**2.3 · The confirmation**

**DEFINITION**

The confirmation. A short written message, sent within the hour, restating the instruction as you understood it. Never a request for approval. A statement of understanding.

"Understood: dinner Thursday, eight o'clock, four people, quiet table, no photographs, the allergy noted with the kitchen, car at seven forty. I will confirm the table today." It takes forty seconds. It has saved more careers than any other habit in this profession, and it does two things at once: it catches the misunderstanding while it is still free, and it produces the written record that Week 3 taught you to keep.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Most failures are failures of the brief, not of execution. Six questions before you move: who, what, when, where, how much, who else knows. Confirm in writing, within the hour, as a statement of understanding rather than a request for approval.

## Sourcing and the black book

*How the supply side of this world actually works*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Source and vet a supplier to the standard of a private household. Build a black book that is an asset rather than a liability.

**3.1 · What a black book actually is**

**DEFINITION**

The black book. Your private, vetted, tiered list of people who deliver at this level: their names, what they are genuinely good at, what they are not, what they cost, and whether they can be trusted with a confidence.

It is the single most portable asset in this profession. It travels with you, it grows for thirty years, and it is a large part of why an experienced estate manager is paid what she is paid. And it is not the list of names. It is the fact that they answer.

**3.2 · The four tiers**

**Tier.** What it means

**Tier 1.** You have used them personally, more than once, including on a difficult day. They have never disappointed you and they have never repeated anything. You would put them in front of a principal without hesitating.

**Tier 2.** You have used them and they were good. Not yet tested under pressure. Perfectly acceptable for the ordinary work, never for the important thing.

**Tier 3.** Recommended by someone you trust, and not yet used. Useful, and not yet real. Most people's entire black book is tier 3, and they do not know it.

**Tier 4.** Found. Advertised. Impressive website. Unknown. This is not a supplier, it is a candidate.

**3.3 · Vetting, in the order that matters**

**·.** Discretion first, always. Have they worked with families of this profile, and what did they say about it when you asked? The supplier who tells you, warmly and helpfully, who else they work for, has just told you exactly what they will say about you.

**·.** Insurance and legal standing. Registered, insured, and able to show you the certificate without a delay. This is not bureaucracy: an uninsured contractor in a house is a catastrophe with a date on it.

**·.** Capacity under pressure. Not what they do on a good day. What they did the last time everything went wrong. Ask them. The answer, and the willingness to give it, is the whole interview.

**·.** References from a peer, not from a website. One conversation with another estate manager is worth more than every testimonial ever published.

**·.** Price, last. It comes last for a reason. Price is the only thing on this list you can negotiate, and the only one that is not a risk.

THE RULE OF THE BLACK BOOK

A supplier who talks about their other clients will talk about you. It does not matter how good they are, how convenient they are, or how much the principal likes them. This is a permanent disqualification, and you will be tempted to make an exception, and you must not.

**3.4 · Protecting it**

Your black book is yours, and the household's suppliers are the household's. That distinction is not always obvious and it matters enormously. A supplier you brought in remains in your book. A supplier the family has used for twenty years is not yours to take anywhere, and their contact details are household information under the Week 3 classification.

## Money

*The domain that ends careers without anyone stealing anything*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Operate a budget, a threshold and a reconciliation to a professional standard. Understand why proximity to money is the most dangerous part of the role.

**4.1 · The principle, before any technique**

**THE ONLY RULE THAT MATTERS**

You will handle a great deal of money that is not yours, and almost nobody will check. That is the entire risk. It is not theft: almost nobody in this profession steals. It is sloppiness, and rounding, and a receipt that was lost, and an expense that was never quite explained. And sloppiness with money looks exactly like dishonesty from the outside, and it is treated as though it were.

**4.2 · The threshold**

Every house has a figure above which you must ask. It is almost never written down, and almost nobody will tell you what it is. Establish it in your first week, explicitly, by asking. "What is the level above which you would want me to check with you first?" It is a professional question, it will be respected, and it is the cheapest insurance you will ever buy. And then never test it. Not once, not for a good reason, not because you were obviously right. Read the case of the two thousand euros again, in Week 4.

**4.3 · The systems**

**System.** How it is actually run

**The budget.** Annual, by category: household, staff, maintenance, travel, provisions, entertaining. Reviewed monthly against actuals, and the variance explained in one line, before anyone asks.

**System.** How it is actually run

**The card.** A household card, in the household's name, never your own. If you are ever asked to put a household expense on your personal card, it happens once, it is reimbursed within the week, and you find another way. A pattern of personal-card spending is how good people end up in conversations they cannot win.

**Receipts.** Every one. Photographed the same day, filed against the category, no exceptions for small amounts. The small amounts are where the doubt starts.

**Reconciliation.** Monthly, and you present it before it is requested. A statement nobody asked for, delivered on the second of every month, is worth more to your standing than any single thing you will do all year.

**Petty cash.** The most dangerous object in a household. Logged to the euro, counted weekly, and never mixed with your own. If you cannot account for eleven euros, you have a problem, and the problem is not the eleven euros.

**4.4 · Commissions, and the word for it**

Suppliers in this world will offer you money. Not crudely. It will be called a referral fee, a courtesy, a small thank you, an industry standard. It is sometimes even normal in their sector. You decline it, and you tell the house that it was offered. Both halves matter. Declining protects your integrity. Telling them protects you from the day they find out that it was offered and you said nothing, which is a day that arrives more often than you would expect. The moment your supplier selection is influenced by what a supplier pays you, you are no longer working for the family. You are working against them, from inside their house, and there is no coming back from that, ever, in any house, anywhere.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Establish the threshold in week one, by asking. Then never test it. Receipts the same day, reconciliation on the second of every month, delivered before it is requested. Commissions are declined and disclosed. Both. Every time.

## Logistics

*Movement, and the fact that it always breaks at the seams*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build a travel file that a stranger could execute. Run an advance, and understand the handover as the point of failure.

**5.1 · The travel file**

One document. Everything on it. Printed as well as digital, because the day it matters is the day the telephone is dead.

**Section.** What goes in it

**The movement.** Every leg, with times, and, next to each, the time they must leave rather than the time it departs. Buffers built in and not stated.

**The people.** Who is travelling, passport numbers held securely by the office, visas, expiry dates checked and not assumed. A passport within six months of expiry is a crisis waiting for a border.

**The ground.** Every car, every driver, name and telephone number, and the second option for each. This is where it fails.

**The property.** Hotel or residence, the room, the arrival standard: what is in the room before they walk in, and who put it there.

**The contacts.** One name and one number per link in the chain, and a named person at each end of every handover. Not a company. A person.

**The contingencies.** What happens if the flight is delayed. If the car does not come. If a bag is lost. Written, in advance, in one line each, so that nobody is inventing at midnight.

**5.2 · Aviation, in one page**

You do not need to be an aviation expert. You need to know what you are looking at, what the questions are, and who to call.

**Mode.** What you need to understand

**Commercial, first or business.** More common at this level than the newspapers imagine. Seat preferences recorded. Lounge and fast-track arranged. Ground crew briefed on the arrival, not on the person.

**Charter.** Priced per flight, not per seat. You are quoted for the aircraft and the routing, and the quote varies enormously between brokers for exactly the same aeroplane. Always take two quotes, always. Ask about repositioning costs, which is where a cheap quote hides.

**Empty leg.** A repositioning flight sold at a heavy discount. Genuinely useful, entirely unreliable, and it can be cancelled. It is never the plan for a meeting that matters. It can be excellent for a return with flexibility.

**Fractional and cards.** Pre-purchased hours, guaranteed availability, a fixed hourly rate. If the family already have this, learn the rules of their programme in your first week: the notice period, the peak days, the cancellation terms. This is entirely learnable and almost nobody bothers.

**5.3 · The advance**

**DEFINITION**

The advance. Going ahead of the principal to the place they are about to be, and resolving everything that would otherwise be discovered by them.

The room is checked. The temperature is set. The water is right. The route is driven, once, at the hour it will be driven, not at three in the afternoon when the road is empty. Nothing about the arrival is discovered in front of the principal.

**5.4 · The seams**

Learn this and you will never lose a day to it: logistics do not fail in the middle. They fail at the handovers. Between the car and the aircraft. Between the driver and the hotel. Between what the office booked and what the house believes. At every seam, there is a named person on both sides, and they have each other's number, and they have spoken before the day.

## The yacht

*The most expensive object you will ever be asked to handle*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand who is who on a yacht, and who you may and may not speak to. Read a charter contract, and understand the APA, which is where every mistake happens. Coordinate with a captain without ever appearing to give him an order.

**6.1 · Why this chapter exists**

If your family owns a yacht, or charters one for August, you will find yourself handling sums of money and a chain of command unlike anything else in the household. A week aboard a decent charter yacht in the Mediterranean costs more than most people earn in a year, and the person who gets the details wrong is almost never the captain. It is the person on land who booked it. You do not need to be a yacht professional. You need to know who is who, what the words mean, and where the money actually goes. That is what this chapter gives you, and it is enough.

**6.2 · Who is who, and who you speak to**

**Who.** What they do, and how you deal with them

**The captain.** Absolute authority aboard, on everything, and this is a matter of law and not of ego. He decides where the vessel goes, whether it goes at all, and who comes aboard. You never give the captain an instruction. You pass on the principal's wishes, and you ask what is possible.

**The chief stewardess.** Your real counterpart, and the person who will make your summer easy or impossible. Interior, service, provisioning, guest preferences. Send her the preference file before the guests arrive, in writing, and she will remember you for years.

**Who.** What they do, and how you deal with them

**The charter broker.** Represents the client, meaning you. Finds the vessel, negotiates the contract, holds the funds. Paid a commission by the owner's side, typically around fifteen per cent, which is normal, disclosed, and not your problem.

**The central agent.** Represents the yacht and its owner, not you. Pleasant, helpful, and on the other side of the table. Never confuse the two.

**The yacht manager.** Runs the vessel as an asset for the owner: crew, maintenance, compliance, insurance. If your family owns rather than charters, this is the person you will speak to every week.

**The purser.** On larger vessels, the person who holds the accounts. If there is a purser, the APA questions go to the purser, and everything becomes considerably easier.

**6.3 · The words you must know**

**The word.** What it actually means

**Charter fee.** The base price of the vessel and her crew for the period. This is the number people quote, and it is never the number you pay.

**APA.** Advance Provisioning Allowance. Typically twenty-five to thirty-five per cent of the charter fee, paid in advance, held by the captain, and spent on fuel, food, drink, port fees, dockage and everything else. It is the family's money, held on trust, and it is reconciled at the end. Learn this word tonight.

**MYBA agreement.** The standard charter contract in the Mediterranean. Read it. The clauses that matter are the cancellation terms, the delivery and redelivery ports, and what happens in bad weather.

**VAT.** Charged on the charter fee, and it varies enormously by country and by itinerary. It is a large number. Ask the broker to state it in writing, before you present a total to the principal.

**Delivery / redelivery.** Where the charter starts and ends. Changing it costs money, sometimes a great deal, and it is one of the most common late requests you will receive.

**Crew gratuity.** Customary, and not automatic. In the Mediterranean, five to fifteen per cent of the charter fee, at the principal's discretion, usually given to the captain to distribute. Raise it with the principal quietly, in advance, so that nobody is improvising on the last morning.

THE ONE NUMBER THAT CATCHES EVERYBODY

The principal is told the yacht costs 200,000 for the week. The real figure, with APA and VAT, is closer to 290,000 before a single tip. If you present the charter fee as the price, you have not lied, and you have set up a conversation that will go very badly. Always present the all-in figure, in writing, with the three lines separated. Charter fee. APA. VAT. Then the total.

**6.4 · The APA, and where the trouble is**

The APA is the single most misunderstood thing in yachting, and it is where your reputation will be made or lost.

**·.** It is not a fee. It is the family's money. The captain holds it and spends it on their behalf, and every euro must be accounted for at the end of the charter.

**·.** You ask for the running balance, mid-charter, politely and in writing. A good captain provides it without being asked. A captain who is vague about the APA is a captain you note in your file.

**·.** You read the final reconciliation, line by line. Fuel, berthing, provisions, ordered wine, guest requests. If something is unclear, you ask once, calmly, before the yacht leaves.

**·.** If it runs low mid-charter, the captain will ask for a top-up. This is normal and it is not a warning sign. What matters is that you saw it coming, because you asked for the balance on day three.

**·.** Anything left over is returned to the family. If nobody chases it, sometimes it is not. You chase it.

**6.5 · How you actually work with the crew**

Every rule from this programme applies aboard, and two of them apply with force.

**The rule.** Why it matters more at sea

**You never contradict the captain in front of the principal.** Not on weather, not on the itinerary, not on whether the tender can run at midnight. If you disagree, you speak to him alone, once, and then you support his decision entirely. His authority is not a preference. It is the safety of everyone aboard.

**You brief the chief stewardess in writing, early.** The preference file, the allergies, the sleeping arrangements, the drinks, the music, the children. Send it a week ahead, not on the morning of embarkation. This single habit will make you her favourite person on land.

**The rule.** Why it matters more at sea

**You never go aboard as a guest.** Even when invited, and you will be invited. You are working, you are dressed for working, and you leave the moment the family are settled. The staff member who is found sunbathing on the aft deck is the staff member nobody trusts again.

**You handle the tip conversation early and privately.** Never at the passerelle on the last morning, in front of the crew, with everybody waiting. Discuss it with the principal quietly, three days before, and have the envelope ready.

**THE SENTENCE THAT SAVES YOU**

"Captain, the principal would like to be in Portofino for dinner on Thursday. What is possible?" Never: "we need to be in Portofino by Thursday." You have just given an order to a man who is legally responsible for the vessel, and you have done it about weather you cannot see, and he will remember it for the rest of the season.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The captain has absolute authority aboard. You pass on wishes and you ask what is possible. Learn the APA. It is the family money, held by the captain, and you ask for the balance mid-charter and read the reconciliation line by line. Never present the charter fee as the price. Charter fee, APA, VAT, total. In writing, always.

## The residence

*The asset, and the maintenance that is always cheaper today*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Run a residence on a calendar rather than on emergencies. Manage a contractor without being managed by one.

**7.1 · The maintenance calendar**

A house that is run on emergencies costs three times what a house run on a calendar costs, and it is exhausting, and the exhaustion is visible, and visible strain is what Week 4 told you is being counted.

**Rhythm.** What is on it

**Weekly.** The walk-through. You, alone, every room, with a notebook. Ten minutes. It catches everything that would otherwise become a crisis in March.

**Monthly.** Systems: heating, water, alarms, cameras, vehicles, generators, pool plant. Each has an owner and a date, and the date is met.

**Quarterly.** Deep clean, gardens and grounds by season, inventory spot-check, insurance and compliance documents reviewed.

**Annual.** Insurance renewal, valuations, the inventory in full, contracts and rates renegotiated, the maintenance budget rebuilt from what actually happened rather than from what was budgeted last year.

**7.2 · Contractors in the house**

**·.** They sign something. Confidentiality, at minimum, for anyone who crosses the threshold.

This is normal, it is expected at this level, and a contractor who resists is telling you something.

**·.** They are supervised. Nobody works unaccompanied in a private residence. Nobody. Not the celebrated architect, not the twenty-year electrician, not the one the principal likes.

**·.** They arrive and leave through the agreed door, at the agreed hours. A renovation is a security breach that lasts four months, and it is the single most common way a household is compromised.

**·.** Nothing is photographed. Not for their portfolio, not for their instagram, not "just for the file". This is a written term, not a conversation.

**·.** The scope is written and the variations are written. Every project in the history of the world has grown. The ones that end badly are the ones where nobody wrote down when it grew, or who agreed.

**7.3 · The inventory**

Photographed, valued, insured, updated. Art, furniture, wine, watches, vehicles. It is tedious, it takes weeks, and the day something is damaged, lost, or claimed for, the inventory is the difference between a form and a catastrophe. It is also, quietly, the document that protects you, because the person without an inventory is the person who cannot prove what was there when they arrived.

## Staff

*Coordination without authority, which is the normal condition of your role*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Coordinate people you do not manage. Understand the two rules that decide whether the staff make you or end you.

**8.1 · The structural problem of your job**

You will very often be responsible for a household without having authority over the people in it. The housekeeper does not report to you. The driver has been there eleven years. The chef answers to the principal directly, and knows it. This is not a flaw in your position. It is your position, and the people who cannot operate inside it do not last, and they usually leave complaining that nobody respected their role.

**8.2 · The two rules**

**RULE ONE**

Credit goes down, and responsibility comes up. When it goes well, it was the team. When it goes badly, it was you, in front of the principal, without exception and without qualification, even when it was demonstrably someone else. You will do this three or four times, and the staff will notice all four, and they will decide about you permanently.

**RULE TWO**

You correct in private, once, factually, and you never repeat it to anyone. A member of staff corrected in front of another member of staff is an enemy for the rest of your time in that house, and their view of you will reach the principal by a route you cannot see and cannot answer.

**8.3 · The mechanics**

**Instrument.** What it does

**The weekly staff brief.** Fifteen minutes. What is happening this week, who is where, what is unusual. It removes ninety per cent of household friction and almost nobody does it.

**The rota.** Written, visible, and honoured. Changing someone's day off at short notice, twice, will cost you more loyalty than any gesture will buy back.

**The handover.** Between shifts, between staff, between you and your cover. Written, not spoken. Spoken handovers are how things get lost.

**The escalation line.** Everyone knows what they escalate, to whom, and how fast. A house where people do not know when to escalate is a house that finds out about problems late.

## Systems and the handover file

*The document that proves you are a professional*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build a household manual. Understand why making yourself replaceable makes you valuable.

**9.1 · The counterintuitive point of the whole week**

Most people in this profession protect their position by being the only one who knows. The passwords are in their head. The suppliers answer only to them. The system exists nowhere except in their own memory. They believe this makes them indispensable. It makes them a risk, and a house that identifies a risk removes it, calmly, at a convenient moment, with a good reference. The professional does the opposite. She writes everything down, hands it over completely, and takes three weeks of holiday during which nothing happens. And that is the person who is promoted, because she has demonstrated the only thing that actually matters at the senior level: that the house runs.

**9.2 · The household manual**

**Section.** Contents

**The house.** How everything works. Systems, codes held in the correct place, suppliers by category, the maintenance calendar, the quirks that nobody writes down and everybody discovers at midnight.

**The people.** Who does what, when, and who to call. The staff, the office, the trusted contractors, the doctor, the security contact.

**The standards.** How things are done here. The table, the beds, the flowers, the cars, the arrival. Photographs where words fail, which is often.

**The rhythms.** The week, the month, the year. School terms, the trips that happen every year, the dates that matter and are never mentioned.

**Section.** Contents

**The emergencies.** One page. What to do, who to call, in what order. Printed and on the wall of the office, not in a folder.

## When it breaks

*The escalation protocol, and the thirty seconds you get*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Escalate correctly, and fast. Deliver bad news in the format that a principal can act on.

**10.1 · The three questions, in order, in ten seconds**

**·.** Is anyone in danger? If yes, nothing else in this document matters. Emergency services, then the office, then everything else.

**·.** Is it reversible? If it is reversible, you fix it and you report it. If it is not reversible, you report it immediately, before you attempt anything at all.

**·.** Who needs to know, and how fast? Almost always: faster than feels comfortable. Bad news does not improve with age. It compounds.

**10.2 · The format**

**DEFINITION**

The escalation. Four sentences. What happened. What it affects. What I have already done. What I need from you.

That is the whole thing. Not the story. Not the reasoning. Not who is at fault, which is a conversation for a calmer day and usually never. Four sentences, in under thirty seconds, and then you stop talking and let them think.

**10.3 · The tone**

Flat. Not dramatic, not apologetic, not defensive. The person who delivers a genuine crisis in a level voice is the person who is given the next one to handle, and being given the crises is how you become senior.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Danger, then reversibility, then who needs to know. In that order, in ten seconds. Escalate in four sentences: what happened, what it affects, what I have done, what I need. Flat voice. The composure is the qualification.

**Glossary**

The brief · The instruction, completed by the six questions and confirmed in writing within the hour. The confirmation · Your written restatement of an instruction. A statement of understanding, never a request for approval. The black book · Your private, tiered, vetted list of suppliers. Its value is not the names. It is that they answer. The threshold · The spending level above which you always ask. Established in week one, never tested. Reconciliation · The monthly account of what was spent against what was budgeted, delivered before it is requested. The advance · Arriving before the principal to resolve everything they would otherwise discover. The seam · A handover between two parties. Where logistics fail, every time. Empty leg · A repositioning charter flight sold cheaply. Useful, and never the plan for something that matters. The walk-through · Ten minutes, alone, every room, weekly, with a notebook. The household manual · The written system of the house. The document that makes you replaceable, and therefore promotable. The escalation · Four sentences: what happened, what it affects, what I have done, what I need. APA · Advance Provisioning Allowance. Twenty-five to thirty-five per cent of a yacht charter fee, paid in advance, held by the captain, spent on the family's behalf, and reconciled line by line at the end. MYBA agreement · The standard Mediterranean yacht charter contract. Read the cancellation, delivery and weather clauses. The charter broker · Represents you, the client. The central agent represents the yacht. Never confuse them. The captain's authority · Absolute, aboard, in law. You pass on wishes and ask what is possible. You never instruct him.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing, before the practice.

1. Name the six domains, and give the characteristic failure of each.

2. Write the six questions of the brief. Then take this instruction and complete it: "Book somewhere nice for Thursday." 3. What is the single question that tests whether you have built a system rather than a dependency? 4. A supplier, charming and excellent, mentions in passing which other family they work for. What do you do, and why is there no exception to this? 5. A supplier offers you a fifteen per cent referral fee, which is standard in their industry. Write exactly what you say, and exactly what you then do. 6. You have been in a house for one week. Write the question you must ask about money, word for word. 7. Explain why petty cash is more dangerous than a hundred-thousand-euro renovation budget. 8. Where do logistics fail? Give three real seams from a London to Sardinia journey, and the named person on each side of each one. 9. Why does writing a complete household manual, which would allow anyone to replace you, make you more valuable rather than less? 10. The principal is quoted 200,000 for a week aboard a yacht. Explain, in three lines, what you actually present to them, and why the quoted figure is not the price. 11. What is the APA, whose money is it, and what are the three things you do about it during a charter? 12. The principal wants to be in Portofino on Thursday. Write, word for word, what you say to the captain, and explain what the wrong version would cost you. 13. Escalate this in four sentences: the renovation will miss its completion date by five weeks, and the family are due to move in.

**Further study**

**·.** Start your black book this week, honestly tiered. You will find it is almost entirely tier 3 and tier 4, and that is normal, and knowing it is the point.

**·.** Learn the vocabulary of charter aviation: repositioning, empty leg, block hours, fixed base operator. You do not need expertise. You need to not be lost in the conversation.

**·.** Read one household inventory, if you can find one. Any insurer or valuer can show you the format. It will change how you look at a room.

**·.** Take one instruction you received this week and complete it against the six questions.

Count how many were missing. It is usually four.

## How this world really treats you

*You are the door, and everybody knows it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand your position in the eyes of the supply side. Recognise the flattery for what it is.

From the moment you take a role in a wealthy household, your professional life changes in a way that nobody warns you about: you become interesting to a great many people who have never met you. Galleries. Interior designers. Charter brokers. Jewellers. Property agents. Wine merchants. Private banks. Concierge companies. Watch dealers. Every one of them wants access to your principal, and you are standing in the doorway. And so you will be treated extremely well. You will be invited to previews. You will be offered upgrades, and dinners, and a car when you need one, and a table at a restaurant that told the public it was full. It is genuinely pleasant, and it is genuinely intoxicating, and here is the sentence you must hold on to:

**THE THING TO REMEMBER, PERMANENTLY**

None of it is for you. All of it is for the door you are standing in. That is not cynicism and it is not bitterness. It is simply an accurate description of a commercial relationship, and the professionals who understand it are calm and pleasant and never confused. The ones who believe the flattery is personal are the ones who are eventually compromised, and they never see it coming, because it did not feel like a bribe. It felt like a friendship.

There is one perfectly clean version of this: a supplier treats you well because you are professional, you pay on time, you brief them properly and you do not waste their day. That is a real relationship, and it is the one you want, and it is recognisable by a simple test. Would they still take your call if you left the household tomorrow? If yes, it is real. If not, it never was.

## The commission economy

*The machinery that will be explained to you as normal*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise every form the offer takes. Know exactly what you say, and what you then do.

Nobody will ever offer you a bribe. That is not how it works, and if it were that crude, nobody would fall for it. What happens is this. Somebody explains an arrangement to you, kindly and casually, as though it were an ordinary feature of the industry. And in their industry, sometimes it genuinely is.

**What you will be told.** What it actually is

**"There is a referral fee, it is completely standard in our sector.".** A payment to you for choosing them. It is standard in some sectors, and it is still a payment to you, from someone your employer is about to pay, to influence a decision you are making with your employer's money.

**"We would take care of you, obviously.".** The same thing, unquantified, so that you cannot refuse a number that was never named.

**"Let us put your commission in the quote, the client never sees it.".** This one at least is honest. It tells you exactly who is paying: the family, without knowing.

**"Come and stay for a weekend, on us, so that you know the property.".** Sometimes entirely legitimate, and it is called a site inspection, and it is normal in travel. The test is whether your house knows about it. If you would not mention it to the principal, it is not an inspection.

**"A small thank you." A watch. A handbag. A case of wine.** A gift above a trivial value, from a supplier, to the person who selects suppliers. Whatever it is called, that is what it is.

**2.1 · What you say**

Warmly, without a lecture, without any suggestion that they have insulted you, because very often they have not and they genuinely do not understand:

**THE WORDS**

"That is kind, but I do not take commissions. I am paid by the family and that is the only side I am on. It would not be right and it would not be worth it to me." Then continue the conversation about the work as though nothing happened, because nothing did.

**2.2 · What you then do, which is the half that people skip**

You tell the household that it was offered. In one line, without drama, at the next natural moment. "The broker offered a referral fee, I declined it." People find this step unnecessary and it is the most important step there is. You did nothing wrong, so there is nothing to hide, and saying so out loud converts a private virtue into a documented one. And on the day someone else in the house is offered the same fee and takes it, you are the person who already said so.

**2.3 · The gift policy you will need**

**·.** Trivial and consumable is fine. A bottle at Christmas, chocolates, flowers. Nobody has ever been compromised by a panettone.

**·.** Anything of real value goes to the house. Declared, logged, and it belongs to the household, not to you, and if the household tells you to keep it then you keep it with a clear conscience and a written record.

**·.** Anything from a supplier you are currently selecting is declined, always. Even a small thing. Especially a small thing, because the small thing is the one that establishes the pattern.

## What a supplier is actually selling you

*And it is not the service*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read a quote correctly. Understand what you are paying for at this level.

**3.1 · The uncomfortable arithmetic**

At this level you will very often pay two or three times what something is objectively worth, and it will be the correct decision. This confuses people badly in their first year, and it makes them feel foolish, and some of them try to be clever about it and cost themselves the position. You are not buying the flowers. You are buying: certainty, availability, discretion, and a person who answers the telephone at eleven at night. Those four things are the product. The flowers are the packaging.

**3.2 · Where the money actually goes**

**You appear to be paying.** You are actually paying for for

**The car.** A driver who has been vetted, who has signed something, who will not tell anyone who was in the car, and who is there at 06:40 rather than 06:52.

**The table.** The certainty of the table. On the night. At the hour. Without a photograph. That certainty has a price and it is not the food.

**The charter.** Not the aircraft. The absence of a terminal, a queue, a photograph, and a schedule that belongs to somebody else.

**The contractor.** That they will not photograph the house, will not discuss the house, and will be gone by the day they said.

**3.3 · The one place you must be ruthless**

None of the above is a reason to be a soft buyer. The suppliers know exactly how they are treated by wealthy households, and a great many of them price accordingly. So: always two quotes, always, even when you are certain. Ask what the price would be without the elements you did not ask for. Ask what the repositioning costs. Ask what happens if it is cancelled. Every one of those questions is normal, professional, and unembarrassing, and asking them will earn you more respect from a serious supplier than accepting the first number ever will. And know the difference between the two mistakes. Being expensive is a mistake you can explain in one sentence. Being taken for a fool is a mistake that makes the house wonder what else you are not watching.

## The friend of the house

*The position you are quietly working towards*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what a supplier relationship becomes when it is done properly.

There is a thing that happens, after about three years in this profession, that nobody explains to you in advance and that is the real reward of the work. Suppliers begin to answer your calls first. Not because you pay them the most. Because you are the client who briefs properly, pays on time, never blames them in front of the family, never asks them to lie, and never makes their evening worse than it needed to be. There are very few of those, and every good supplier in a city can name them.

**WHAT THIS IS WORTH**

The table on the night everything is full. The florist who opens on a Sunday. The broker who finds an aircraft in four hours. The contractor who moves you to the front. That is the black book, and it is not a list. It is a reputation, and it takes years, and it belongs to you rather than to any house, and it is a large part of why the twelfth year of this career is paid so much better than the second.

And it is built almost entirely out of small, unglamorous decencies. You gave them the brief in writing. You told them the truth about the budget. You paid the invoice in eleven days rather than sixty. You called to say it went well. You never once made them the villain in a story you told the principal.

## The renovation

*The single most dangerous project in this profession*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand why renovations destroy careers. Know the four rules before you are inside one.

If you are given a renovation, understand what has actually happened: you have been handed the project with the highest failure rate in private service, and you have been handed it because someone senior did not want it. Renovations end careers, routinely, and for reasons that have almost nothing to do with taste.

**Why it kills people.** What it looks like

**It always overruns.** Every project, everywhere, in every century. The failure is not the overrun. The failure is that the principal learned about it late, from someone else.

**The scope grows invisibly.** A hundred small "while we are here" decisions, each individually reasonable, and none of them written down, and the total is forty per cent over budget and nobody can say when it happened.

**The money is enormous and the oversight is thin.** You will be signing off amounts that would have been a scandal in your previous life, and the person checking is you.

**The house is open for months.** Contractors, deliveries, unfamiliar people, doors propped open, photographs taken. Every Week 3 rule you learned is under pressure every single day.

**5.1 · The four rules**

**·.** Everything in writing, including the small things, especially the small things. Every variation, dated, priced, and approved by the person who is entitled to approve it. Verbal approval of a variation does not exist. It was never given, whatever anyone remembers.

**·.** The weekly report, whether or not anyone asks for it. One page. Where we are, what has changed, what it costs, what I need. Every week, on the same day, for the entire project.

This one habit is worth the whole module.

**·.** Bad news travels at full speed. The moment you know the date will slip, they know. Not when you have a solution. Not when it is confirmed. When you know.

**·.** You are not the designer. You do not have opinions about their taste, you do not steer choices towards what you would have chosen, and you never, ever say "I would have gone with the other one" to anyone at all.

## Houses I have seen

*Six operational stories*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise these before you are inside one.

**The eleven euros**

A house in Geneva. Petty cash. A house manager of four years, entirely honest, universally liked. At the audit, the cash was eleven euros short. Not eleven thousand. Eleven. She could not say why, and she was not lying, and there was nothing to find. She was gone within two months. Nobody thought she was a thief. They thought she was not in control, and in a household, those two things produce exactly the same outcome, and this is the single most misunderstood fact in this profession.

**The referral fee**

A charter broker, over eight years, paid an estate manager a quiet five per cent on every flight he booked. He booked a great many. It was, in the broker's industry, entirely normal, and it was never hidden from the broker's side. It surfaced in a routine cost review, from the broker's own accounts, in a sentence in an email. He argued, and he was not entirely wrong, that he had always chosen good aircraft, that the family had never suffered, that the prices were competitive. It made no difference at all. The moment the family understood that his selection had a second beneficiary, everything he had ever recommended was reopened. Eight years of work, all of it fine, all of it now suspect. He left the profession.

**The quote he did not take twice**

A young house manager, capable and eager, booked a charter from the first broker he called, at forty-one thousand for a routing that a second quote, obtained twenty minutes later by a colleague, priced at twenty-six. Nobody was defrauded. The first price was a real price, honestly quoted, in an opaque market. The principal never mentioned it. The family office did, once, to the principal. He was not fired for the fifteen thousand. He was reclassified in a single conversation, from someone who runs things to someone who needs checking, and he never recovered the position.

**The Sunday florist**

A different estate manager, in the same city, over six years, did nothing remarkable at all. She briefed her suppliers in writing. She paid their invoices in ten days when everyone else in the city paid in ninety. She never once blamed one of them in front of a principal, including on the occasions when it was entirely their fault. On the Saturday evening before an important lunch, an entire delivery was ruined in transit. Her florist opened his shop on the Sunday morning, alone, and rebuilt the whole thing in four hours, and would not invoice for the extra. That is what the black book actually is, and it cannot be bought, and it cannot be taken from her.

**The renovation that was fine**

A country property, an eighteen-month project, forty per cent over the original budget, and four months late. And the house manager who ran it was promoted at the end of it. Because every single week, for seventy-eight weeks, a one-page report arrived on the same day. Every variation was written, priced, and approved before the work was done. And on the day she knew the date would slip, which was five weeks before it slipped, she said so, in four sentences, with two options. The principal never learned a single thing about that project from anybody except her. That is the whole of it.

**The handbag**

An interior designer, at the end of a large and profitable project, sent the house manager a handbag. Retail, a little over four thousand euros. A thank you, and quite sincerely meant, and in her industry, unremarkable.

The house manager kept it. She did not hide it, exactly. She simply did not mention it, because mentioning it felt awkward, and it was a gift, and the project was finished. Eleven months later the same designer was proposed for the second property, and the family office asked, routinely, whether there was any reason to prefer them. And a colleague, without malice, mentioned the bag. Nothing was ever said to her. She was not accused of anything, because there was nothing to accuse her of. She simply stopped being asked for her opinion on suppliers, and then, eventually, on anything.

**Glossary**

The door · What suppliers actually want from you. Access to your principal. It is never personal, and understanding that keeps you calm. The referral fee · A payment to you for selecting a supplier. Standard in some industries, fatal in this one. Declined and disclosed. The site inspection · A legitimate visit to a property you are considering. The test: does your house know about it? The gift line · Trivial and consumable, you may keep. Anything of value goes to the house, declared and logged. The second quote · Always. Even when you are certain. Especially when you are certain. Friend of the house · The supplier relationship built over years on decency, correct briefing, and prompt payment. The real black book. The variation · Any change to an agreed scope. Written, priced, approved, before the work. A verbal variation does not exist. The weekly report · One page, same day, every week, whether or not anyone asked for it. The habit that saves renovations.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing.

1. A supplier treats you extremely well. Give the one question that tells you whether the relationship is real. 2. Write, word for word, what you say when a referral fee is offered. Then write what you do in the ten minutes afterwards. 3. Why is disclosing the offer as important as declining it? Answer in terms of what happens eleven months later. 4. A supplier sends you a handbag after a completed project. Walk through your exact actions, in order. 5. Explain why paying three times the objective price can be the correct decision, and what you are actually buying. 6. Explain the difference between being an expensive buyer and being a foolish one, and why only one of them is survivable. 7. In the case of the eleven euros: nobody believed she was a thief. Why was she gone in two months? 8. You are handed an eighteen-month renovation. Write the four rules you will operate under, before you take a single decision. 9. Why is a verbal approval of a variation worth nothing, even when the person who gave it is entirely honest? 10. Of the six cases, which failure is closest to one you could make? Do not choose a flattering answer.

## How this week is practised

*One brief, five exercises*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the sequence and why it is in this order.

**Exercise.** What it trains

**1 · Complete the brief.** Noticing what you were not told. This is the exercise most people rush, and it is the one that decides the other four.

**2 · The itinerary.** Building a document a stranger could execute in your absence.

**3 · The budget.** Operating a threshold, and presenting money without being asked.

**4 · Vetting.** Choosing a supplier for the right reasons, in the right order.

**5 · The pre-mortem, and the crisis.** Structured pessimism, and then a four-sentence escalation under time pressure.

## The brief

*Exactly as you would receive it, which is to say, badly*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read a real instruction, and notice what is missing.

**THE INSTRUCTION, AS GIVEN**

"We are going to Sardinia in August, the usual sort of thing, nine days. The Rossis are coming for part of it. Can you sort it out." That is all you are given, and it was said while they were leaving the room, and they will not be available until Thursday.

What you know, from your preference file, because you have been keeping one:

**·.** The household: the principal, his wife, three children (14, 11, 6), and a nanny who travels with them.

**·.** They have been to Sardinia in August for six consecutive years, to the same villa, on the

Costa Smeralda. The villa has been booked directly with the owner every year.

**·.** The principal flies private on the outbound and, most years, commercial on the return, because he leaves later than the family.

**·.** The wife does not like a full staff in the villa. Last year there was a cook who came at eleven and left at three, and it was mentioned, once, approvingly.

**·.** The 6 year old has a serious nut allergy. It is recorded in your file exactly, and it is not approximate.

**·.** The principal has, in each of the last two years, taken one day to Milan in the middle of the trip.

**· ·.** The Rossis have four people, and they have never been before. Nobody has told you when in August.

## Exercise one

*Complete the brief*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Produce the questions before you produce anything else.

Before you book a single thing, write the questions you must ask, and separate them into three lists. This separation is the exercise.

**List.** What goes in it

**Must ask, before I can do anything.** The things where the whole plan changes. Usually two or three. If your list here is long, you have not thought hard enough about what you can infer.

**Must ask, but not urgently.** The things you need before you commit money, but that do not block you today. Ask them together, once, in one message, not in six.

**Do not ask, infer.** What your preference file already tells you. Every question in this column that you moved into the first column is a small failure of preparation, and every item you left here that you should have asked about is a much larger one.

Submit: the three lists, and one sentence under each item in the third column saying what your evidence is.

## Exercise two

*Build the itinerary*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Produce a document a stranger could execute.

Now build the travel file. Assume the dates you were given after you asked. Assume the villa is confirmed. The test of this document is not that it is complete. It is that a competent stranger could execute it if you were unreachable. Write it for that person.

**It must contain.** Specifically

**Every leg.** Outbound and return, for the family, for the principal, for the nanny, and for the Rossis if you are handling them, and you must decide whether you are, and justify it.

**Leave times, not departure times.** For every movement. The time they walk out of the door.

**The ground.** Every car, with a named person and a number at both ends. And the second option for each.

**The arrival standard.** What is in the villa before they walk in. Who put it there. When. This is the section that separates a booking from an arrival.

**The allergy.** How it is handled, with whom, in writing, at every point where food is prepared or served. Do not be brief here. Be exactly as thorough as the situation deserves, which is very.

**The Milan day.** He has taken it twice. He has not mentioned it. Decide what you do about that, and be able to defend your decision, because there are two defensible answers and one indefensible one.

**The contingencies.** One line each. Flight delayed. Bag lost. Car does not arrive. Someone is ill. A guest extends.

The one-page version Everything above, compressed to a single page that fits in a pocket. If you cannot do this, the plan is too complicated.

## Exercise three

*The budget*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build and present a budget without being asked to.

You have not been given a budget. You have not been given a threshold. This is normal, and it is the most common condition of the work. Produce a one-page budget with the categories, the estimate for each, and the total. Then, at the foot of it, write the two sentences you will send with it.

**·.** The estimate, by category. Villa, flights, ground, provisions, the cook, activities, contingency. The contingency is a line, and it is stated, and it is usually ten per cent.

**·.** The two or three decisions that are theirs. Not a list of options. The specific places where money and preference intersect and where it would be presumption for you to choose.

**·.** The question about the threshold, written the way you would actually write it, once, professionally, without apologising for asking.

## Exercise four

*Vet a supplier*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Vet in the correct order, and reject for the correct reason.

You need a cook for the villa: eleven to three, six days a week, nine days, in a house with a child with a serious nut allergy and a family that values their privacy above almost everything. You have three candidates.

**Candidate.** What you know

**A.** Recommended by the villa owner. Has cooked in that villa for four summers. Excellent, by all accounts. Mentions, in your first call, and warmly, the name of a family he cooked for last month, whom you have heard of.

**B.** Found through an agency. Insured, contracted, will sign anything. Good but unremarkable references. Twice the price of A. Has never worked in that villa. Asks you, in the first call, what the allergy protocol is and who else will be in the kitchen.

**C.** Recommended by another estate manager you trust, who used her for two seasons and describes her as "invisible". Available. Reasonable price. Has never worked with a serious allergy and says so, immediately, without being asked.

Choose. Write your reasoning in one paragraph, in the order the module gave you, and state explicitly what disqualified the others and what risk you are accepting with the one you chose. There is a right answer here, and there is a defensible second answer, and there is one answer that would end you.

## Exercise five

*The pre-mortem, and then the day it goes wrong*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Predict the failure, then handle the one you did not predict.

**7.1 · The pre-mortem**

Twenty minutes. The trip has failed. Write down what the failure was, in the past tense, with names and times. Find at least five, and for each one, name the seam it happened at, and write the second option.

**7.2 · The day**

DAY FOUR, 18:40

The Rossis land in ninety minutes. The car company has just told you that the second vehicle, the one for their luggage and their two children, will not come, and that they have nothing else, and it is August on the Costa Smeralda. The principal is at dinner. His wife is at the villa with three children. You are two hours from the airport by road.

Write, in order and with times: what you do in the first five minutes, who you call, in what order, and the exact four sentences you send to the principal, and when you send them.

## The debrief

*Read only after all five exercises are written*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Compare your judgement to the professional standard.

**Exercise one · the brief**

There are exactly two things that genuinely block you: the dates, and whether the Rossis are your responsibility to move or their own. Everything else can wait or be inferred. If your "must ask now" list had six items on it, you have handed the principal a questionnaire, and a questionnaire is six withdrawals from an account that is nearly empty. And there is one item that belongs in "do not ask" and that almost everyone puts in the wrong column: the villa. Six consecutive years, same villa, booked direct. That is not a question. That is a standing decision, and asking about it tells them you have not been paying attention.

**Exercise two · the itinerary**

The allergy is the whole exercise. If you wrote two lines about it, you failed, and the failure is not a marking failure. It is written to the cook, to the villa, to the restaurants, to the nanny, in writing, confirmed in writing, before departure, in Italian as well as English, and it is checked on arrival, in person, by you. A serious nut allergy in a foreign country in August is the single most dangerous fact in this brief and everything else in it is logistics. The Milan day: he has taken it twice, unprompted, and has never mentioned it. You do not book it, because you have no instruction and it is his day. You hold it: you know the flights, you know the car, you know what a day in Milan would cost and how late he could leave and still be back. And you say nothing at all, and if he mentions it on day three, it is already arranged. That is the entire method of Week 4, applied to a real brief.

**Exercise three · the budget**

The mistake here is presenting options. You are not a travel agent. You present a plan, a number, a contingency, and the two or three decisions that are genuinely theirs, and nothing else. The threshold question is asked plainly, once, and it is never apologised for.

**Exercise four · the supplier**

A is disqualified, immediately and permanently, and it does not matter that he is excellent, that he knows the villa, that he is cheaper, or that the villa owner recommended him. He named a family in his first call with a stranger. He will name yours. This is the rule from Module 1 and it is the rule you will be tempted to break, and the fact that he is the obviously best cook is precisely what makes it a test. C is the right answer. Vetted by a peer, described as invisible, and she volunteered her own weakness without being asked, which is the single most reliable indicator of professional honesty there is. You accept the allergy risk and you manage it: written protocol, a briefing before departure, no nuts in the villa at all, and the nanny and the cook both confirm it in writing. B is the defensible second answer, and if you chose B and said "I am paying double for a lower risk on the one thing that could kill a child", that is a professional answer and I would accept it. If you chose A, read Week 3 again, and then read it a third time.

**Exercise five · the day**

First five minutes: you do not call the principal. You solve. Second car company from your black book, then the villa owner, then the hotel concierge in Porto Cervo, then, if all of that fails, the villa's own vehicle with a driver you have already vetted, and, if all of that fails, you go yourself and you send the nanny in the second car. You message the principal only once you have a solution or once you are certain there is not one, and you do it before the Rossis land, never after. Four sentences. "The second car for the Rossis has fallen through. I have a replacement from Olbia arriving at 20:10, ten minutes after they land. It costs three hundred more. Nothing is needed from you." And notice the last sentence. Nothing is needed from you. That is what you were hired to be able to say.

## Marking scheme

*What I am looking for*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know how this is assessed.

**Criterion.** What earns it

**The brief.** Two blocking questions, not six. And the villa in the right column.

**The allergy.** Treated as the most serious thing in the document, in writing, in two languages, confirmed by name.

**Executability.** Could a stranger run your itinerary if you vanished? I will read it as though you had.

**The seams.** Named people, both sides, every handover, with second options. Missing seams are the most common failure in this exercise.

**The supplier.** Choosing C, or defending B properly. Choosing A is an automatic fail, and I will tell you so plainly.

**The escalation.** Four sentences, after the solution, before the landing, ending with what is needed from them, which is nothing.

## What you are producing

*Eight instruments, not an essay*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what the toolkit is for.

The toolkit is the set of documents you walk into a household with on day one. Not filled in: blank, and ready. It is what allows you to be operational in a strange house in a week rather than in three months. And it is the thing that, in an interview, ends the conversation about whether you know what you are doing.

## The eight instruments

*One page each, no more*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build each one.

**Instrument.** What it does

**1 · The brief sheet.** The six questions, as a form. Who, what, when, where, how much, who else knows. Plus the confirmation message, pre-written, that you send within the hour.

**2 · The travel file.** Your itinerary format. Legs, leave times, ground, arrival standard, contacts by name at both ends of every seam, contingencies, and the one-page pocket version.

**3 · The vendor card.** One card per supplier. Name, category, tier, what they are excellent at, what they are not, price, insurance status, who introduced them, the date you last used them, and one line on how they behaved when something went wrong.

**4 · The expense log.** Date, category, amount, purpose, receipt held yes or no. And the monthly reconciliation format that you deliver on the second of every month without being asked.

**5 · The maintenance calendar.** Weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual. Each item with an owner and a date. Blank, and ready for any house.

**6 · The household manual.** The contents page, at least, and the structure. The house, the people, the standards, the rhythms, the emergencies. This is the one that takes a year to fill and ten minutes to structure.

**7 · The pre-mortem checklist.** Your questions. Single points of failure, unchecked assumptions, seams. Five to eight lines.

**8 · The escalation card.** The three questions, in order. The four-sentence format. And, written out in full, the sentences you will say when you have made a mistake. Written now, calmly, so that you are not composing them on the worst morning of your year.

## What makes a template usable

*And what makes one decorative*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build for a tired person, in a strange house, at seven in the morning.

**·.** One page. Anything longer will not be used, and a template that is not used is worse than no template, because it gave you the feeling of being prepared.

**· ·.** Fields, not prose. If your template contains paragraphs, it is an essay wearing a costume. No branding, no design, no colour. It is a working document. If you have spent an evening on the typography, you have spent an evening avoiding the work.

**·.** Printable. The day the systems fail is the day you need the file, and it will be the day the systems fail.

**·.** Filled in once, as a test, with a real example. A template you have never used is a guess about your own future behaviour.

## How it will be judged

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the standard.

**What I am looking for.** What that means

**Usability.** Could you find the right line in fifteen seconds, in a strange house, tired? That is the only real test.

**The vendor card.** I will look here first. If it does not have a tier and a line about how they behaved when something went wrong, you have built an address book and called it a black book.

**The expense log.** I will look here second. If the reconciliation is not scheduled, and dated, and delivered without being asked, you have not understood Chapter IV.

**The escalation card.** If the three sentences for your own mistake are not written out in full, in advance, you have not understood why they need to be.

**Restraint.** Eight pages. If you have sent me thirty, you have misunderstood the entire week.

## The American annex

*Running an American household, legally*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Operate an American household payroll to a professional standard, and know the two hour rules that are most often confused.

**A5.1 · Payroll, in one page**

The day you run the household rather than sit inside it, this becomes yours, and it is the domain in which good people are quietly destroyed.

**W-2, not 1099.** Household workers are employees. Misclassification is the most common and most expensive error made in private homes, and it is made by families who believe they are being helpful.

**Schedule H.** Household employment taxes are reported on Schedule H with the family's own return. Social Security and Medicare are owed on cash wages above three thousand dollars for 2026.

**Form I-9.** Every new hire completes one within three business days of starting. No exceptions, and not "when things calm down".

**Workers' compensation.** Required for household employees in many states, optional in others, and the threshold is a state question. Confirm it for your state before anyone is hired, not after somebody falls off a ladder.

**A5.2 · Hours, and the live-in trap**

Domestic workers are covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act. Two points matter, and they are routinely confused, including by the families themselves.

Live-in domestic employees are exempt from federal overtime. They are not exempt from the minimum wage, and their hours must still be recorded. A live-in schedule does not turn every hour into a working hour: time genuinely free of all duties, sleeping, eating, private pursuits, is not paid. It must be genuinely free, and which hours those are should be agreed in writing before the first night.

Your state may be stricter than the federal floor, and several are. California pays overtime to a personal attendant after nine hours in a day or forty-five in a week. Sixteen states now have a Domestic Workers Bill of Rights, New York first in 2010, California in 2013, Washington in 2026, adding written agreements, rest periods, privacy protections and anti-retaliation rules. Find out this week whether you live in one of them.

**A5.3 · Aviation and water, American variations**

**Part 91 and Part 135.** An aircraft the family owns and operates for itself sits under Part 91. A charter sold to the public sits under Part 135, with a different regulatory burden and different rules about who may be aboard and who may pay. When a broker quotes you, know which one you are buying.

**Chartering in United States waters.** A foreign-flagged yacht generally may not charter in US waters, which is why the Florida and New England fleet looks nothing like the Mediterranean one. Your contract will often be an AYCA agreement rather than the MYBA form described above.

**Gratuity.** Five to fifteen per cent is the Mediterranean convention. In the Caribbean and the United States the customary range runs higher, commonly fifteen to twenty per cent. Ask the broker what is normal for that fleet, in writing, before you put a figure in front of the principal.
