## How this market actually works

*And why the job boards are the last place to look*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the structure of a market that is mostly invisible. Stop applying, and start being introduced.

**1.1 · The structure**

The private service market has a shape that almost nobody explains to newcomers, and misunderstanding it is why capable people spend a year applying to advertisements and receiving nothing at all.

**Layer.** What it is, and how a role is filled there

**The advertised market.** What you can see. Agency listings, a handful of job boards, the occasional direct posting. Real, and it is the smallest layer, and it is the most competitive because everyone can see it.

**The agency market.** Filled from a list that already exists. The agency does not begin looking when the family calls: it opens a folder of people it already knows. Being in the folder, before the role exists, is the entire game.

**The referral market.** A principal asks another principal. An estate manager asks a former colleague. A butler recommends a friend. This is where a great many of the best roles go, and none of them are ever seen by anyone outside a very small circle.

**The invisible market.** A role that is created because a specific person became available. It is not filled. It is written around someone. It is the most senior tier and it will apply to you in about eight years.

THE SENTENCE THAT REFRAMES THE WEEK

In this market, you are not looking for a job. You are making sure that when your name is said in a room you are not in, the right thing is said about you. Everything in this module is a method for making that happen deliberately rather than by luck.

**1.2 · Why the advertised market treats you badly**

An agency listing at this level receives an enormous number of applications, and the great majority are from people with no relevant experience who have read the salary. Your carefully written letter arrives in that pile. This is not unfair. It is simply arithmetic, and no amount of care in the letter changes the arithmetic. The same letter, sent by an agency consultant to a family with the sentence "I know her, she is good", is not in a pile at all. Same person. Same letter. Entirely different market. That sentence is what you spend this week learning how to earn.

## The map

*Where the roles physically are, and when*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know the geography of the market and its seasons. Choose a market deliberately rather than by accident of birth.

**2.1 · The centres**

**Market.** What you need to know

**London.** The deepest private household market in Europe, and the most professional agency infrastructure. Formal, reference-driven, and slow to trust an outsider. Roles range from a live-out head butler or house manager in a townhouse at around sixty-five to ninety thousand pounds, upwards.

**Monaco and the Riviera.** Concentrated, seasonal, extremely relationship-driven. Many roles are never advertised anywhere at all. French and English are effectively mandatory, Italian and Russian are advantages. Villa and estate roles in Antibes and Saint-Tropez run through the summer and are frequently packaged with housing.

**Switzerland.** Discreet, stable, well paid, and closed. Geneva, Zurich, Gstaad, Verbier. Chalet and guest-relations roles in season are a legitimate way in and they climb quickly.

**The United States.** The largest market by volume. New York, Palm Beach, the Hamptons, Los Angeles, Aspen, and increasingly the tech families in California. An estate manager in New York is commonly around two hundred thousand dollars, and multi-residence estate management with a large team can reach three hundred and fifty thousand as a package. Visa status is the whole question, and you must answer it honestly before you spend a year on it.

**The Gulf.** Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Riyadh, Doha. Tax free, frequently live-in, often royal or quasi-royal households. Salaries are commonly stated on request, and the packages can be very large. VVIP experience is usually required, so this is rarely a first rung, and the cultural expectations must be studied seriously and not assumed.

**Market.** What you need to know

**Yachts and aviation.** Its own market, with its own rules, its own certifications, and its own season. A genuinely excellent way in for someone with no household experience, it travels, and it is physically demanding in a way that people underestimate.

**2.2 · The seasons**

This market breathes, and knowing when it inhales is worth more than any single piece of advice in this module.

**Period.** What is happening

**January and February.** Hiring for the year. Families reorganise after the holidays. Agencies are busy and receptive. This is the single best moment to make yourself known.

**March to May.** Recruitment for the summer season. Villas, chalets closing, yachts crewing up. If you want a summer role, you are already late in May.

**June to August.** The market is running, not hiring. Nobody is reading letters. Use it to build, to learn, and to work a season.

**September and October.** The second window. Post-summer reorganisation, and the winter season is being staffed. Nearly as good as January.

**November and December.** Quiet, and the exception is short-term holiday cover, which is a small and genuinely useful door.

## The ladder

*And the rung you can actually reach*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Place yourself honestly on the ladder. Understand what each rung pays, and what it leads to.

**3.1 · The honest conversation**

These roles form a ladder, not a menu. The single most common error made by ambitious, capable people entering this profession is to apply for the rung they want rather than the rung they can be taken on. It costs them a year, and it is entirely avoidable, and I would rather you were disappointed today than in March.

**Role.** What it pays, and where it sits

**Guest relations, villa and chalet host.** Fifty-five to a hundred and twenty thousand and upwards. The honest first rung if you are new to private service. It is not a lesser role: it is the one that teaches you the codes in a real house, and it climbs fast.

**Private concierge, lifestyle manager.** Seventy to a hundred and fifty thousand and upwards. Reachable directly from luxury hospitality, aviation, or private assistance. The purest expression of the craft in this programme.

**Private or executive PA to a UHNW principal.** Up to around two hundred and fifty thousand at the top of the market. A strong first private role if you come from a serious assistant background.

**Estate or house manager.** Median around a hundred and eighty-five thousand, with the top quartile above two hundred and sixty thousand. A senior step, after several years inside a household. Not an entry point, and anyone who tells you otherwise is selling you something.

**Chief of staff.** A hundred and fifty to three hundred and fifty thousand and upwards. The summit of a private-service career. Built on years of trust, not on eight weeks of study. We show you the path honestly, and the path is long.

**Role.** What it pays, and where it sits

**Yacht and private aviation interior.** Fifty to a hundred and thirty thousand and upwards. An excellent door, and it travels, and the season structure is unlike anything on land.

**THE RULE**

Choose the rung you can reach, and be impeccable on it. The person who is impeccable in a chalet for one season is offered a household. The person who spends that season applying for estate manager roles she will not be given is, at the end of it, exactly where she started, and a year older, and considerably more discouraged.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The market has four layers, and only the smallest one is visible to you. It has seasons. January and September are the windows. Know them and use them. It has a ladder. Choose the rung you can reach and be flawless on it.

## The agencies

*How they actually work, and how to be in the folder*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the agency's incentives, which are not yours. Register in a way that puts you in the folder rather than in the pile.

**4.1 · What an agency is, commercially**

An agency is paid by the family, usually a percentage of the first year's salary, and only when a placement is made. Understand what that means about their behaviour, because it explains everything that will otherwise frustrate you.

**·.** They do not work for you. They are pleasant to you, and they are not your advocate.

Their client is the family, and their loyalty follows their invoice, and this is entirely proper and you should never resent it.

**·.** Their reputation is their only asset. Which means they will not put forward anyone they are not confident about. One bad placement with a good family costs them the family for a decade.

**·.** They are not looking when you write to them. They are filing. The role you are eventually presented for probably did not exist when you first made contact, and that is precisely why early contact matters.

**·.** They will ghost you, routinely, and it is not personal. A consultant is holding forty candidates and eleven roles. Silence is not a judgement. It is arithmetic.

**4.2 · The serious agencies, and how to tell**

The market contains excellent firms and a number of unserious ones. The specialist houses that recur across London, Paris, Monaco, New York and Dubai include Morgan and Mallet International, Silver Swan Recruitment, Private Staff Group, Rose's Agency, Harper Associates, Luxury Assets, Exclusive Household Staff, and Achieve Hospitality. That is not an endorsement and it is not a list of contacts: it is a starting point for your own research, and doing that research is part of this week.

A serious agency will: interview you properly, take up your references before presenting you, tell you the truth about your rung, and never ask you for money. Hold that last one. It reappears in Chapter IX.

**4.3 · How to register so that you are remembered**

**Most people.** What you will do

**Send a CV to a general inbox with the subject line "Application".** Write to a named consultant, having found out what that consultant actually recruits for, and say so in the first line.

**Say what they want.** Say what you are, precisely, and what rung you are on, and be the rare candidate who is realistic. Consultants remember realism because they see almost none of it.

**Apply to a specific role.** Ask to be registered, and say that you are not expecting anything immediately, and mean it. This single sentence changes how the conversation feels.

**Follow up anxiously, or never.** Follow up once, at ninety days, with something real: a course completed, a season worked, a language reached. Not "just checking in", which is a message that asks them for something and gives them nothing.

## The direct channel

*The family office, and the introduction that is not cold*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what a family office is and how it hires. Approach the direct channel without being an intrusion.

**DEFINITION**

The family office. The private organisation that manages the wealth, investments, legal affairs, philanthropy, property and often the personal staffing of a single very wealthy family. A single-family office serves one family; a multi-family office serves several.

This is the entity that will very often employ you, pay you, and hold your contract. The principal is not your employer in any administrative sense. The family office is, and the people in it are the people whose opinion of you decides your renewal.

**5.1 · How they hire**

Quietly, through people they already trust, and with an overwhelming preference for someone who has been vouched for. A family office receiving an unsolicited approach from a stranger is receiving, from their point of view, a small security question. Understand that, and approach accordingly, and never take the silence personally.

**5.2 · Where you are actually visible to them**

**· ·.** Through the agencies they use. Which is why Chapter IV comes first. Through the staff they already employ. The single most powerful channel in this profession, and the one you build in Chapter VI.

**·.** Through the suppliers who serve them. The florist, the private chef, the charter broker, the villa manager. These people know who is hiring, months before anybody else, and almost nobody in your position thinks to be useful to them.

**·.** Through a season worked impeccably. A chalet, a villa, a yacht. Every one of those houses is full of guests who have households of their own.

## Building a network from nothing

*In ninety days, starting with nobody*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build a real professional network from zero. Understand why giving comes before asking, always.

**6.1 · The principle**

THE ONLY RULE OF NETWORKING IN THIS PROFESSION

You give first, you give without expectation, and you ask for nothing for a long time. This is not a moral position. It is the only thing that works, because everybody in this world can recognise a person who wants something within ninety seconds, and they have all been approached a hundred times.

**6.2 · The ninety-day plan**

**Phase.** What you actually do

**Days 1 to 30 · Map.** You do not contact anybody. You build the map: sixty names, real people, in four categories. Agencies and named consultants. People currently doing the job you want. Suppliers who serve this world. Adjacent professionals: private chefs, chalet managers, charter brokers, villa agents.

**Days 31 to 60 · Appear.** You become visible in the places these people already are. Professional associations. Industry events. Serious online groups, where you contribute and do not sell. You answer questions, you never ask for anything, and you let people see that you are competent and quiet.

**Days 61 to 90 · Reach.** Now you write. Fifteen letters, each one individual, each one giving something and asking for almost nothing. Chapter VII is the method, and it is precise, and it works.

**Day 91 onwards · Hold.** A network that is not maintained is a list. Two contacts a week, forever. A useful article. A congratulation that is real. A supplier recommendation that costs you nothing and helps them.

**6.3 · The people to actually cultivate**

New entrants chase principals, and principals are unreachable and would not know what to do with them anyway. The people who will actually change your career are two rungs above you, not twenty. A house manager in Geneva. A chalet manager in Verbier. A private chef who works for three families. A charter broker. These people hear about roles first, they are asked "do you know anyone", they are pleased to be useful, and they are entirely reachable. Nobody ever writes to them. Write to them.

## Cold outreach that becomes a warm

*introduction*

The method, precisely

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Write an approach that is answered. Understand why every element of the standard approach fails.

**7.1 · Why the normal message fails**

The normal message is about the sender. It says who they are, what they want, and what they are looking for, and it asks the recipient for time, advice, or a role. It is entirely reasonable, and it goes unanswered, and the sender concludes that this world is closed. It is not closed. It is simply full of people asking for things.

**7.2 · The structure that works**

**Element.** What it does

**The specific first line.** You demonstrate, in one sentence, that you know exactly who they are and that this message could not have been sent to anyone else. Not flattery. A fact. Generic openings are deleted before the second line.

**The honest position.** One sentence, entirely without inflation, on what you are and where you are. Nobody at this level is fooled by inflation, and everybody is disarmed by accuracy.

**The gift.** Something useful to them, for nothing. A supplier they would want to know about. A candidate for a role you saw that you cannot fill yourself. A piece of information from your own world that is genuinely of use in theirs. If you cannot find a gift, you are not ready to send the message.

**The small ask, or none at all.** Not a job. Not an introduction. Not a coffee, which is an hour of their life. At most: one specific question that takes them ninety seconds to answer and that flatters their expertise rather than their ego.

**Element.** What it does

**The exit.** You make it effortless not to reply. "If it is not a good moment, please ignore this entirely." It sounds like a small politeness. It is the line that most often produces the reply.

**7.3 · Then you wait, and you do the hard part**

You do not follow up in a week. You wait. And if they answered, however briefly, you write again in three months with something else that is useful to them and no ask at all. And the third time, six months later, you may ask, and you will get an answer, because by then you are not a stranger asking for something. You are a person they know, who has never once cost them anything. That is what a warm introduction actually is, and it takes nine months, and there is no faster version of it that works.

## Your reputation

*How this world actually checks you*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand that the reference is the whole market. Build a reputation that speaks for you before you enter a room.

**8.1 · How a hire really happens**

A family will interview you. They will check your references, and they will do it properly. And then they will do the thing that decides it, and you will never see it happen. They will ask someone they trust about you. Not a referee you named. Someone they already know, who might know you. And that conversation, which lasts about ninety seconds, is worth more than every document you have ever produced.

**8.2 · What this means about how you behave, everywhere, always**

**·.** You never leave a house badly. Not the house that treated you unfairly. Not the house you were right about. This world is very small, and the person you were entitled to be angry with will be asked about you, and they will remember.

**·.** You never speak badly of a former employer. Ever, anywhere, to anyone, including in an interview where you are invited to and it would be entirely justified. The person listening is not learning about them. They are learning what you will say about them.

**·.** You are decent to people who cannot help you. The junior housekeeper. The driver. The agency assistant who is twenty-three. They talk to each other, they will all be senior in ten years, and this world is smaller than you can currently imagine.

**·.** You do what you said you would do. The single most reliable reputation in this market belongs to the person whose word is boringly, unremarkably good.

## The traps of this market

*Read this chapter twice*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise every common exploitation of new entrants. Know what a legitimate process looks like, so that the illegitimate one is obvious.

This profession attracts a certain number of people who make their living from the hopes of newcomers. You will meet them. They are pleasant and they are convincing and they will find you within weeks of your first application.

**The trap.** How to recognise it, and what to do

**The agency that charges you.** A legitimate agency is paid by the family. It never charges the candidate a registration fee, a placement fee, a "priority listing" or a mandatory training. If you are asked for money to be represented, you walk away, and you tell other people.

**The unpaid trial that never ends.** A paid trial day is normal and reasonable. Two unpaid weeks "to see how you fit" is not a trial. It is free labour, and it does not end in a role.

**The training that promises placement.** Anyone guaranteeing you a job at the end of a course is either lying or has not read the law in their country. A school teaches. A school cannot hire you. This one applies to us too, and I want you to hear it from me: Maison Amarine does not place you, and would be lying to you if it said otherwise.

**The role with no name.** A family that will not tell an agency who they are, or a "principal" who approaches you directly with no verifiable identity. Discretion is normal at this level. Total anonymity, before any contract, is not discretion. It is a warning.

**The contract that does not exist.** Cash, no contract, no papers, "we prefer to keep it simple". It is simple until the day you are dismissed, or injured, or accused of something, and then you have nothing at all, and you are alone in a foreign country.

**The role that is not a role.** Especially in certain markets, and I will say this plainly because nobody else will: if the interview process is asking about your appearance more than your competence, if the role is vague, if the salary makes no sense for the work described, leave. Immediately, without politeness, and without answering the next message.

THE RULE THAT COVERS ALL SIX

If you cannot verify who is hiring you, what you will be doing, what you will be paid, and what your contract says, you are not being offered a role. And no opportunity, at any salary, is worth what those six situations cost the people who accepted them.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

A real agency is paid by the family, never by you. There is no exception to this. No school can promise you a placement. Including this one, and I would rather you heard it from me. If you cannot verify the family, the role, the salary and the contract, there is no role.

**Glossary**

The hidden market · The referral and invisible layers, where most good roles are actually filled, and which you cannot see. The folder · The agency's existing list of known candidates. Being in it before a role exists is the whole game. Family office · The private organisation managing a family's wealth, property and staffing. Very often your legal employer. Single-family office · Serves one family. Multi-family office: serves several. The rung · The role you can realistically be taken on today, as opposed to the one you want. The season · The market's hiring rhythm. January and September are the two windows. The gift · The useful thing you give a stranger before you ever ask them for anything. The core of all outreach that works. The warm introduction · What cold outreach becomes after nine months of giving and not asking. The back channel · The unnamed person a family asks about you. It decides the hire, and you will never see it.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing, before the practice.

1. Name the four layers of this market and explain why the one you can see is the worst one to fight in. 2. It is the fifteenth of June. You want a role. What does the season tell you about what you should be doing this month, and what you should not? 3. Place yourself honestly on the ladder. Name the rung, and name the evidence for it. Then name the rung you were tempted to write instead. 4. Explain, in one paragraph, why an agency is pleasant to you and is not on your side, and why you should never resent this. 5. What are the four things a serious agency does, and the one thing it never does?

6. What is a family office, and why does the person who signs your contract matter to how you behave? 7. Name the four people you should be cultivating who are two rungs above you, not twenty. Give real names if you can. 8. Write a cold outreach message of under a hundred and twenty words, containing a specific first line, an honest position, a real gift, and an exit. Then delete the ask. 9. Why is a warm introduction a nine-month process, and what is the faster version, and why does the faster version not work? 10. You are asked for four hundred euros to be added to an agency's priority list. Write your reply, and then write what you do next.

**Further study**

**·.** Research five agencies properly this week. Not their homepage. Who their consultants are, what they specialise in, which markets they actually serve, and what they say they require. Then write down which one is genuinely for you, and which three are not.

**·.** Find ten people currently doing the job you want, and read everything public they have written. You are not contacting them yet. You are learning what the job sounds like from inside.

**·.** Learn the vocabulary of the family office: single family office, multi-family office, principal, chief of staff, private trustee. You will be in a conversation where these words are used within your first month.

**·.** Ask yourself, honestly, whether you would hire you. Write the answer down. This is the exercise that produces the market entry plan.

## What a consultant actually sees

*Eight seconds, and what happens in them*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand how your application is really read. Write for the reader who exists rather than the one you imagine.

You imagine a consultant reading your letter carefully, weighing your experience, considering your potential. What actually happens is this. She has forty-one unread applications, eleven live roles, a family in Monaco who wants a decision today, and a candidate who has just withdrawn from a placement she has been working on for three weeks. Your letter is opened at 18:40 and it gets eight seconds. In those eight seconds she is answering three questions, in this order, and nothing else:

**·.** Is this person placeable at all? Meaning: is there any role, out of my eleven, that a family would take them for. If no, she stops here, and it is not a judgement of you, it is a judgement of the eleven.

**·.** Would I be embarrassed to put them in front of a family? This is the question that kills.

Presentation, spelling, tone, inflation, a photograph that was a mistake, an email address from 2009. It is not fair and it is not going to change.

**·.** Do I already know this person? If yes, the previous two questions are answered much more generously. That is the entire reason for this week.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOUR LETTER

It is not read. It is scanned, at speed, by someone tired, for reasons to stop. So: no paragraph over four lines. The rung you are on, stated in the first sentence. No inflation, because inflation is the fastest way to be dismissed by someone who has seen ten thousand CVs. And nothing, anywhere, that would make her hesitate to attach your name to hers.

## Why they never reply

*And why it is not what you think*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Stop interpreting silence. Understand the arithmetic that produces it.

You will write to agencies and you will hear nothing. Not a rejection. Nothing at all. And you will decide, because everybody decides this, that you are not good enough, or that this world is closed to people like you. Here is what is actually happening. A consultant is not paid to reply to you. She is paid when a family signs. Every hour spent replying to candidates she cannot currently place is an hour not spent on the eleven roles that will feed her family this quarter. It is not rudeness, though it feels exactly like rudeness. It is arithmetic, and it is the same arithmetic in every agency in every city, and no amount of hurt on your part changes it.

**2.1 · So what actually works**

**Instead of.** Do this

**Following up in a week, anxiously.** Nothing at all for ninety days. Silence is not a door closing. It is a door that has not opened yet.

**"Just checking in.".** A follow-up that contains something new and real: a season worked, a language reached, a course completed, a reference gained. You are not asking. You are updating a file that she is keeping.

**Writing to the general inbox.** Writing to a named consultant, about the market she actually covers, having done the research. Ninety per cent of candidates cannot be bothered to do this, and she knows it.

**Applying to a role.** Asking to be registered, and saying, in one line, that you are not expecting anything immediately. She has not heard that sentence in a year.

## The reference economy

*The currency of this entire profession*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand that you are building references from your first day, forever. Handle the reference you cannot use.

In most industries a reference is a formality. In this one it is the product. A family is about to give a stranger the keys to their home, their movements and their children. There is no qualification on earth that answers that. The only thing that answers it is another person, whom they trust, saying that you are safe.

**3.1 · What this changes about your entire career**

**·.** Every house is a reference, including the bad one. Especially the bad one, because the way you left it is what will be described.

**·.** Every colleague is a reference. The housekeeper you were short with in a difficult week will be somewhere else in five years, and someone will ask her.

**·.** Every supplier is a reference. The florist knows six households. When one of them asks whether he knows anyone, he does.

**·.** Two years is the unit. A CV of eight-month positions, however good the reasons, reads as a person who does not last, and there is no explaining your way out of it in eight seconds.

**3.2 · The reference you cannot use**

You will very likely have a house you cannot name. An NDA that forbids it. A family whose identity is not yours to give. This is normal at the top of this profession, and it is not the obstacle you think it is, and here is how it is handled.

**THE FORMULATION**

"I worked for a private family in [city or region], for [duration], with responsibility for [functions]. I am bound by a confidentiality agreement and I am not able to give their name. My reference for that period is available through [the family office, the agency, a named senior colleague who is permitted to speak]." Then you stop. You do not hint. You do not smile as though you might say more. You do not offer a clue about who it was.

And understand what has just happened in the room. You have said, without saying it, that you were trusted by a family who required an NDA, and that you are still keeping it, in the room, to your own commercial disadvantage. For the right employer, that is not a gap in your file. That is the single most persuasive thing you could possibly have done. The wrong employer will push. That is information too, and it is free.

## The room you are not in

*What is actually asked about you*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the question that decides your hire.

Somewhere, a principal turns to someone he trusts, and he asks about you. It lasts ninety seconds. It is not recorded, you will never know it happened, and it is worth more than your entire application. And here is what is asked. Not "is she good at the job". They assume that, or you would not have reached this room.

**The question.** What it is really testing

**"What is she like?".** Whether you are pleasant to have in a house every day for four years. An enormous number of technically excellent people fail here.

**"Would you have her back?".** The only reference question that matters. Any hesitation, of even half a second, is the answer.

**"Does she talk?".** The Week 3 question, and it is asked about you by people who have never met you, on the basis of what they have heard.

**"Is she solid?".** Meaning: will she still be here in three years. Meaning: is she dramatic, is she fragile, does she create weather.

WHAT YOU ARE ACTUALLY BUILDING, ALL THE TIME

You are building the answer to a question you will never hear, asked by a person you have never met, to somebody who worked beside you five years ago. That is the whole market. Everything else is paperwork.

## The years nobody warns you about

*The honest version of the timeline*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Have a realistic expectation of the first three years. Understand why most people leave, and it is not the work.

I would rather you were disappointed by this page than by your first eighteen months.

**Period.** What it is actually like

**Months 0 to 6.** You are applying and hearing very little. This is the phase where most people conclude that the market is closed and stop. It is not closed. You are simply not yet in anybody's folder, and you have not yet been vouched for by anyone, and both of those take exactly this long.

**The first role.** Very often not the one you wanted. Frequently a season, a villa, a chalet, a maternity cover, a temporary role over a holiday. Take it, and be flawless in it. Every single person in this profession has one of these at the bottom of their CV and none of them mention it.

**Years 1 to 2.** You are learning that you knew nothing, which is uncomfortable and entirely normal. Your value here is not your competence. It is your reliability and your discretion, and they are being measured every day.

**Year 2.** The turn. Somebody says your name in a room. You will not know it happened. But the second role is easier than the first by an order of magnitude, and the third is offered to you without your applying at all.

**Years 5 to 10.** You are now inside the referral market, which means you have stopped applying for work, and work has begun to be brought to you. This is the entire objective, and it is reached by exactly one route, which is the boring one.

And the reason people leave is almost never the work, and almost never the money. It is the first six months of silence. They are talented, and they are ready, and nobody answers, and they conclude that it was not for them. It was for them. They stopped four months too early.

## Houses I have seen

*Six ways in*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise how doors actually open.

**The chalet season**

A woman from luxury hotels, thirty-one, no household experience at all, two hundred applications sent over eight months, four replies, none of them good. She took a chalet season in Verbier. Guest relations, four months, a fraction of the salary she wanted, and by any measure a step backwards from the hotel she had left. In February she looked after a family for eleven days. In March the wife wrote to her directly. She has now been their private concierge for six years. She did not get that role by applying. She got it by being in a room with the right family, doing an ordinary job flawlessly. This is the most common route into this profession and almost nobody plans it deliberately.

**The florist**

A man in London, unable to get past a single agency, was introduced to a florist who worked for four private households, and, having nothing else to do with his afternoon, was useful to him. He sent him a supplier. He recommended him to a hotel. He asked for nothing, for a year. When one of the four households lost its house manager, the florist was asked, over the flowers, on a Tuesday, whether he knew anyone. The people who hear about roles first are not the agencies. They are the suppliers. They are entirely reachable, nobody ever writes to them, and they are usually delighted to be asked.

**The letter that was answered**

Two hundred candidates wrote to a senior estate manager in Geneva that year. She answered one. It began with an accurate, specific line about a talk the estate manager had given, which the writer had clearly attended rather than merely found. It stated, in one sentence and without inflation, exactly what the writer was and what rung she was on. It contained a supplier recommendation, in Geneva, that was genuinely useful. It asked for nothing at all, and it ended: "if it is not a good moment, please ignore this entirely." The estate manager replied in four lines. Three months later the writer sent something else useful, again with no ask. Nine months later she was recommended for a role that was never advertised. The whole thing took under a year and cost her nothing except patience.

**The one who inflated**

A capable woman with four years in a serious hotel described herself, on her CV, as having "managed a UHNW household portfolio". It was a stretch of something that was partly true. Everybody does it, and she thought nothing of it. The consultant, who had placed two hundred people, asked one gentle follow-up question in the interview. It took eleven seconds to establish what had actually happened. She was not confronted, and she was not accused. She was simply never presented for anything again, by that agency, and by any consultant who moved from that agency to another one. Her actual four years in a hotel would have been perfectly respectable, and would have got her a first rung. The inflation cost her the market.

**The reference she could not give**

A candidate, in a final interview, was asked directly for the name of the family she had worked for. She said, calmly, that she was bound by a confidentiality agreement, that she was not able to give their name, that her reference for the period was available through the family office, and then she said nothing else, and she did not look away. The principal pressed once. She repeated it, in the same words, without apology and without any change of expression. She was offered the role that week. He said afterwards, to the agency, that it was the only moment in the interview that had told him anything. He had not been testing her discretion by accident.

**The four hundred euros**

An agency, glossy website, impressive photographs, extremely encouraging. They loved her profile. They had families waiting. There was simply a four hundred euro fee to be added to their priority list, entirely standard, refundable against her first placement. She paid it. She was never contacted again, and the telephone number stopped working within the year. A legitimate agency is paid by the family. It is not paid by the candidate. Domestic placement offices charged candidates for the best part of a century, and a handful still try; at the reputable end of private service today, it does not happen. If you are asked for money to be represented, that is not an agency, and the sole product being sold is your hope.

**Glossary**

The eight seconds · How long your application is actually read, by someone tired, looking for a reason to stop. The silence · The absence of reply. It is arithmetic, not judgement, and misreading it is why people leave the profession. The ninety-day follow-up · A follow-up that contains something new and real. Never "just checking in". The reference economy · This market's actual currency. Every house, every colleague, every supplier, forever. The undisclosable house · A role you cannot name. Handled with one calm formulation and no hints, and it becomes your strongest asset. "Would you have her back?" · The only reference question that matters. Hesitation is the answer. The turn · Year two, when your name begins to be said in rooms you are not in.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing.

1. What are the three questions a consultant answers in eight seconds, and which one kills the most candidates? 2. Explain why an agency does not reply, without using the words rude, closed, or unfair.

3. Write a ninety-day follow-up message that contains something real. Then count the words. It should be under sixty. 4. Why is a reference the actual product in this market, and what is it really answering for the family? 5. You cannot name your former employer. Write out, word for word, what you say, and then describe exactly what you do in the four seconds afterwards. 6. What is the only reference question that matters, and why is hesitation the answer?

7. In the case of the inflated CV: her real experience was respectable. Explain precisely what the inflation cost her, and why it was disproportionate. 8. In the case of the chalet season: she took a step backwards and it worked. Explain why, structurally, and not sentimentally. 9. Why do most people leave this profession in the first six months, and what would you need to believe to survive that period? 10. You are asked for a fee to be represented. Write your reply, and then write what you owe to the next person.

## How this week is practised

*Five exercises, and one of them is uncomfortable*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the sequence.

**Exercise.** What it trains, and how long

**1 · The honest rung.** Self-assessment without flattery. Thirty minutes, and it is the hardest thirty minutes in the programme.

**2 · The map of sixty.** Structured network building. Three to four hours, and it is worth every one of them.

**3 · The agency research.** Learning to see an agency as a business rather than as a gatekeeper. Two hours.

**4 · Three letters.** The method of outreach. One hour, and then you send them, which is the point.

**5 · The calendar.** Turning all of it into something that survives contact with a Tuesday.

## Exercise one

*The honest rung*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Place yourself on the ladder accurately, and defend it with evidence.

Write one page. It has three parts, and the third one is the exercise.

**Part.** What you write

**The rung I can reach today.** Name the role. Then, underneath, the evidence: what in your actual history would make a family or an agency take you for this. Not your qualities. Your evidence.

**The rung I want in five years.** Name it. Then name the three things that stand between the two, specifically, and how long each one takes. If your answer to any of them is "less than a year", check it again.

**The rung I was tempted to claim.** This is the exercise. Write down the role you wanted to put in the first box, and then write one honest paragraph on why you are not there yet. Nobody will read this except you and me, and it is the paragraph that will save you a year.

**WHY THIS IS FIRST**

Every letter you write, every agency you approach, every conversation you have this year will be calibrated by the answer to this exercise. And a consultant can tell, within one line, whether a candidate has done it honestly. Realism is so rare in this market that it functions as a credential.

## Exercise two

*The map of sixty*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build a real, named, categorised network map from nothing.

Sixty real people. Named. Findable. Not "agencies in London", which is not a person and cannot be written to. This will take you three or four hours and you will want to stop at twenty. Do not stop at twenty. The value of this exercise is entirely in the last third, because the first twenty are obvious and the last twenty are where your actual career is.

**Category.** How many, and who

**Agencies: named consultants.** 15. Not the agency. The person. Their name, the market they cover, the roles they actually recruit for, and the source where you found it.

**People doing the job you want.** 15. Estate managers, house managers, private concierges, chalet managers. Real people, currently in role, findable. You are not writing to them yet.

**Suppliers to this world.** 15. Private chefs. Florists. Charter brokers. Villa agents. Watchmakers. Wine merchants. Security consultants. These are the people who hear first, and nobody writes to them, and this is the column that will surprise you.

**Adjacent and lateral.** 15. Luxury hotel directors of guest relations. Yacht chief stews. Aviation cabin crew. Family office administrators. Private club managers. The people whose world touches yours at an angle.

For each of the sixty, one line: who they are, why they are on the map, and what you could give them. That last column is the exercise. If you cannot write it for someone, they are not on your map yet, and you should find out more before they are.

## Exercise three

*The agency research*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

See an agency as a business with incentives, not as a gate.

Choose five agencies from your map. For each one, produce half a page.

**·.** What do they actually place, and where? Not what their homepage says. What their live roles say.

**· ·.** Who is the consultant for my market and my rung? By name. What do they say they require? Quote it. Then, honestly: do I meet it, or am I hoping they will not notice?

**·.** What is their process? Registration, interview, references, presentation. Any agency that will not tell you this is telling you something.

**·.** Do they charge candidates anything, in any form, for any reason? If yes, they leave your map today, and you write down why. Then, at the end, one sentence: which of the five is genuinely for me, and which three am I on the wrong list for. Being on the wrong list is not a failure. Not knowing it is.

## Exercise four

*Three letters*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Write, and send, three real approaches.

Three letters. Under a hundred and twenty words each. Real people, from your map, and you will send them, and that is not optional, because a letter you did not send taught you nothing.

**Letter.** To whom, and what it must contain

**One · to a named consultant.** A specific first line proving you know what she covers. Your rung, honestly, in one sentence. A request to be registered, with the sentence that you are not expecting anything immediately. No attachment except your CV. No paragraph over three lines.

**Two · to someone doing the job you want.** A specific line about them that is a fact and not flattery. Your honest position in one sentence. One question that takes them ninety seconds to answer and that flatters their expertise. And the exit line, which makes it effortless not to reply.

**Three · to a supplier.** A gift, and only a gift. Something genuinely useful to their business, for nothing. No ask at all. Not one. This is the letter people find hardest to write and it is the one most likely to be answered.

**THE RULE FOR ALL THREE**

If you removed your name from the top, could this letter have been sent by anyone, to anyone? If yes, it will not be answered, and you should not send it. Rewrite it until it could only have been sent by you, to them.

## The debrief

*Read after you have written all three*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Mark your own letters against the standard.

**6.1 · The five failures, and almost every letter has at least two**

**The failure.** What it looks like, and why it kills

**The generic opening.** "I hope this message finds you well. I am writing to enquire about opportunities." Deleted at the second line. It could have been sent by anyone, to anyone, and it therefore says only one thing: that you sent forty of these.

**The inflation.** Any sentence that is technically defensible and practically untrue. The reader has seen ten thousand CVs. She will find it, and she will not confront you, and you will simply never hear from her again.

**The ask that costs them.** "Could we have a coffee?" You have asked a stranger for ninety minutes of their life, including travel, in exchange for nothing. It is a large ask disguised as a small one, and it is refused by silence.

**The absence of a gift.** If there is nothing in the letter for them, the letter is a request. Everybody at this level receives requests. Almost nobody receives anything.

**The desperation.** It is audible, it is always audible, and it is the one thing that cannot be fixed by editing. If you are writing from a place of need, wait a day, and write from a place of usefulness.

**6.2 · What a good letter feels like to receive**

Short. Specific. Accurate about itself. Useful. And easy to ignore, which is the paradox that nobody believes until it works. The letter that says "if it is not a good moment, please ignore this entirely" is answered far more often than the letter that pleads, because it removes the one thing the reader is defending, which is the obligation.

**6.3 · Now the hard part**

You will hear nothing from most of them. This is Chapter II of Module 2 and you already know the arithmetic, and you will still take it personally, and you must go back and read it again when you do. Three letters, and one reply, is an excellent week. One reply is a person who now exists in your professional life and did not exist on Monday. Do it again next week. Do it again the week after. This is the whole method and there is no faster one.

## Exercise five

*The calendar*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Turn the plan into something that survives an ordinary Tuesday.

Everything above is worthless if it is not on a calendar. Write the next twelve weeks, specifically, with dates.

**Rhythm.** What is on it

**Every week.** Three letters. Not four, not one. Three, on the same day, at the same hour, for twelve weeks. That is thirty-six real approaches, and it is more than most people make in a year.

**Every week.** Two acts of maintenance. A useful article sent to someone on your map. A real congratulation. A supplier recommendation that costs you nothing.

**Every month.** Five new names on the map, and five removed, honestly, because they were never real.

**At ninety days.** The follow-up round. Everyone who did not reply, once, with something new and real. Not before. Never "just checking in".

**At January and September.** The two windows. Your calendar should show, right now, what you will be doing in those two months, because everybody else in this market will remember them a fortnight too late.

## Marking scheme

*What I am looking for*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know how this is assessed.

**Criterion.** What earns it

**The honest rung.** I will read the third box first. If it is flattering, or missing, we will have a conversation about it, and it will be a kind one and it will not be a comfortable one.

**The map.** Sixty real, named, findable people. Not categories. And the supplier column filled properly, because it is the column that most people fake.

**The gift column.** If you cannot say what you could give each person, you have built a list of targets rather than a network.

**The letters.** Sent. Not drafted. And each one impossible to have sent to anyone else.

**The calendar.** Dates. Real ones. Three letters a week for twelve weeks, on a specific day, at a specific hour.

## What you are producing

*A plan, not an intention*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what separates a plan from a hope.

Almost everyone who finishes a programme like this one leaves with an intention. They intend to approach agencies. They intend to build a network. They intend to be strategic about it. And in March they are exactly where they were in July, and they have concluded that the market is closed. The difference between the people who get in and the people who do not is almost never talent, and it is almost never luck. It is that one of them had a piece of paper with dates on it.

## The six sections

*Three pages, no more*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build each section.

**Section.** What it contains

**1 · My rung, honestly.** The role I can be taken on for today, and the evidence. Two sentences. And the role I want in five years, and the three things between them, with a duration on each.

**2 · My market.** The city or cities. The season I am aiming at. The visa or language question answered honestly, and if it is not answered, the date by which it will be. Choosing one market and being serious about it beats being available everywhere, which reads as being wanted nowhere.

**3 · My map.** The sixty names, by category, with the gift column filled. This is the engine of the plan and it is the section that will take you the longest.

**4 · My agencies.** The five, researched. Which one is actually for me. Which three I am on the wrong list for, and what would have to change for that to be different.

**5 · My calendar.** Twelve weeks, with dates. Three letters a week, on a named day. Two acts of maintenance a week. The ninety-day follow-up round, dated. And what I will be doing in January and in September.

**6 · My honest first role.** Written down, in advance, so that you recognise it when it appears and do not turn it down out of pride. A season. A cover. A villa. A chalet. Name what you would accept, and name what you would not, and know the difference between a step backwards and a way in.

## The sentence at the top of page one

*And I want you to write it by hand*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Commit to the one thing that decides the year.

WRITE THIS, AND THEN COMPLETE IT

"I will write three letters every [day], at [hour], for the next twelve weeks, whatever happens, and I will not stop when nobody answers, because I have read what the silence actually means." Then sign it, and date it. It looks sentimental and it is not. In February, when nothing has happened and it is dark at five o'clock, this sentence is the only thing standing between you and the two hundred people who stopped four months too early.

## How it will be judged

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the standard.

**What I am looking for.** What that means

**Honesty about the rung.** If your plan targets estate manager and your evidence is four years in a hotel, I will say so plainly, and I would rather say it than let the market say it to you in silence for a year.

**Dates.** Every commitment has a date. "Regularly" is not a date. "Soon" is not a date. Tuesdays at nine, for twelve weeks, is a date.

**The gift column.** Filled, for all sixty. This is the section that tells me whether you understood the week or merely enjoyed it.

**The named first role.** Written down before you need it. The people who have not decided this in advance are the people who turn down the chalet season in a moment of pride, and it is the single most expensive moment of their career.

**Three pages.** If it is fifteen, you have written a document you will never open. Three.

## The American annex

*Where an American candidate actually looks*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Name the desks that place this work in the United States, and understand which way the visa question runs for you.

**A6.1 · The agencies that matter here**

The houses named earlier in this module are the European and international end of the market, and they are worth knowing. These are the desks that place the work in the United States, and several have been doing it for decades.

**Colonial Agency.** Domestic and estate staffing since 1963, nationwide.

**The Calendar Group.** Connecticut, more than twenty years placing household and estate staff for private families.

**Career Group Companies, Private Services and Estate Staffing.** New York, Los Angeles, San Francisco.

**Morgan & Mallet, United States.** New York, Los Angeles, Miami, San Francisco, Palm Beach and the Hamptons.

**Private Staff Group.** Boutique search, private service and estate management, for UHNW households and family offices.

**Heartland Estate Staffing, Deb's Domestic Agency, DM Estate Staffing, Lorraine Lee Estate Staffing.** Regional and national desks, the last of them Palm Beach.

Register with several. The most common error in this market is choosing one and waiting.

**A6.2 · The visa question runs the other way for you**

This module treats work authorisation as a barrier into the United States. As an American it is not your barrier at home. It becomes your barrier the moment you look at the markets this programme describes most warmly.

The United Kingdom, Switzerland, Monaco and the Gulf each require a permit or sponsorship for an American to work in a private household. Sponsorship for a domestic role is uncommon, and the household has to want you enough to arrange it. That is a realistic ambition for a second or third role, carried there by a reference. It is very rarely a first one, and any agency that tells you otherwise is describing its own hopes rather than yours.

**A6.3 · Where the numbers come from**

Treat every published figure with suspicion, including the ones in Week 1. Agency salary guides describe the households that agencies place, which is the top of the market. General salary sites describe a far wider population, including roles that share a title with yours and nothing else. Both are true about different things.

When you negotiate, quote the agency guides, and know as you do it that you are quoting the top of the range.

## The paradox

*A world that will not be sold to*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand why the ordinary rules of self-presentation invert at this level. Stop persuading, and start being verifiable.

**1.1 · Everything you were taught about applying is wrong here**

You were taught to sell yourself. To have a pitch. To be enthusiastic, dynamic, passionate. To say why you would be a great fit and what you would bring to the role. Every one of those instincts will damage you in this market, and it will damage you in the first ninety seconds, and nobody will tell you why.

**Ordinary hiring rewards.** Private service rewards

**Enthusiasm.** Composure. A person who is visibly excited to be near this family is a person who is excited by the wrong things.

**Persuasion.** Verifiability. They are not weighing your argument. They are checking whether you are safe.

**Ambition, stated.** Reliability, demonstrated. Ambition is fine and it is not the product. Nobody hires a stranger into their home because she wants to progress.

**Being memorable.** Being unremarkable, in the specific sense of never being the thing anyone has to think about. That is not a lack of personality. It is the discipline of a professional.

**Talking about yourself.** Answering, precisely, and then stopping. The silence after your answer is not yours to fill.

THE SENTENCE THAT GOVERNS THE WHOLE WEEK

They are not deciding whether you are impressive. They are deciding whether they will regret you. Everything in your dossier, everything in your bearing, everything you say in that room, is answering that one question. Impressiveness does not answer it. Very often, impressiveness makes it worse.

**1.2 · What this means practically**

You do not persuade. You present, accurately, and let them verify. The dossier is not an argument. It is evidence, arranged so that a busy person can check it in four minutes and find nothing that worries them.

## The CV at this level

*What it is, and the eleven things that kill it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build a CV that survives eight seconds and a reference check. Handle gaps, short tenures, and the house you cannot name.

**2.1 · What it actually is**

**DEFINITION**

The private service CV. A two-page document whose only purpose is to be verifiable, unembarrassing, and clear about what rung you are on. It is not a marketing document and it does not have a personal brand.

**2.2 · The structure**

**Section.** What goes in it

**The header.** Name, city, telephone, an email address that is your name and nothing else, nationality and work authorisation, languages with an honest level. Photograph only where it is the local convention, and if you use one, it is plain, recent, professional and boring.

**The line.** One sentence. What you are and what rung you are on. "Private concierge, four years in luxury hospitality, currently supporting a private household in Geneva." No adjectives. None at all.

**Experience.** Reverse chronological. For each: the house or the employer, described at the correct level of confidentiality, the dates, and three to five lines on scope. Scope, not duties. Household size, staff coordinated, residences, budget managed, travel volume.

**Education and training.** Brief. Including this programme, described accurately, which is to say as an eight-week professional programme, and never as a qualification that it is not.

**Section.** What goes in it

**Languages and practical.** Languages with honest levels. Driving licence. Visa status. First aid if you have it, and get it if you do not, because it costs a weekend and it appears on the CV of every serious candidate.

**References.** "Available on request." Nothing else. You never put a referee's telephone number on a document that will be forwarded.

**2.3 · The eleven things that kill it**

**·.** Inflation of any kind. The consultant has seen ten thousand CVs. She will find it in eleven seconds, she will not confront you, and you will simply never hear from her again.

**·.** Naming a family. Even a former one. Even one who would not mind. Doing this on a CV is, on its own, a complete disqualification for anyone serious, and it is the single most common fatal error.

**·.** Adjectives about yourself. Passionate, dynamic, dedicated, detail-oriented. They are unverifiable, everybody writes them, and they take up the space where evidence should be.

**·.** A personal statement of six lines. Nobody reads it. Replace it with the one line from the structure above.

**·.** Duties rather than scope. "Responsible for travel arrangements" is a duty. "Coordinated travel for a family of five across three residences, including private aviation" is scope, and it is checkable.

**·.** An unprofessional email address, or a personal social media link. Both are read as a judgement failure, and in this profession judgement is the whole job.

**·.** Three pages. Two. If your career genuinely does not fit on two, it is because you have written duties instead of scope.

**·.** Unexplained gaps. A gap is fine and is expected, and an unexplained gap is a question mark in a document whose entire purpose is to remove question marks. One line: what you were doing.

**·.** A pattern of eight-month positions. There may be excellent reasons for every one of them. It does not matter. Two years is the unit of credibility in this market, and if your CV shows five short roles you must address it in the letter, in one sentence, without defensiveness.

**·.** Design. No colour, no columns, no icons, no photograph of a suitcase. It is a document, not a portfolio. Serious houses read seriousness in restraint.

**·.** A typographical error. One is enough. This is a profession in which the entire product is that you notice things.

**2.4 · The house you cannot name**

Write it exactly like this, and then say nothing more about it:

**ON THE CV**

**Private household, Geneva · 2021 to 2024**

Ultra-high-net-worth family, five residences. Personal affairs, relocation and renovation projects, staff and supplier coordination. Bound by confidentiality. Reference available through the family office. That is complete, it is honest, it names nobody, and it tells a serious reader everything they need. And it tells them one more thing, which is that you keep your word.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

The CV is evidence, not persuasion. Two pages, no adjectives, no design. Never name a family. It is a complete disqualification and it is the most common fatal error. Scope, not duties. Everything on the page must be checkable.

## The letter

*One page, four paragraphs, and no pleading*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Write a letter that is read to the end. Address the difficult thing yourself, before they find it.

**3.1 · The four paragraphs**

**Paragraph.** What it does

**One · why this house, specifically.** Two sentences. Something true and specific about this role, this family profile, this city. If it could have been written for any role, it will be read as having been.

**Two · what I am.** Three or four lines. Your rung, your evidence, your scope. No adjectives, no ambition, no "I have always been passionate about".

**Three · the difficult thing.** If there is a gap, a short tenure, a career change, an unnameable house, you address it here, yourself, in one sentence, without apology and without a paragraph of explanation. The candidate who raises it first is a candidate who is in control of her own history.

**Four · the close.** Available for a conversation. Not desperate to be considered. Not grateful for their time. One line, and then your name.

**3.2 · The tone**

Warm, precise, and entirely without need. Need is audible in writing, it is always audible, and it is the single most damaging quality a candidate can transmit in this market, because a household is buying calm. Read your letter aloud. If any sentence sounds like it wants something, cut it.

## The dossier

*What a complete candidacy actually contains*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Assemble a candidacy that is complete before it is requested.

Most candidates send a CV. A complete candidacy, sent when it is asked for and never before, contains rather more, and it is one of the quiet ways a serious person separates herself in this market.

**Element.** Notes

**The CV.** Two pages. As above.

**The letter.** One page. As above.

**References.** Three, named, briefed, and warned. Chapter V.

**Certificates.** First aid, driving, food hygiene, any language certification, any professional training. Scanned, legible, current.

**Work authorisation.** Stated plainly. Do not make an employer discover a visa problem at the offer stage. It will not be forgiven, and it will be remembered by the agency.

**A confidentiality statement.** One paragraph, in your own words, on how you handle information. Almost nobody includes this, and at this level it is read very carefully indeed, because it is the entire question.

Your code of conduct From Week 3. Optional, and it is the single most distinctive thing you could put in front of a serious household, and I have never seen a candidate do it.

## References

*The product, and how to protect it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Choose, prepare and protect your referees. Understand what is actually asked, and by whom.

**5.1 · Choosing**

**·.** Three. A former employer or their family office. A senior colleague who saw you work.

And, if you have one, a supplier or a peer who can speak to how you behave when something goes wrong.

**·.** Recent, and relevant, and reachable. A magnificent reference who does not answer the telephone is not a reference.

**·.** Nobody who will hesitate. Read Week 6 again. The only question that matters is "would you have her back", and a half-second pause is the answer. If you are not certain of someone, do not use them, however senior they are.

**5.2 · Preparing them, which almost nobody does**

You call each referee before you give their name. Not after. You tell them what the role is, what the household profile is, and what will most likely be asked. You remind them of two or three specific things you did, because people forget, and a vague reference is a poor one even when it is warm. And you tell them when to expect the call. A referee who is surprised sounds hesitant, and hesitation is the answer.

**5.3 · Protecting them**

You never give a referee's details on a document that circulates. You give them when they are asked for, to a named person, for a specific role. Your referees are doing you a favour, and a referee who receives six calls about six roles you did not tell them about will quietly stop being your referee.

## The interview

*Three rooms, three entirely different conversations*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Prepare correctly for each of the three interviews. Understand what is being tested in each.

**The room.** What is actually being tested

**The agency consultant.** Whether she can put you in front of a family without embarrassment. She is not assessing your soul. She is assessing her own risk. Be precise, be realistic about your rung, be easy to place, and never inflate, because she has heard everything.

**The family office.** Competence, verifiability, and stability. They will go through your CV forensically, and they will ask about money, contracts, notice periods and visas, and they will not be charmed. Be exact, and have your documents in order, and do not be wounded by the coldness. It is not personal, it is the function.

**The principal, or their partner.** Nothing on your CV, which they have probably not read. They are answering one question, and it takes them about four minutes: could I have this person in my house every day, for years, without ever thinking about it.

**6.1 · The principal's interview, which is the one nobody prepares for**

It will feel like a conversation. It will be pleasant. It may last twenty minutes. It may barely touch your experience, and you will leave wondering whether it went well, and this is normal, and it is the most consequential interview of your life. They are testing four things, and none of them will be asked directly:

**·.** Composure. Are you easy to be around. Do you fill silences. Do you laugh a half-beat too eagerly. Are you visibly excited to be here.

**·.** Discretion. They will invite you to be indiscreet, warmly, and it will not feel like a test.

See Module 2.

**·.** Judgement. A situational question, thrown away casually. "What would you do if..." They are listening for whether you would ask, or decide.

**·.** Whether you want something from them. Beyond the job. Access, proximity, a story, a life. They have met a great many people who wanted something, and they can feel it across a room.

**6.2 · The composure rules**

**Rule.** Why

**Answer, and then stop.** The silence after your answer belongs to them. The candidate who keeps talking into it is the candidate who is nervous, and nervousness in a house is a cost.

**Never name a family, or hint at one.** Not even a little. Not even if invited. Not even if they name one first. Chapter II, and Week 3, and the rest of your life.

**Never criticise a former employer.** Not even the one who deserved it. They are not learning about that family. They are learning what you will say about them.

**Ask questions about the work, not about the life.** "How many residences?" "How is the staff structured?" "What does the role find difficult?" Never: who they are, what they do, who else works there in any personal sense.

**Do not talk about money first.** If they raise it, answer plainly and without apology. If they do not, it goes to the agency or the family office, which is what those people are for.

## The trial day

*What it is, what it is not, and what it is really measuring*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Handle a trial correctly, and refuse an exploitative one.

**7.1 · The legitimate trial**

One day, occasionally two or three. Paid. Agreed in advance in writing: the dates, the hours, the rate. This is entirely normal at every level of this profession and you should welcome it, because it is also your interview of them.

**7.2 · What it is actually measuring**

**·.** How you are with the staff. This is the whole trial and almost nobody understands it. The housekeeper will be asked. The driver will be asked. Their answer will outweigh anything the principal thought.

**·.** Whether you ask or assume. On a trial day, you ask. About everything, cheerfully, once.

Nobody has ever failed a trial for asking. People fail them every week for guessing.

**·.** What you do when there is nothing to do. The candidate who stands in a corridor waiting for instructions has failed. The candidate who finds the useful, invisible, unglamorous thing, and does it without announcing it, has passed. This is Week 4, in a room, being watched.

**·.** Whether you notice. They will leave something slightly wrong. Nobody will tell you it was deliberate, and very often it was not, and it does not matter.

**7.3 · The trial that is not a trial**

Two unpaid weeks is not a trial. A "trial" with no agreed end date is not a trial. A trial where you are asked to work alone in the residence on your first day is not a trial and it is a serious warning. Decline, politely, once, and leave. Week 6, Chapter IX.

## Presentation, arrival, and the room

*The first eleven seconds, and how to be forgettable in the right way*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Arrive correctly. Present yourself so that nothing about you is a thought.

**8.1 · Appearance**

The objective is not elegance and it is certainly not fashion. The objective is that nobody in the room has a single thought about how you look. That is a much harder target than looking impressive, and it is the correct one.

**Element.** The standard

**The clothes.** Impeccable, unremarkable, well cut, quiet. Navy, grey, black, cream. Nothing new-looking, nothing loud, nothing that requires adjusting when you sit down.

**The details.** Immaculate shoes, and they will be looked at, by people who look at shoes. Clean, short nails. Hair that does not need touching. Nothing that catches the light.

**Scent.** None. Not a light one. None. You will be in small rooms and other people's cars for years.

**The visible logo.** No. Not one. You are not a client of this world, and the candidate who dresses like the family is the candidate who has misunderstood the position, and it is noticed instantly and it is never mentioned.

**The bag, the notebook, the pen.** A plain notebook and a working pen, and you will use them, because writing things down is the visible proof that you took it seriously.

**8.2 · Arrival**

You are in the area thirty minutes early, and at the door six minutes early. Not fifteen: fifteen is an imposition, and someone has to deal with you. Six.

You are pleasant to everybody, in order of how little they can do for you. The person at the gate. The housekeeper who opens the door. The assistant who is twenty-three. Every one of them will be asked, and the candidate who was charming upstairs and brisk downstairs has already failed, and will never know why.

## The offer

*What to negotiate, what to check, and what you never do*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read an offer properly. Negotiate once, calmly, and on the right things.

**9.1 · What must be in writing before you accept anything**

**Term.** Why it matters

**Salary, gross, and the currency.** And who is the legal employer: the family office, a company, or an individual. This is not a detail. It is your entire legal protection.

**Hours, and what "flexibility" means.** It will say flexible. Everything at this level says flexible. Ask, plainly, what a normal week actually looks like, and what happens in August, and what happened to the last person in this role.

**Days off, and whether they are real.** A written day off that is cancelled twice a month is not a day off, and this is the single largest cause of burnout in this profession.

**Accommodation, if live-in.** What exactly. Whose furniture. What happens to it if the role ends on a Tuesday. Read this clause twice and then read it again.

**Travel.** How much, how far, how often, and whether it is paid differently. "Some travel" has meant a hundred and forty nights a year to people I have known.

**Notice, both ways.** And the probation. Symmetry here tells you a great deal about the house.

**The confidentiality agreement.** You will sign one, and you will read it properly, and you will take two days, and you will take advice if a clause is unclear. Week 3.

**9.2 · Negotiating**

You may negotiate, once, calmly, on substance, and you will be respected for it if you do it well. Salary, days off, travel load, a probation review at three months.

You do not negotiate with excitement, and you do not negotiate twice. One considered response, in writing, on two or three points at most, with a reason for each, and then you accept the answer gracefully whatever it is. The candidate who reopens the negotiation after it has closed has told the family exactly how the next four years will go.

**9.3 · The thing you never do**

**NEVER**

You never use another offer as leverage, and you never accept a role and then continue to interview. This world is very small, and both of those things will be known within a season, and they will be known by the agency, and the agency has a very long memory and only one asset.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Get the legal employer, the hours, the days off and the travel in writing, before you accept. Negotiate once, calmly, on substance, and then accept the answer gracefully. Never use a second offer as leverage. It will be known, and it will follow you.

**Glossary**

Scope, not duties · The only way to describe experience on a CV at this level. Checkable, quantified, unadorned. The unnameable house · A confidential former employer. Described by profile, referenced through the family office, never named. The complete dossier · CV, letter, references, certificates, work authorisation, a confidentiality statement, and your code of conduct. The briefed referee · A referee you called before giving their name. The unbriefed referee hesitates, and hesitation is the answer. The three rooms · The agency, the family office, the principal. Three entirely different conversations, testing three different things. The trial day · Paid, dated, agreed in writing. It measures how you are with the staff and what you do when there is nothing to do. Forgettable presentation · Dressing so that nobody in the room has a single thought about how you look. Harder than looking impressive, and correct. The legal employer · The family office, a company, or an individual. Establish this before you sign anything at all.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing, with your own CV in front of you.

1. Rewrite the sentence "they are deciding whether you are impressive" into what they are actually deciding, and explain what that changes about your CV. 2. Go through your own CV and find the adjectives about yourself. Count them. Delete them. What is left, and is it evidence? 3. Take one line of duties from your CV and rewrite it as scope, with numbers.

4. Write the CV entry for a house you cannot name. Four lines. Then read it aloud and check that it names nobody and hints at nothing. 5. Write the third paragraph of your letter: the difficult thing, in one sentence, without apology. 6. Name your three referees. For each, answer honestly: would they hesitate, for even half a second, on "would you have her back"? 7. What are the four things a principal is testing in an interview, and why is none of them on your CV? 8. You are on a trial day and there is nothing to do. Describe exactly what you do in the next twenty minutes. 9. You are offered a role. List, in order, the seven things that must be in writing before you accept. 10. You have an offer and a second interview elsewhere that you think may go better. What do you do, and what would it cost you to do the other thing?

**Further study**

**·.** Rebuild your CV this week, from nothing, on two pages. Not editing the old one. From nothing. Editing preserves the inflation, and you will not see it, because you wrote it.

**·.** Get a first aid certificate. It costs a weekend. It appears on the CV of every serious candidate in this profession and its absence is noticed.

**·.** Call your three referees this week, before you need them, and ask them plainly whether they would be comfortable. The answer, and the speed of the answer, is information you need now rather than in March.

**·.** Have someone film you answering five interview questions, and watch it with the sound off. You are looking for one thing only: whether you look like a person who is comfortable, or a person who wants something.
