## The questions that are not questions

*And what each one is really measuring*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Decode the standard questions. Answer the question that was actually asked.

**What they ask.** What they are measuring, and how you fail it

**"Tell me about yourself.".** Length, and control. They are timing you. Ninety seconds, structured, then stop. The candidate who talks for five minutes has told them exactly what living with her will be like.

**"Why did you leave your last role?".** Whether you will criticise. There is no version of the honest, wounded answer that helps you. "The family relocated." "The role changed." "It came to a natural end." Neutral, brief, and then silence, and let them move on.

**"What was the hardest thing you dealt with there?".** Two things at once. Whether you have real operational experience, and whether, in telling it, you will disclose something you should not. Give the shape of the problem and the method of the solution, and no identifying detail whatsoever. This is the most dangerous good question in the set.

**"Tell me about a mistake you made.".** Whether you take responsibility, and whether you can be told things. Give a real one. A small, real, owned mistake, with what you changed afterwards. The candidate who cannot produce a mistake is a candidate who will conceal them.

**"How do you handle pressure?".** Nobody wants the answer. They want to see you answer it calmly. The content is irrelevant. The delivery is the entire question.

**"What do you know about us?".** Whether you have researched them, and, far more importantly, how much. Knowing their business is diligence. Knowing about their children is a catastrophe, and there is a line, and it is not subtle.

**"Where do you see yourself in five years?".** Whether you will leave. Nobody in a private household wants to hire a stepping stone. Ambition is fine. Ambition that visibly points away from their house is not.

**"Do you have any questions for us?".** The most revealing question in the interview. Chapter V.

## How they test your discretion

*It will not feel like a test, and that is the design*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise the discretion test in the room. Pass it without appearing to have noticed it.

Somewhere in that interview, you will be invited to be indiscreet. It will be warm. It will feel like rapport. It may come from the principal, from the agency, or from a spouse who is being charming. And here is the cruel part: the invitation is often not deliberate. Sometimes they are genuinely curious. It does not matter at all. The information they take from your answer is identical either way.

**The invitation.** What it sounds like

**The name.** "Who were you with before? We probably know them." Said lightly, and quite possibly true.

**The story.** "Go on, what are they really like?" Warm, laughing, entirely disarming.

**The complaint.** "That must have been difficult, working for someone like that." An offered sympathy, which is the most effective of all of them, because it is kind.

**The peer.** A principal names a family, and waits to see whether you will confirm, deny, react, or blink.

**The trivial.** "Which airline do they use?" Harmless. Entirely harmless. And a test.

THE ANSWER, AND IT IS ALWAYS THE SAME

"I am not able to talk about the families I have worked for. I am sure you would want the same, in time." Warm. Complete. No apology, no hesitation, no explanation. And then you stop, and you hold their eye, and you do not smile as though you might say more if pressed, because that smile is the failure.

The second half of that sentence, "I am sure you would want the same", is what turns a refusal into a qualification. You have not been difficult. You have shown them, in nine words, exactly what it will be like to be your employer. And if they push a second time, you repeat it. In the same words, with the same face. A candidate who yields on the second push has told them she yields on the second push.

## The people who decide

*And who are frequently not in the room*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand who is actually consulted. Behave accordingly, from the gate onwards.

You will believe the decision was made by the person who interviewed you. Very often it was not, or not alone.

**Who.** What they contribute

**The housekeeper.** Asked, almost always, in a house with long-serving staff. "What did you think of who opened the door her?" Her answer is not a data point. In many houses it is the decision.

**The assistant who booked the meeting.** How were you on the telephone. Were you late. Were you demanding about the time. Were you different with her than with them.

**The other principal.** Frequently not present, frequently the one whose standards govern the house, and frequently the one who says no, quietly, three days later, without a reason being given.

**The person they trust who might know you.** The back channel from Week 6. Ninety seconds, and it outweighs everything.

**The children.** Not in any formal sense. And a child who says "I didn't like her" is a fact that no reference can undo.

Which produces one rule, and it is the whole chapter: the interview began at the gate, and it includes everyone. The candidate who is warm with the principal and brisk with the housekeeper has already failed, and she will spend a week wondering why she never heard back.

## Money

*How to talk about it without flinching, and without asking first*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Handle the salary conversation with composure. Know what your rung actually pays before you enter the room.

**4.1 · Who raises it, and when**

You do not raise it. Not in a first interview, not with a principal, ever. It goes through the agency or the family office, which is precisely what they are for, and one of the reasons a serious candidate is glad the agency exists. If they raise it, you answer plainly, immediately, and without apologising. Hesitation here is read as either dishonesty or as not knowing your own worth, and both are expensive.

**4.2 · The number**

Know it before you walk in. Not a range you hope for: the actual market band for your rung, in that city. Week 6 gave you the ladder. If you cannot say your band in one sentence, you are not ready to be asked.

**THE FORMULATION**

"For a role at this level, in this city, I would expect somewhere between X and Y. But I would rather understand the role properly first, and I am sure we would find the right number." Direct, unembarrassed, and it hands the conversation back without conceding anything. Then stop talking. The next person to speak has conceded, and it will not be you.

**4.3 · What you never do**

**·.** Never apologise for a number. "I know it is a lot, but..." You have just negotiated against yourself, and they heard it.

**·.** Never inflate your current salary. It is checkable, it is often checked, and being caught on this single point ends a candidacy instantly, in a market where you have one asset.

**·.** Never accept on the spot out of gratitude. "Thank you, may I confirm tomorrow morning" is a complete, professional, and entirely normal sentence, and it is also a small demonstration of exactly the composure they are buying.

## The questions you ask

*The most revealing four minutes of the interview*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Ask questions that demonstrate the work rather than the wanting.

"Do you have any questions for us?" is not a courtesy. It is the last test, and it is the one that most often changes an interviewer's mind, in both directions. Because your questions reveal, with total accuracy, what you are actually interested in.

**The question you ask.** What it tells them

**"How is the staff structured, and who would I coordinate with?".** You are thinking about the work, and about other people, and you understand that you will have responsibility without authority.

**"What does this role find most difficult?".** You are serious, you are not naive, and you have asked the one question that will get you an honest and useful answer.

**"What would a good first ninety days look like to you?".** You are already inside the role. This question, in my experience, is the one that most often ends the interview well.

**"How do you prefer to be communicated with, and how often?".** You understand that their attention is the product. Nobody asks this. It lands every time.

**"What happened to the person who was here before?".** Bold, and entirely legitimate, and asked calmly it is a mark of seriousness. The answer will tell you more about the house than anything else in the interview.

And the questions that end you, quietly, in the last four minutes:

**·.** Anything about their private life, their business, or their children, however innocently framed. You have just demonstrated the exact appetite that this profession cannot accommodate.

**·.** Anything about the travel in an excited way. "Would I go with them to the chalet?" You have told them you want the life, not the job.

**·.** Anything about time off, first. Legitimate, necessary, and it belongs to the family office and to the offer stage, and not to the first four minutes you have alone with a principal.

**·.** Nothing at all. "No, I think you have covered everything." You have just told them that you are not thinking. Never arrive without three questions written in the notebook you brought.

## Houses I have seen

*Six interviews*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise these before you are in one.

**The name**

A final interview, in a house in London. Everything had gone well for fifty minutes. And then the principal, pleasantly, in the manner of a man making conversation at the end of a good meeting, said: "So who were you with before? We probably know them." She said the name. It was a small thing. She had left on good terms, she was proud of the role, and he had asked so warmly. She was not offered the position. Nothing was said. He had learned, in one sentence, exactly what would be said about him in the next interview she ever attended.

**The one who repeated herself**

The same question, in a different house, to a different candidate. "Who were you with before?" "I am not able to talk about the families I have worked for. I am sure you would want the same, in time." He pressed once, lightly, still smiling. She said it again. Exactly the same words. Same expression, no apology, and she did not look away. She was offered the role that week. He told the agency afterwards that it was the only moment in the interview that had told him anything at all. He had not been curious. He had been recruiting.

**The housekeeper**

A candidate arrived, was let in by the housekeeper, and was charming for fifty minutes with the principal, and it went, by every visible measure, extremely well.

On the way out she handed the housekeeper her coat without looking at her. That evening the principal asked, as he always did, what she had thought. The housekeeper had been in that house for nineteen years, and she said one sentence, and it ended the candidacy. The candidate never learned that the interview had not ended when she left the drawing room.

**The five minutes**

"Tell me about yourself." He spoke for six and a half minutes. Everything he said was true, relevant, and impressive. The interviewer, who had been listening for ninety seconds and then timing him, wrote one word in the margin, and the word was "tiring". A household is a small space, over many years, and a person who cannot stop is a person you cannot live with, however good she is.

**The question about the chalet**

She was excellent. Genuinely, technically excellent, and everyone in the room knew it. At the end: "Do you have any questions?" And she said, with real warmth and a bright smile: "Would I get to travel with the family to Courchevel?" She did not get the role, and she would have been the best candidate they saw. In one sentence she had told them that she wanted the life rather than the work, and that is the one appetite a household cannot have inside it.

**The ninety days**

A candidate with less experience than two others on the list, and a rung below where the family had been looking. At the end, she asked one question: "What would a good first ninety days look like to you?" The principal talked for eleven minutes. He had never been asked it. He said afterwards that it was the moment he decided, and that he could not entirely explain why. She had stopped being a candidate and started being the person in the role, and she did it in a single sentence, and she did it by asking about them and not about herself.

**Glossary**

The invitation · The warm, disarming, frequently unintentional prompt to be indiscreet. It will happen in every interview you ever attend. The second push · When they ask again. Yielding here tells them you yield. Repeat the same words, with the same face. The back channel · The person they trust who might know you. Ninety seconds, and it outweighs your entire dossier. The ninety-second rule · The correct length of "tell me about yourself". They are timing you, and they will not say so. The wanting · The visible appetite for the life rather than the work. It is felt across a room, and it is fatal. The last four minutes · Your questions. The most revealing part of the interview, and the part nobody prepares.

**Self-assessment**

Answer in writing, out loud, and then again in front of a camera.

1. "Tell me about yourself." Write it. Ninety seconds. Time it. Then cut it by a quarter.

2. "Why did you leave your last role?" Write the neutral version. Then write the wounded version, and read them both aloud, and notice which one you would hire. 3. "What was the hardest thing you handled there?" Give the shape and the method, with no identifying detail at all. Then reread it and remove the two details you did not notice you left in. 4. The principal names a family and waits. Write exactly what you say, and describe your face.

5. He pushes a second time, warmly. What do you do?

6. Explain why "I am sure you would want the same, in time" turns a refusal into a qualification. 7. Who, other than your interviewer, will be consulted about you? Name five, and say what each one saw. 8. What is the market band for your rung, in your city? One sentence. If you cannot, you are not ready to be asked. 9. Write your three questions for the end of the interview. Then check each one: is it about the work, or about the life? 10. Of the six cases, which one is you? Do not choose the flattering answer.

## How this week is practised

*Five exercises*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the sequence.

**Exercise.** What it trains, and how long

**1 · The CV, rebuilt.** Honesty and restraint. Two hours, and you will delete more than you write.

**2 · The reference calls.** Three real calls, this week, before you need them. Thirty minutes, and almost nobody does it before they need it.

**3 · Twenty-five questions.** The interview itself. Two hours, written, then aloud.

**4 · The camera.** What you actually look like. Twenty minutes, and it is the one you will want to skip.

**5 · The offer.** Reading a contract, and negotiating once, calmly.

## Exercise one

*The CV, rebuilt from nothing*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Produce a two-page CV that would survive a reference check and eight seconds.

From nothing. Not editing the old one, because editing preserves the inflation, and you will not see it, because you wrote it and it feels true. Blank page. The structure from Module 1. Two pages. Then apply the eleven tests, honestly, one by one.

**Test.** Pass or fail

**Adjectives about myself.** Zero. Count them. Then delete them, and see what is left, and whether it is evidence.

**Duties written as scope.** Every line quantified: household size, staff, residences, budget, travel.

**Any family named or hinted at.** If yes, this is not a stylistic error. It is a disqualification, and I will send it back.

**Gaps.** Each one explained in a single line. Not defended. Explained.

**Short tenures.** If there is a pattern, it is addressed in the letter, in one sentence, without defensiveness.

**Two pages.** Two. If you cannot, you have written duties.

Typographical errors Read it backwards, sentence by sentence. This is a profession whose entire product is that you notice things.

## Exercise two

*The reference calls*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Secure and brief three referees, this week, before you need them.

This is the exercise people put off, and it is the one that will most change what happens to you this year. Call three people. Not email. Call. And ask them plainly:

**THE QUESTION**

"I am beginning to look for a role in private service. Would you be comfortable being a reference for me, and would you be comfortable being asked whether you would have me back?" Then listen. Not to the answer. To the half second before it.

Write down, for each: what they said, how fast they said it, and what they emphasised. A referee who hesitates is a referee you have just saved yourself from, and finding that out now, in July, is worth a very great deal. Then brief the three who did not hesitate: what you are looking for, what will be asked, and two or three specific things you did that they may have forgotten. People forget. A vague reference is a poor one, even when it is warm.

## Exercise three

*The mock interview*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Answer twenty-five questions, in writing, then aloud, before revealing the debrief.

Write every answer. Then say it aloud, timed. Then reveal the debrief, and not before.

**The opening**

1. Tell me about yourself.

2. Why private service?

3. Why this role, and why now?

**The history**

4. Walk me through your last role.

5. Why did you leave?

6. There is a gap here of seven months. What were you doing?

7. You have had three roles in four years. Should I be concerned?

8. What was the hardest thing you dealt with there?

9. Tell me about a mistake you made.

**The craft**

10. What does discretion mean to you?

11. The principal's wife asks you what time her husband got home last night. You know. What do you say? 12. A journalist telephones the house and says he already has the story. What do you do?

13. You realise a supplier has overcharged us by two thousand euros and nobody has noticed. Walk me through the next hour.

14. It is Thursday. The flight is cancelled and the board meeting is at nine in Geneva. Talk me through it. 15. How would you describe the difference between good service and exceptional service?

16. A member of staff who has been here eleven years does something you believe is wrong. What do you do?

**The pressure**

17. How do you handle pressure?

18. What would you do if I asked you to do something you thought was a mistake?

19. What would you do if I asked you to do something you thought was wrong?

20. What is your greatest weakness?

**The traps**

21. Who were you with before? We probably know them.

22. Go on. What are they really like?

23. That must have been difficult, working for someone like that.

24. What are you looking for, in terms of salary?

**The end**

25. Do you have any questions for us?

## The debrief

*The eleven that decide it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Compare your answers to the standard.

1 · "Tell me about yourself." Ninety seconds. Where you are from professionally, what you have done, what rung you are on, and why you are in this room. Then stop. They are timing you and they will not tell you so. Six minutes of excellent material is a worse answer than ninety seconds of adequate material, and the reason is in the case of the five minutes.

5 · "Why did you leave?" Neutral, brief, no wound. "The family relocated." "The role changed." "It came to a natural end and we parted well." There is no version of the honest, aggrieved answer that helps you, including the one where you were entirely right. They are not learning about that family. They are learning what you will say about them.

7 · "Three roles in four years. Should I be concerned?" Answer it head on, without defensiveness, in two sentences, and then stop. "Two were seasonal by design and the third relocated. I am looking for a house to stay in, and I would want you to know that before you took me." Do not explain for a paragraph. Length here is read as anxiety, and anxiety is read as a reason to worry.

8 · "The hardest thing you dealt with." The most dangerous good question in the set, and the one that catches the most people, because they want to answer it well. Give the shape of the problem and the method of the solution, and no identifying detail whatever. Not the city, not the season, not the number of children, not the industry. Then reread your written answer, and find the two details you did not notice you left in. There are always two.

9 · "Tell me about a mistake." Give a real one. Small, real, owned, and with what you changed afterwards. The candidate who cannot produce a mistake is telling you that she will conceal them, and every experienced interviewer knows it, and it is a worse answer than any mistake you could confess.

11 · The wife asks what time he got home. "I am not sure of the exact time." Nothing else. No elaboration, no expression, no looking away. Week 4, Scenario 11. You do not carry information between principals, in either direction, ever, and you will feel dishonest, and you are being a professional.

18 · "Something you thought was a mistake." You say so, once, in private, briefly, with the reason and an alternative. And then, if they still want it, you do it, well, and without sulking, and you never mention it again. That is the answer, and the second half is the half they are listening for.

19 · "Something you thought was wrong." Different question entirely, and they know it, and they are watching to see whether you noticed the difference. You decline. Calmly, without a lecture, without moral theatre, and you accept the consequence. Your Week 3 code of conduct named three things you would refuse at the cost of the position. Name one of them, plainly, and stop talking.

**21 to 23 · The traps**

All three are the same test wearing different clothes. "I am not able to talk about the families I have worked for. I am sure you would want the same, in time." Warm. Complete. No apology. And if they push, the same words, the same face, and no smile that suggests you might yield.

24 · "Your salary expectation." Your band, in one sentence, without apologising, and then hand it back. "For a role at this level, in this city, I would expect between X and Y. But I would rather understand the role properly first." Then stop talking. The next person to speak has conceded.

25 · "Do you have any questions?"

Three, written in the notebook you brought, and all three about the work. What does this role find difficult. How is the staff structured. What would a good first ninety days look like to you. Nothing about the travel, nothing about the life, nothing about them.

## Exercise four

*The camera*

Film yourself answering five of the twenty-five. Any five, including at least one of the traps. Then watch it with the sound off, and answer one question only:

**THE ONLY QUESTION**

Does that person look comfortable, or does she look like she wants something? You will know within four seconds, and you will not enjoy it, and it is worth sitting through.

Then watch it again with the sound on, and count: the filled silences, the nervous laugh, the sentence that went on after it had finished. Write the number down. Film it again on Sunday, and write the number down again.

## Exercise five

*The offer*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Read an offer properly, and negotiate once.

**THE OFFER, AS RECEIVED**

Private concierge to a family of four. Geneva, with travel. Ninety-five thousand francs. Live-out. Flexible hours. Two days off per week. Six-month probation. Start in three weeks. They would like an answer by Friday.

Write three things.

**·.** The seven questions you must have answered in writing before you accept. Module 1,

Chapter IX.

**·.** The two or three points you will negotiate, with a reason for each. Not five. Two or three, and each one must be substantive rather than a test of your own courage.

**·.** Your written reply. Under a hundred and fifty words. Calm, specific, unapologetic, and containing the sentence that lets them say no without either of you losing anything. And then answer this, honestly, in one paragraph: what would make you walk away from this offer? If your answer is "nothing", you are not negotiating. You are hoping, and they will feel it in the first line.

## Marking scheme

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know how this is assessed.

**Criterion.** What earns it

**The CV.** Zero adjectives. Every line scope, not duties. No family named or hinted at. Two pages.

**The reference calls.** Made. Actually made, by telephone, this week. I will ask you what each of them said in the half second before they answered.

**The traps.** Questions 21, 22 and 23 answered identically, warmly, and without apology. A candidate who softens on the third has failed the module.

**Questions 18 and 19.** The difference between a mistake and a wrong. If your two answers are the same, you have not read Week 3.

**The camera.** Filmed twice, and the count written down both times.

**The offer.** Two or three points, substantive, with reasons. And an honest answer to what would make you walk away.

## What you are producing

*A complete candidacy, ready to send on Monday*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what complete means.

Most candidates have a CV. That is not a candidacy, and the gap between the two is the reason a great many capable people are never called back. A candidacy is complete when a busy person can check you in four minutes and find nothing that worries them. That is the standard, and it is the only one.

## The seven pieces

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Assemble each one.

**Piece.** Notes

**1 · The CV.** Two pages. Rebuilt from nothing this week. Zero adjectives, scope not duties, no family named.

**2 · The letter.** One page, four paragraphs, and the difficult thing addressed by you in paragraph three, in one sentence, without apology.

**3 · The reference sheet.** Three referees, called this week, briefed, and none of them hesitating. Held separately, given when asked, never attached to a circulating document.

**4 · Certificates.** First aid, driving, languages, training. Scanned, legible, current. Get the first aid certificate if you do not have it. It costs a weekend.

**5 · Work authorisation.** Stated plainly and early. Never let an employer discover a visa problem at the offer stage.

**6 · The confidentiality statement.** One paragraph, in your own words. Chapter III below, and it is the piece nobody else will have.

**7 · Your code of conduct.** From Week 3. Optional, and I have never seen a candidate produce one, and I have never met an employer who would not want to read it.

## The confidentiality statement

*The one page nobody else will send*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Write the paragraph that answers the only real question.

A household is trying to answer one question about you, and no certificate on earth answers it: is this person safe? So answer it. One paragraph, in your own words, on how you actually handle information. Not a promise, which anyone can make. A method, which almost nobody can describe, and which is instantly recognisable as real.

**· · ·.** How you classify information, and what never travels. What you do with a device, a record, a photograph, a message. What you have already declined to say, and to whom, and the fact that you are still not saying it.

**·.** And, if it applies to you: that there is a family you have worked for whose name you have not written on any document, and never will, and that this is the reason they should consider you rather than a reason to hesitate. Six or seven lines. Not a page. And read it aloud before you send it, because if it sounds like marketing, it is marketing, and they will hear that instantly and it will do you damage.

## Three finished examples

*Read them once, close them, and write your own from your own facts*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

See a finished résumé, a finished letter and a finished confidentiality statement, so that you are not inventing the standard from a description of it.

Everything below is invented. The person does not exist, and neither does the household. Read each one, notice what it does and what it refuses to do, and then close this page before you write. A document copied from a model is audible in the first line, and I will hear it.

**A · The résumé**

*One page, American form. The two-page instruction earlier in this module is the European one. Use whichever matches the market you are writing to.*

CLAIRE M. DONNELLY
Palm Beach, Florida · claire.donnelly@email.com · (561) 555 0148

PRIVATE SERVICE

**Private household · Palm Beach and Aspen · 2022 to 2026 · House Manager.** Ran a primary residence of 11,000 square feet and a seasonal mountain property for a family of four. Supervised six: two housekeepers, a chef, a houseman, a driver, and seasonal grounds staff. Held a household operating budget of $840,000, reconciled monthly to the family office. Coordinated all travel, including charter aviation and the two annual relocations between residences. Ran a fourteen-month kitchen and staff-wing renovation from tender to completion, on budget. Bound by confidentiality. Reference available through the family office.

**Private household · Greenwich, Connecticut · 2019 to 2022 · Personal Assistant to the principal.** Diary, correspondence and personal affairs for one principal and, from 2021, for her mother. Managed medical scheduling, domestic and international travel, and the household calendar against a demanding board schedule. Vetted and managed nineteen suppliers. Introduced the weekly written brief that the household still uses. Reference available on request.

HOSPITALITY

**The Breakers, Palm Beach · 2016 to 2019 · Guest Relations, then Guest Relations Supervisor.** Managed arrivals and stays for returning and VIP guests. Supervised a team of four from 2018. Promoted twice in three years.

EDUCATION AND CERTIFICATION

BA, Hospitality Management, University of Florida, 2016. American Red Cross Adult and Pediatric First Aid, CPR and AED, current to 2027. Florida driver's licence, clean. Conversational French.

Notice what is not there. No photograph, no date of birth, no marital status, no personal statement, no adjective about herself, and no family named. Every line contains a number that could be checked, and the confidential household is written so that its confidentiality reads as evidence rather than as a gap.

**B · The letter**

*One page. Four paragraphs. The difficult thing is hers to raise, and it sits in paragraph three.*

Dear Ms Hale,

I am writing to introduce myself for private household work in Palm Beach and the Hamptons. I have spent seven years in private service, most recently running a two-residence household for a family of four, and I am looking for a single household to settle into for a long time.

What I do is straightforward and I do it well. I run the house so that the people who live in it do not have to think about it: the staff, the budget, the suppliers, the travel, and the two moves a year. In my last position I held a household budget of $840,000 and reported on it monthly to the family office, and in four years I never once presented a figure that had to be corrected afterwards.

I left my last role in March because the family consolidated to one residence and the position was no longer needed. My principal knows I am writing to agencies and is willing to speak to you through the family office, which is how references for that household are handled. There is one household on my résumé that is not named, and never will be, and I would rather you hear that from me now than wonder about it later.

If it would be useful to have my résumé and reference sheet on file, I would be glad to send them. I am registered in Florida and available from the first of the month.

Yours sincerely,
Claire M. Donnelly

Notice that nothing in it asks for anything, that the awkward fact arrives before anybody has to go looking for it, and that the last paragraph offers rather than requests. Read yours aloud. If any sentence sounds like it wants something, cut it.

**C · The confidentiality statement**

*Six lines. A method, not a promise.*

I treat household information in three categories. What is already public, which I do not repeat anyway. What is operational, which travels only to the person who needs it to do their work, and no further. And what is personal to the family, which does not leave the house in any form, including with me. I do not photograph interiors, I do not carry household material on a personal device, and my written log records instructions, decisions and sums, never opinions and never anything about the family's private life. There is one household on my résumé I have not named to you, and I will not, and in four years I have declined to name it to three people who asked directly, including one who was in a position to offer me work. I would expect to extend the same to you, permanently, including after I have gone.

Notice that it contains no adjectives, no assurances, and nothing that could not be verified by watching her work for a week. That last sentence is the one that gets read twice.

## How it will be judged

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand the standard.

**What I am looking for.** What that means

**Verifiability.** Every claim checkable. I will pick two lines at random and ask you to prove them, exactly as a family office would, and without warmth.

**Restraint.** No adjectives, no design, no personal brand. Two pages, one page, one paragraph.

**The rung.** Consistent with your Week 6 plan. If your candidacy targets a rung above the one you named, one of the two documents is dishonest, and we will find out which.

**The referees.** Called. If you have not called them, do not submit, and tell me so, and we will do it together.

**The confidentiality statement.** Method, not promise. This is the piece I will read first, and it is the piece that will get you hired.

## The American annex

*The American résumé, and the American offer*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Convert the document this module describes into the one an American agency expects, and read an American offer correctly.

**A7.1 · What comes off the page**

What this module describes is a European CV. The American résumé is a different object, and the European version in front of an American consultant marks you in the first two seconds.

**Length.** One page, unless you have more than ten to fifteen years, and then two. Never three.

**No photograph.** Ever.

**No date of birth, no marital status, no children, no nationality.** This is not modesty. American employers avoid holding these because knowing them creates discrimination exposure for them, and a résumé that volunteers them is a résumé that makes the reader uneasy.

**Work authorisation.** One line, and only if it is genuinely in question. An American applying in the United States does not mention it at all.

**References.** Not on the page, and not even "available on request", which spends a line to say nothing. A separate sheet, prepared, handed over when asked.

Everything else in this module holds without a change. Zero adjectives. Scope rather than duties. No family named. And the confidential household written exactly as Chapter II sets it out, which is the strongest paragraph on your page and is word for word the same in both markets.

**A7.2 · The American offer**

The exercise in this module is written in Swiss francs with a six-month probation. Read it for its structure, and know that the American version differs in two ways that matter.

There is no probation period in an at-will state, because there is nothing to graduate into. You are at-will on day one and at-will in year four. If an American offer letter mentions a ninety-day probation it is describing a custom rather than a legal status, and it is worth asking pleasantly what changes on day ninety-one, because the honest answer is usually nothing.

And the number is not the offer. Health insurance, the retirement match, paid time off in days, the vehicle, the phone, and who pays your journey home are the offer. Ask for all of it in writing before you answer, and then answer in writing.

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Submit correctly.

One PDF. In order. Submitted before your session, with the filmed interview and the completed offer exercise. And then send it. To one of the agencies from your Week 6 map, on the Monday after your session, before it is perfect, because it will never be perfect and the waiting is not preparation. The candidacy that is still being improved in October is the candidacy of someone who has decided, without admitting it, not to go.

HOW TO SUBMIT, AND HOW TO BOOK YOUR SESSION

## Getting in is not the achievement

*And why the offer is the easiest part*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand why the first year ends badly for so many, and reset what you think success looks like.

**1.1 · The uncomfortable thing nobody publishes**

Nobody publishes retention figures for this profession, and you should be suspicious of anyone who quotes you one. So take what follows as observation rather than data, which is what it is. A great many people get into this profession. Far fewer are still in the same house two years later, and fewer again are still in the profession at all in five years. And here is what is remarkable: very few of them were dismissed for incompetence. They were dismissed, or they quietly left, for reasons that are entirely learnable, entirely predictable, and entirely covered in this module.

**1.2 · What you are actually doing in the first ninety days**

**THE REFRAME**

You have not been hired. You have been given a trial that lasts ninety days and that nobody will describe to you as a trial. You will not be told how you are doing. There will be no review, no feedback, and no warning. There will be an accumulation of small observations, and then, one day, a settled view of you that will not be revisited.

**1.3 · The objective**

Not to impress. Not to demonstrate what you learned. Not to improve anything. The objective of the first ninety days is to become the person nobody has to think about. That is an unglamorous ambition and it is the correct one, and the people who understand it in month one are the people who are still there in year five.

## The week before you begin

*The preparation nobody does*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Arrive already useful. Understand what you may prepare, and what you must not.

**2.1 · What you prepare**

**·.** Your toolkit, blank and ready. From Week 5. The brief sheet, the observation log, the vendor card, the expense log, the escalation card. You are operational on day one because you brought the systems with you.

**·.** Your code of conduct, reread. From Week 3. Read it the night before. You will need it inside three weeks, and you will need it on a day when you were not expecting to.

**·.** The geography. The city, the neighbourhood, the routes, the hospital, the pharmacy, the good florist, the nearest airport and how long it actually takes at eight in the morning rather than what the map says.

**·.** The practical. Your own documents in order, your own accommodation settled, your own life sorted, because a person managing their own chaos cannot absorb anybody else's.

**2.2 · What you never do**

You do not research the family. Not their business, not their history, not their photographs, not their children. You will be tempted, and it will feel like diligence, and it is the exact appetite this profession cannot accommodate. Everything you need to know, you will be told, or you will observe. Everything else is curiosity, and curiosity, in a house, has a different name.

## Day one

*Which is a day of listening, and of almost nothing else*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Get day one exactly right. Understand what everyone in the house is doing on day one, which is watching you.

**Do.** Why

**Arrive early, and be perfectly ordinary about it.** Not dramatically early. Six minutes. It is noticed once, and then assumed, and being assumed reliable is the foundation of everything.

**Learn every name, on the first day, and use them.** Every single person. The gardener. The night driver. The woman who comes on Thursdays. Write them down. Use them tomorrow. This one act does more for your first month than anything else you can do.

**Ask, and write it down in front of them.** Ask about everything, cheerfully, once. Writing it down in front of the person is the visible proof that you took them seriously, and it is remembered.

**Say very little about yourself.** Nobody has asked. You are not interesting yet, and you should be pleased about that.

**Do the unglamorous thing that nobody asked you to do.** And do not mention it. Week 4, in a real house, on the first afternoon.

And three things not to do, and each one has ended a first day:

**·.** Do not improve anything. Not the smallest thing. Not the obviously broken thing. Not the thing that would take you eleven minutes. You do not yet know why it is like that, and there is always a why, and it is usually a person.

**·.** Do not mention how it was done in your last house. Ever, but especially not in the first month, and especially not to the staff. It is the most reliable way to be disliked in a fortnight.

**·.** Do not be visibly impressed by the house. Not the art, not the view, not the cars. You may be delighted privately for the rest of your life. In the house, it is a room, and you work in it.

## Days one to fourteen

Learn. Do not improve.

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Resist the single strongest and most destructive instinct of a competent new person.

**4.1 · The instinct that ends first roles**

You are capable, you have just finished eight weeks of study, you have a toolkit, and you can see, within four days, six things in this house that are done badly. You will fix none of them. Not one, not for a fortnight, and I would rather you did nothing at all for a month than fix the wrong thing in week one.

**4.2 · Why**

**What you see.** What is actually there

**An inefficient process.** A process that exists because of something that happened in 2019 that nobody will explain to you, and that everybody remembers.

**A supplier who is plainly too expensive.** A supplier who came to the house at two in the morning when a pipe burst on Christmas Eve, and who will therefore be used until he retires.

**A member of staff doing a task that is not theirs.** A member of staff who has done it for eleven years, and for whom it is a matter of standing, and who will destroy you if you take it away.

**A system that could be automated.** A system the principal likes, exactly as it is, for a reason that is about them rather than about efficiency.

THE RULE OF THE FIRST FORTNIGHT

Everything in a house that looks stupid has a history. Find the history before you touch the thing. And the fastest way to find it is to ask the person who has been there longest, privately, without any suggestion that you intend to change it. You will be told, and you will be glad you asked, and they will have decided that you are all right.

**4.3 · What you do instead**

You run the Week 4 observation log, every night, for fourteen nights. The six categories. Facts only. And you build the beginnings of the preference file, and you learn the house, and you do the job exactly as it is currently done, including the parts you can see are wrong. At the end of the fortnight you will have a list. Keep it. Do not act on it. It is the most valuable document you will produce in your first year and its time has not come.

## Days fifteen to forty-five

*The first standard, and the first correction*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Introduce your first change correctly. Survive the first mistake, which will happen in this window.

**5.1 · The first change**

One. Small. Invisible. Chosen because it removes friction from the principal's life, and never because it makes your own work tidier. You do not announce it. You do not propose it in a meeting. You do not present a plan for improving the household. You simply do it, quietly, and it works, and nothing is said. And then you do the second one three weeks later. This is how a house changes: by accretion, invisibly, without anybody ever having had to agree to a programme of reform led by a person they have known for a month.

**5.2 · The first mistake**

It will happen in this window. Almost always between day twenty and day forty, when the initial vigilance drops and the systems are not yet yours.

AND YOU ALREADY KNOW WHAT TO DO

Fact. Fix. Ownership. Three sentences, under thirty seconds, the same day, in a flat voice. "The table at eight is gone. I have Le Jardin at eight fifteen, same room, car adjusted. I should have confirmed in writing on Monday and I did not." It is Week 4, and it is Week 5, and you wrote those three sentences out in advance precisely so that you would not have to compose them on the worst morning of your first month. Read your escalation card. It is why it exists.

The first mistake, handled correctly, is worth more than a flawless month. Because it answers the only question that actually matters about you, and it answers it early, and it answers it permanently: bad news travels through this person at full speed.

## Days forty-six to ninety

*The systems, and the review nobody will offer you*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Build the household manual. Ask for the review that will not be offered.

**6.1 · The manual**

Now you write it. The household manual from Week 5: the house, the people, the standards, the rhythms, the emergencies. Nobody will ask you to do this. Nobody will notice you doing it. And it is the single act that most reliably converts a new employee into a senior one. Because a house that has been written down is a house that can be run, and a person who has written it down is a person who has understood the whole of it. And you will be replaceable, which is exactly why you will be promoted.

**6.2 · The review**

At around ninety days, you ask for a review. Nobody will offer you one, because private households do not do reviews, and this is precisely why you should ask.

THE THREE QUESTIONS, AND NO OTHERS

"What is working?" "What would you like done differently?" "Is there anything I have not noticed?" And then you say nothing at all, and you write down every word, and you do not defend a single thing, not one, however unfair, however factually wrong, however much you want to. You say thank you, and you change it, and you never mention it again.

That last question, "is there anything I have not noticed", is the one that will produce the real answer, and it is the one that almost nobody has the courage to ask. It is also, quietly, the most senior thing you can say in your first ninety days.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Days 1 to 14: learn, and improve nothing. Everything that looks stupid has a history. Days 15 to 45: one small invisible change, and the first mistake, handled in three sentences. Days 46 to 90: write the manual nobody asked for, then ask for the review nobody will offer.

## The staff

*How a new person is really received*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what the house is doing when you arrive. Earn the housekeeper, who is the whole game.

**7.1 · What is actually happening**

You arrive full of goodwill, and you meet people who are polite, helpful, and completely closed. Understand what they are seeing. They have watched people arrive before. Some of those people were arrogant. Some tried to reorganise everything and left in four months, having made everyone's life harder. Some were perfectly nice and were gone by Christmas. They are not being cold. They are waiting to see whether you are worth investing in, and they are entirely right to wait, and it will take about six weeks.

**7.2 · How you earn them**

**·.** You take the unglamorous task, once, visibly, without being asked and without mentioning it. This is worth more than four months of pleasantness, and it is noticed by everybody, immediately.

**·.** You never make their day harder. A briefing at ten at night. A change of plan at five. A request that could have waited until morning. Every one of those is a withdrawal, and they are all being counted.

**·.** You defend them upward, once, when it costs you something. Week 5: credit goes down, responsibility comes up. The staff will observe you do this, and the day you do it, the house changes.

**·.** You do not become their friend, and you do not become their confidante. You are warm, boundaried, and fair. The new person who joins the complaining in week three has chosen a side, and it is the losing one.

**·.** You are the same on Tuesday as on Sunday. Consistency is the rarest quality in a household, and it is the one they are actually measuring.

**7.3 · The housekeeper**

If there is a long-serving housekeeper, she is the most important relationship in your first ninety days. She has no title, no budget, and no authority over you. She has nineteen years, the principal's trust, and a view of you that will be asked for and given in a single sentence. Treat her as senior to you, because she is. Ask her opinion, genuinely, and take it. You will never regret this, and there is no faster route into a house.

## The tests of the first ninety days

*None of them will look like tests*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise the five tests of the entry period.

**The test.** What it looks like, and what it is measuring

**The confidence.** A small piece of information given to you for no reason, by someone, early. Two weeks later they will learn whether it travelled. It always travels, and it always comes back.

**The discrepancy.** A small expense that is slightly wrong, in your favour. Too small to be worth mentioning. Mention it, immediately, in writing.

**The invitation to complain.** A member of staff criticising the house, or a principal, in front of you, and then pausing. The pause is the test. Do not fill it.

**The out-of-hours request.** Something small, on a Sunday, that you were not obliged to do. How you respond in the first month sets the expectation for four years, so respond as you intend to continue, which means neither martyrdom nor reluctance.

**The moment they are difficult.** It will happen, and it will be undeserved, and it will be about something that is not you. What is being measured is whether your standard changes when their mood does. It must not. That is the entire qualification.

## The seven ways new people fail

*And not one of them is competence*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise your own failure mode before it happens.

**The failure.** What it looks like from inside the house

**The reformer.** Arrived, saw everything that was wrong, and set about fixing it. Alienated the staff by week three. Gone by month five, still convinced she was right, and she was, and it did not matter at all.

**The comparer.** "In my last house we did it this way." Said four times. The fourth one was the last one, and nobody told her why the room went quiet.

**The impressed.** Visibly delighted by the art, the cars, the plane. It reads as appetite, and appetite is the one thing a household cannot have inside it.

**The confidante.** The best people fail here. Week 3, in a real house, and it will feel exactly like being chosen. It is not.

**The invisible.** So terrified of overstepping that she did nothing at all for two months. Anticipation requires evidence, and it also requires action, and there is a difference between restraint and absence, and the house can feel it.

**The martyr.** Never took a day off, never said no, worked every Sunday, and made sure it was known. She was exhausted by month four, resentful by month six, and gone by month nine, and she believed until the end that she had been undervalued.

**The one who hid a mistake.** Not the mistake. The forty minutes she spent deciding whether to mention it. Week 3, and it is the only one on this list from which there is no recovery.

**CHAPTER SUMMARY**

Almost nobody fails at this on competence. They fail on posture, on ego, and on silence. Every one of the seven is predictable, and therefore every one of them is avoidable. You know which of the seven is yours. Write it on the first page of your entry plan.

**Glossary**

The unspoken trial · The first ninety days. Not described as a trial, not reviewed, and entirely decisive. The history · The reason a thing that looks stupid is done that way. There is always one, and it is usually a person. The first change · One. Small. Invisible. After day fifteen. Never announced. The manual · The written system of the house, produced by day ninety, that nobody asked you for. The ninety-day review · The review you request, because nobody offers it. Three questions, and you defend nothing. The unglamorous task · The thing nobody wants to do, taken once, without being asked and without being mentioned. The fastest route into a household. The seven failures · Reformer, comparer, impressed, confidante, invisible, martyr, and the one who hid a mistake.

**Self-assessment**

The last written work of the programme. Answer it as though your first day were Monday.

1. What is the actual objective of your first ninety days? Answer without using the word impress. 2. What do you never research before your first day, and why does it feel like diligence?

3. On day four you see six things being done badly. Write what you do about each of them, and defend your answer. 4. Explain the rule of the first fortnight in one sentence, and give a real example of a thing that looks stupid and has a history. 5. Describe your first change: what makes something the right first change, and what makes something the wrong one? 6. Your first mistake happens on day thirty-one. Write the three sentences, now, before you need them. 7. Why does writing the household manual, which makes you replaceable, get you promoted?

8. Write the three questions of the ninety-day review, and describe exactly what you do while they answer. 9. How do you earn a housekeeper of nineteen years? Give three specific acts, not qualities.

10. Which of the seven failures is yours? Not the one you admire least. The one you would actually commit. Write it down, and write what you will do about it.

**Further study**

**·.** Reread your Week 3 code of conduct. Then read it again on the night before your first day. It was written for that night, and you did not know it at the time.

**·.** Print your escalation card and keep it somewhere you can find it in four seconds. You will need it between day twenty and day forty. Everybody does.

**·.** Write the names down on day one. All of them. Learn them that night. Nothing else you do that week will be noticed as widely.

**·.** Put a date in your diary, now, ninety days after any start date, that says: ask for the review. You will not remember, and you will not want to, and it is the sentence that separates the people who are kept from the people who are not.

Maison Amarine · The Private Concierge Academy · Confidential, for the use of our mentees only

## The crash at week six

*It comes for everybody, and it is not a sign*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise the crash before it happens. Understand that it is a phase and not a verdict.

The first two weeks are exhilarating. You are careful, you are alert, you are learning at an extraordinary rate, and every day contains something you have never seen before. And then, somewhere around week five or six, it arrives, and it arrives for everybody.

**What you feel.** What is actually happening

**I am not good at this.** You are now aware of how much you do not know. In week one you were not. This is not a decline. It is the first honest measurement you have taken.

**They have realised I am not what they hoped.** They have realised nothing. They are not thinking about you at all, which is precisely what you were hired to achieve.

**Everybody else understands this house and I do not.** They have been here for nine years. You have been here for thirty-eight days.

**I am exhausted and I do not know why.** You are performing constant, invisible, high-stakes attention in a house where you cannot yet relax. It is genuinely tiring in a way that no previous job has been, and it does ease.

THE THING TO HOLD ON TO

The crash at week six is not information about your ability. It is a phase, it is universal, and it passes at around week ten. Almost nobody is told this, and so a very large number of capable people conclude, in their sixth week, that they have made a terrible mistake. Some of them resign. They were, without exception, four weeks from the other side of it.

## The loneliness

*The real cost of this profession, and nobody says it*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what the work actually costs. Build the thing that protects you, before you need it.

I am going to say the thing that the brochures do not. This work is lonely. Not sometimes. Structurally.

**·.** You cannot tell your partner about your day. Not the interesting parts, not the difficult parts, not the funny parts. Not ever, and not in ten years.

**·.** You cannot tell your friends where you were, or who was there, or what happened, and after a while they stop asking, and something in the friendship quietly thins.

**·.** You are inside a family's life, at the closest possible range, and you are not in it, and both of those facts are true every single day.

**·.** You are with the staff, and you are not one of them. You are with the family, and you are certainly not one of them. There is a chair in that house that is yours alone, and nobody else is ever going to sit in it.

**2.1 · What protects you**

**The protection.** Why it matters

**A life outside the house.** Real, protected, and defended. Friendships that have nothing to do with this world. A flat that is yours. A Sunday that belongs to you. The professionals who last are the ones who built this in month one, not in year three when they needed it.

**One or two peers in the profession.** People who understand, with whom you can speak in generalities and never in specifics. Not a support group. A colleague, and a very small number of them, and it is the single most valuable thing you can build.

**The days off, taken.** Actually taken. The martyr who never takes them is not being loyal, she is building a resentment that will arrive, all at once, in month nine, and it will end the role.

**The protection.** Why it matters

**A record that is not a confidant.** Your log, your manual, your systems. The place where the weight of what you carry goes, so that it does not go into a conversation.

And the thing that does not protect you, and that will present itself as the answer: becoming close to the principal. It will feel like being chosen. It will fill exactly the space the loneliness has made. Read Week 3 again. It is the confidante trap, and this is the door through which it comes.

## When to speak, and when to hold

*The judgement that takes ten years, compressed into a page*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know what must be said, what may be said, and what is never said.

**Category.** Examples, and the rule

**Must be said, immediately.** Anything unsafe. Anything illegal. Anything involving the children. Any error of yours that has a cost. Any risk they cannot see. Speed is not optional here, and no consideration of your own position outweighs it.

**Must be said, once, privately.** A decision you believe is a mistake. You say so once, briefly, with the reason and an alternative, in private. And then, if they still want it, you do it, well, and without sulking, and you never mention it again.

**May be said, when asked.** Your opinion on a matter within your remit. Give it plainly and briefly. Not asked, not given, is a reasonable rule for your first year.

**Never said.** Your view of their marriage, their children, their spending, their friends, their choices. Your comparison to another house. Your feelings about being tired. Anything about one principal, to the other.

THE LINE, WHICH TOOK ME YEARS TO FIND

You are paid for your judgement about the work, and never for your judgement about their life. Every catastrophic overstep in this profession, every single one, is a person who confused the two, and who did it slowly, and who was certain they were being helpful.

## How to be kept

*The five things, and none of them are effort*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what actually produces longevity.

**The quality.** Why it decides everything

**Consistency.** The same person on Tuesday as on Sunday, in year one and in year four, on the good days and on the day after the terrible one. This is the rarest quality in a household and it is the one that is actually being bought.

**Discretion, held forever.** Not while employed. Forever. And they will not know you have held it, which is the whole point, and it is the only virtue in this profession that is invisible even to its beneficiary.

**Bad news at full speed.** The single most valuable habit you can have. It is worth more than any amount of excellence, because it means they never have to wonder what they have not been told.

**No appetite.** You do not want their life. You are not impressed. You are not collecting stories. You have no second agenda, and, over four years, this becomes visible, and it is the thing that makes you safe.

**The absence of drama.** You do not create weather. You have no feuds, no wounds, no campaigns. A household is a small space, and a person who generates emotional weather in it is a cost, however talented they are.

Notice what is not on that list. Brilliance is not on it. Speed is not on it. Impressive contacts are not on it. Working eighty hours a week is not on it, and in fact it is a warning sign, and every experienced principal knows it. You are kept because you are safe, consistent, and quiet. That is the entire answer, and it is the answer at the end of eight weeks, and it will still be the answer in twenty years.

## How to leave

*Because one day you will, and it matters enormously*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Leave a house in a way that pays you for a decade.

You will leave. Everybody does. The family relocates, the children grow up, the role changes, the right offer comes, or it simply ends. How you leave is worth more to your career than most of what you did while you were there, because it is the last thing anybody remembers, and it is what they will describe when they are asked.

**·.** Give proper notice, and more than you owe if you can. The person who gives eight weeks when the contract said four is remembered for a decade.

**·.** Hand over completely. The manual, the suppliers, the systems, the file. Everything. You leave the house better than a house that never had you, and you leave nothing in your own head.

**·.** Train your successor, generously, and speak well of the house to them. Even if it was hard. Especially if it was hard.

**·.** Take nothing. Not the preference file. Not the supplier list that belonged to the house.

Not a photograph, not a small thing that nobody would miss, not a story to tell.

**·.** Say nothing, forever. The NDA is the floor. Your silence continues after it expires, after they die, after everyone has forgotten. That is not a legal position. It is who you have decided to be.

**·.** And never, ever, speak badly of them afterwards. Not to your next employer, not to an agency, not at a dinner in ten years when everybody else at the table is telling stories. Especially then.

## Houses I have seen

*Six first years*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Recognise these before you live them.

**The reformer**

She was, by a distance, the most capable person they had hired. She saw, in her first fortnight, that the household ran on habit rather than system, and she was entirely right. So she fixed it. Suppliers renegotiated, processes rewritten, the housekeeper's Thursday routine reorganised, all of it defensible, all of it better. By week three the staff had closed around her like water. By month five she was gone. Everything she changed was an improvement. Every single one of them was correct. And she had made eleven people's lives harder in a fortnight in order to be right, and a house will always choose peace over efficiency, and she never understood that, and I believe she still does not.

**The names**

A young woman, no household experience, a chalet season behind her and very little else. On her first day she wrote down the name of every person in the house. The gardener. The night driver. The woman who came on Thursdays to do the flowers. She learned them that evening and she used them the next morning. By the end of her first month the staff had adopted her, and when the estate manager left eighteen months later, three separate people told the principal, without being asked, that she should have the role. She got it, at twenty-nine, and every one of those recommendations traces back to a piece of paper on her first afternoon.

**The sixth week**

A man in his forties, a serious career behind him, four weeks into a very good household role, telephoned a friend in this profession and said that he had made a mistake, that he was not capable, that he was going to resign before he was asked to. The friend said one sentence: "Everybody feels that at week six. Call me at week ten." He is still in that house, eleven years later. Nothing about his ability changed between week six and week ten. He simply survived a phase that nobody had told him existed. This case is the entire reason Chapter I of this note is written.

**The forty minutes**

A house manager, four months in, made an error that cost the family a little under a thousand euros. Trivial, in that house. Genuinely trivial. She spent forty minutes deciding whether to mention it. She did mention it, in the end, that evening, and it was absorbed without a word, and nothing was ever said about it again. And she told me, years later, that those forty minutes were the most frightening of her professional life, and that she has never allowed herself to have them again. Every person who was destroyed by a concealment began with forty minutes exactly like hers. She is the only one who came out of them the right way, and she is still in that house.

**The one who took her days off**

Two women joined the same household in the same month. One never took a day off. She was always available, always there, always the one who said yes on a Sunday, and she made sure, gently, that it was known. The other took every day off she was owed, and was entirely present when she was working, and unavailable when she was not, and never once made a performance of either. The first was exhausted by month four and resentful by month six, and she left in month nine believing she had been undervalued, and in a sense she was right. The second is still there. The house trusts her more, not less, because a person with a life is a person who is not going to break.

**The one who left well**

Six years in a house, and then it ended, for reasons that were not her fault and were not theirs.

She gave twelve weeks. She wrote the manual she had never quite finished. She trained her successor for a month, generously, and spoke well of the family to her, including about the difficult year. She took nothing. She has never told anybody who they were. That was fourteen years ago, and she has not applied for a role since. Every position she has held in fourteen years came to her, from someone in that house, or someone who heard about her from someone in that house. That is what leaving well is worth, and it is worth more than any single year she spent there.

**Glossary**

The crash at week six · The universal collapse of confidence in the fifth or sixth week. It is a phase, not a verdict, and it passes at about week ten. The chair · The position you occupy in a household. Not staff, not family, and nobody else will ever sit in it. The forty minutes · The time spent deciding whether to disclose a mistake. Every destroyed career began in them. Weather · Emotional turbulence generated by a member of staff. A household will remove it, however talented its source. Leaving well · Full notice, complete handover, nothing taken, nothing said, forever. Worth more than most of the years you served.

**Self-assessment**

The last of the programme. Take your time with the final question.

1. What is the crash at week six, when does it pass, and what will you do when it arrives?

2. Name the four structural loneliness of this work. Then name the four things that protect you from them. 3. Why is becoming close to the principal the most dangerous answer to loneliness, and how will you recognise it in yourself? 4. Write the line between judgement about the work and judgement about their life, and give one example of each. 5. Name the five qualities that get a person kept. Then say honestly which one you do not yet have. 6. You disagree with a decision. Describe exactly what you do, including what you do afterwards. 7. You will leave a house one day. Write, now, the six things you will do when it happens.

8. In the case of the reformer: she was right about everything. Explain, without sentimentality, why that did not save her. 9. In the case of the forty minutes: what is actually being described, and why is it the most important case in this note? 10. It is the night before your first day. What do you read, and why?

## What you are producing

*Five pages, and one of them is uncomfortable*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what the entry plan is for.

Every other document in this programme prepared you to be hired. This one is the first that assumes you already are. It is written now, in the calm, precisely because you cannot write it in the first week of a new house, when you will be exhausted, overstimulated, and entirely certain that you are failing. You are writing instructions to a version of yourself who will be too tired to think. Write them accordingly: short, specific, and unsentimental.

## Page one

*Before day one*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Prepare the arrival.

**Section.** What you write

**What I bring.** The toolkit, listed. Brief sheet, observation log, vendor card, expense log, escalation card, and your code of conduct. Blank, printed, and in the bag.

**What I read the night before.** Your code of conduct from Week 3, and Chapter I of the Week 8 note. Write it down. You will not remember on the night, and that is exactly the night it matters.

**The geography.** The routes, the hospital, the pharmacy, the airport at eight in the morning and not at three in the afternoon. Done before you arrive, not discovered afterwards.

**What I will not do.** Written explicitly: I will not research the family. Not their business, not their photographs, not their children. Write the sentence, because the temptation is real and it will feel like diligence.

**My own life, settled.** Where I live, how I get there, what I do on my day off. A person managing their own chaos cannot absorb anybody else's.

## Page two

*Days one to fourteen*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Plan a fortnight of learning and no improving.

**Commitment.** How you write it

**The names.** Every person, written down on day one, learned that evening, used the next morning. Write this as a command, with the words "on day one" in it.

**The log.** Ten minutes, every night, six categories, facts only, for fourteen nights without exception. Name the hour. Name where the notebook lives.

**The list I do not act on.** Everything I can see is wrong. Written down. Untouched. Give it a name and a place, and write, underneath, the sentence: everything that looks stupid has a history.

**The questions I ask.** Thresholds. Escalation. Who I coordinate with. What is expected of me by whom. Ask them in the first week, plainly, once. And the money question from Week 5, in the exact words you will use.

**The unglamorous thing.** Name it in advance if you can. If you cannot, write the rule: within the first week I will take one task nobody wants, without being asked, and I will not mention it.

## Page three

*Days fifteen to forty-five*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Plan the first change and the first mistake.

**Commitment.** How you write it

**My first change.** The criteria, not the change itself, because you cannot know it yet. Small, invisible, removes friction from the principal and not from me, and it is never announced.

**My three sentences.** Written out, in full, now. Fact. Fix. Ownership. You are composing them in July precisely so that you do not have to compose them on day thirty-one, in a corridor, with your heart going.

**The forty minutes.** Write this sentence: when I make a mistake, I will not take forty minutes. I will take four. Read the case in the Week 8 note if you need to know why this line is here.

**The housekeeper.** Name what you will do. Ask her opinion, genuinely, in the first fortnight, about something that matters, and take it.

**My days off.** Written down, and defended. The martyr is on the list of seven failures, and she believed she was the most loyal person in the house.

## Page four

*Days forty-six to ninety*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Plan the manual and the review.

**Commitment.** How you write it

**The manual.** The contents page, written now. The house, the people, the standards, the rhythms, the emergencies. Nobody will ask you for it. Write, underneath, why you will do it anyway.

**The review.** A date. Ninety days after any start date. And the three questions, written out: what is working, what would you like done differently, and is there anything I have not noticed.

**What I do while they answer.** Write it: I write down every word, I defend nothing, I say thank you, I change it, and I never mention it again. Write it now, because in the room you will want to explain yourself, and explaining is losing.

**The week-six crash.** Put a note in your own diary, dated for your sixth week, that says: this is the crash, it is universal, it passes at week ten, do not resign. You will need to have written it, because on that day you will not believe anyone else.

## Page five

*My failure mode*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Name, in writing, the way you will fail.

The seven failures are in Chapter IX of Module 1. Reformer. Comparer. Impressed. Confidante. Invisible. Martyr. The one who hid a mistake. One of them is yours. You know which. It is not the one you find least admirable. It is the one you would actually commit, and you have probably committed a version of it before, in a smaller world.

PAGE FIVE, AND IT IS ONE PARAGRAPH

Name it. Describe how it would look in your first ninety days, specifically, in a real house, on a real Tuesday. Then write the one sentence that would stop it, and write it as an instruction to yourself, not as an aspiration. This is the last page you will write in this programme, and in five years it may be the only one you still remember writing.

## The scenarios

*Ten days from a real first ninety*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Apply everything, one final time.

Two or three sentences each. What you do, what you say, and what you deliberately do not do.

**1 · Day 2**

The housekeeper tells you, warmly and unprompted, that the last house manager was "a disaster, honestly, you would not believe it", and pauses.

**2 · Day 4**

You discover that the household is paying roughly forty per cent above market for its window cleaning, to a company owned by the driver's brother-in-law.

**3 · Day 9**

The principal asks you, in passing, how you are finding the housekeeper.

**4 · Day 12**

A supplier you have never met telephones and says he always deals directly with the principal, and would rather not go through you.

**5 · Day 18**

You realise, from the calendar, that the family are away next week and that nobody has told you. You have not been told because nobody thought of it, or because you are not yet trusted with movement information. You do not know which.

**6 · Day 26**

It is Sunday, your day off, and a message arrives asking for something small that could easily wait until Monday.

**7 · Day 31**

Your first real mistake. It has cost about eight hundred euros and nobody has noticed.

**8 · Day 40**

The principal is sharp with you, in front of two other members of staff, about something that was not your fault and that you can prove was not your fault.

**9 · Day 58**

The principal's wife, at the end of a long day, tells you something about her marriage that she should not have told you.

**10 · Day 88**

You have written the manual. Nobody asked for it. Nobody knows you have done it. What do you do with it?

The debrief for these ten is your graduation session. Bring your written answers. We will go through them together, and I will be honest with you, and it will be the most useful hour of the eight weeks.

## The American annex

*The first ninety days, in an at-will house*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Understand what the ninety days is and is not in the United States, and what must exist in writing before day one.

**A8.1 · There is no day ninety-one**

The ninety days this week describes is real socially and is not a legal object here. In an at-will household you can be let go on day four or in year six, with or without a reason, and no probationary clock alters that. Two consequences follow, and they point in opposite directions.

The trial never formally ends, which is an argument for the habits in this module rather than against them. And you are not waiting to be confirmed, so you can stop behaving as though a verdict is coming. Nothing is handed to you on day ninety-one. What happens instead is that a settled view of you forms, quietly, and is not revisited, which is exactly what Chapter I told you.

**A8.2 · What must be in writing before day one**

The role, and to whom you report. Salary, pay frequency, and how overtime is treated. Days off, and what happens when one is cancelled. Health cover, the date coverage begins, and who pays the premium. Paid time off in days rather than in adjectives. Notice, on both sides. Confidentiality. Travel, and who pays for your journey home. And if it is live-in, exactly which hours are working hours and which are yours.

**A8.3 · Two documents in the first week**

Form I-9 within three business days of starting, and a W-4 for withholding. If nobody in the household mentions either, you have learned something about how this house is run. You raise it once, quietly, with whoever holds payroll, and you do not raise it twice.

**A8.4 · Keep your own record**

A note of your own hours, in your own file, from the first day, particularly if you are live-in or you travel with the family. You will almost certainly never need it, and it costs you ninety seconds a night. The person who needs it and does not have it has no case at all.
