## Thirty minutes

*Short, sharp, and entirely about you*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Know the shape of the session.

Thirty minutes, and not one of them wasted. We move fast because you are ready to move fast.

**Time.** What we do

**Minutes 1 to 5.** Your rung, and your first move. Where you are, where you can be taken today, and the fastest honest route to a first role. You will leave this section knowing exactly what your next email is.

**Minutes 5 to 20.** The scenarios. This is where the session lives, and where it becomes genuinely fun. I will push you, you will push back, and by the end of it you will trust your own judgement in a way that no amount of reading can give you.

**Minutes 20 to 25.** Your documents. The two or three small things that make the difference between being read and being called back. Small things. Fixable in an evening.

**Minutes 25 to 30.** Your questions, and mine. And then the one thing I want you to do in the next seven days. One thing, specific, and I will ask you about it, because I am not disappearing when this call ends.

Come with your documents open and your scenarios written. Thirty minutes with a prepared mentee is worth more than two hours with an unprepared one, and you have never been more prepared than you are now.

## The last page

*Read this one slowly*

**LEARNING OBJECTIVES**

Take a breath. This one is for you.

Eight weeks ago you wanted to work for a family whose life is unlike any life you have known. What you have actually learned is something older, quieter and far more valuable: how to be trusted with the private life of another human being, how to carry it without ever setting it down, and how to disappear at exactly the right moment. It is a serious profession. It is well paid, it is rare, and it asks for something that not everyone is willing to give. You have done eight weeks of the work, which is the part you control. You will be tested in your first month, and you will pass. You will crash in your sixth week, and you will remember that everybody does, and you will keep going. Somebody will invite you, warmly, to say the one thing you should not say, and you will feel the pull of it. And you will not say it. And one day, in a room you are not in, somebody will say your name. Now go and take it.

**A LETTER FROM ÉMILIE**

**One last thing**

I want to write to you not as your teacher, but simply as myself.

When I began in this world, nobody explained any of it to me. I learned the way most people learn it: alone, by watching, by getting things wrong in rooms where getting things wrong is expensive. There were evenings when I had nobody to ask, and mornings when I was certain I did not belong. I found my way in the end, but I found it the hard way, and I have never forgotten how that felt.

That is why Maison Amarine exists. Not to sell you a dream, and not to hand you a certificate. It exists so that you would never have to stand alone outside a door I have already walked through.

These eight weeks have been, quite honestly, one of the great joys of my professional life. Passing on a craft you love to someone who is genuinely hungry for it is a rare privilege, and you have given me that. Every question you asked, every deliverable you sent me, every time you sat with something difficult instead of looking away, I saw it. And I am so proud of you.

I hope this has given you far more than you came looking for. I hope you close these documents with something you did not have eight weeks ago: not just knowledge, but a way of seeing, and a quiet confidence that stays with you long after the last video ends.

You now carry something real. A craft that cannot be taken from you, standards that will outlive any single role, and the truth about a world that very few people ever get to hear. Carry it well, and carry it kindly.

And when your name is said, one day, in a room you are not in, and the right thing is said about you, I hope you remember this page.

With all my heart, and with every confidence in you.

Émilie
